title: Welcome and Tutorial Survey (Due 9pm Tuesday)
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Welcome to CS234!</paragraph><paragraph>It was great to meet many of you today in class. </paragraph><paragraph>As you all know, large language models are now very good at solving standard homework problems.</paragraph><paragraph>But the goal of learning was never to simply get problems correct. The real goal is to build ways of thinking—frameworks you can use to tackle new problems, explain your ideas to others, and advance a field.</paragraph><paragraph>That’s why, alongside homework, we’re introducing weekly tutorials this term.</paragraph><paragraph>Tutorials are small, structured, discussion-based sessions inspired by the Oxford and Cambridge system. You’ll work in small groups with a staff facilitator to explain ideas, reason through problems, and wrestle with the material together.</paragraph><paragraph>One gap we’ve consistently seen is that students get very little practice articulating technical ideas—yet this is exactly what you’ll be expected to do in research, interviews, and industry. Tutorials are designed to directly address that gap.</paragraph><paragraph>These sessions will be mandatory for on-campus students. You’ll have a 30-minute slot that stays the same each week, and there will be 8 total tutorials. Content will be a mix of explaining (already submitted) homework problems or lecture material, and new problems. No laptops, phones or prior notes will be allowed, and problems will be worked out on a whiteboard. </paragraph><paragraph><bold>If you are an on-campus student, please fill this out by Tuesday at 9pm to share some of your background and provide your availability</bold>:</paragraph><paragraph><link href="https://forms.gle/h3hCMsAz1fNdstD86"><underline>https://forms.gle/h3hCMsAz1fNdstD86</underline></link></paragraph><paragraph>[You need to log in with your stanford sunid to access the form]</paragraph><paragraph>Tutorials will only be lightly assessed as inadequate, adequate and above. Adequate will yield full credit for the tutorial section of the course graded, and will be given to students that come prepared to answer questions about previously submitted homework and that attempt new problems. Above will be given to the top ~5% of students who give particularly clear answers or thoughtful approaches, and will contribute only to extra credit for the course. Inadequate will be given for students who do not attend, who are unprepared to answer questions about previously due homework, and/or do not engage in the tutorial. The bottom two tutorial scores will be dropped. </paragraph><paragraph>Our hope is that tutorials will be a supportive, low-pressure environment where you can deepen your understanding, test your reasoning, and practice explaining complex ideas clearly. We’re excited to try this with you, and we think it will make a real difference in how you learn this material!</paragraph></document>
document:
Welcome to CS234!

It was great to meet many of you today in class. 

As you all know, large language models are now very good at solving standard homework problems.

But the goal of learning was never to simply get problems correct. The real goal is to build ways of thinking—frameworks you can use to tackle new problems, explain your ideas to others, and advance a field.

That’s why, alongside homework, we’re introducing weekly tutorials this term.

Tutorials are small, structured, discussion-based sessions inspired by the Oxford and Cambridge system. You’ll work in small groups with a staff facilitator to explain ideas, reason through problems, and wrestle with the material together.

One gap we’ve consistently seen is that students get very little practice articulating technical ideas—yet this is exactly what you’ll be expected to do in research, interviews, and industry. Tutorials are designed to directly address that gap.

These sessions will be mandatory for on-campus students. You’ll have a 30-minute slot that stays the same each week, and there will be 8 total tutorials. Content will be a mix of explaining (already submitted) homework problems or lecture material, and new problems. No laptops, phones or prior notes will be allowed, and problems will be worked out on a whiteboard. 

If you are an on-campus student, please fill this out by Tuesday at 9pm to share some of your background and provide your availability:

https://forms.gle/h3hCMsAz1fNdstD86

[You need to log in with your stanford sunid to access the form]

Tutorials will only be lightly assessed as inadequate, adequate and above. Adequate will yield full credit for the tutorial section of the course graded, and will be given to students that come prepared to answer questions about previously submitted homework and that attempt new problems. Above will be given to the top ~5% of students who give particularly clear answers or thoughtful approaches, and will contribute only to extra credit for the course. Inadequate will be given for students who do not attend, who are unprepared to answer questions about previously due homework, and/or do not engage in the tutorial. The bottom two tutorial scores will be dropped. 

Our hope is that tutorials will be a supportive, low-pressure environment where you can deepen your understanding, test your reasoning, and practice explaining complex ideas clearly. We’re excited to try this with you, and we think it will make a real difference in how you learn this material!

title: Difference from CS 224R?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>How different is this course compared to CS 224R in terms of the things that are covered? I have taken CS 224R so I'm wondering if there are additional benefits for me to also take this class. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: How different is this course compared to CS 224R in terms of the things that are covered? I have taken CS 224R so I'm wondering if there are additional benefits for me to also take this class.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Chelsea Finn (instructor of 224R) and I have discussed this, and we have heard from multiple students who took both courses and found both valuable. 234 covers more theory and more on exploration, and emphasizes different application areas. However, if you were to take both, I'd generally recommend taking CS234 and then CS224R. If you've already taken CS224R, then I'm not sure I'd take this now. If you have further questions, don't hesitate to ask. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: Chelsea Finn (instructor of 224R) and I have discussed this, and we have heard from multiple students who took both courses and found both valuable. 234 covers more theory and more on exploration, and emphasizes different application areas. However, if you were to take both, I'd generally recommend taking CS234 and then CS224R. If you've already taken CS224R, then I'm not sure I'd take this now. If you have further questions, don't hesitate to ask.

title: Why were we able to factor the Value matrix out?
content: To clarify further, why can we assume V(s) = V(s') when we write them as matrices?
document: To clarify further, why can we assume V(s) = V(s') when we write them as matrices?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Good question, they are not in general the same actually. We don't need to make that assumption, and the confusion might be resolved from carefully recalling the details of matrix-vector multiplication.</paragraph><paragraph>In the setting introduced in class where we have finite states, (say, $n$ states), we can consider enumerating the states from $1$ to $n$ as $(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n)$.</paragraph><paragraph>Then, we can see that the state-value function can be written as a vector: $$V = \begin{bmatrix}V\left(s_1\right) \\V\left(s_2\right)\\ \vdots \\V\left(s_n\right)\end{bmatrix},$$</paragraph><paragraph>Similarly the immediate rewards can also be written as a vector, and $P$ can be written as a $n \times n$ matrix. See lec 1 slide 48.</paragraph><paragraph>Finally, you should be able to see how for any given state $s_i$, how $V(s_i)$ can be written as the $i$'th element of the reward vector plus $\gamma$ times a matrix vector product. So it's not that $V(s) = V(s')$ in particular, but $s'$ varies over all the other states for the matrix vector product. See slide 57, and try to see how for example, $V(s_1) = R(s_1) + \gamma \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(s_i | s_1) V(s_i)$. </paragraph><paragraph>Let me know if that makes sense. It's a little tricky to convert from element-wise equations to matrix-vector equations, but thinking deeper about the exact mechanics of matrix-vector multiplication and reasoning about particular indices helps.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Good question, they are not in general the same actually. We don't need to make that assumption, and the confusion might be resolved from carefully recalling the details of matrix-vector multiplication.

In the setting introduced in class where we have finite states, (say, $n$ states), we can consider enumerating the states from $1$ to $n$ as $(s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n)$.

Then, we can see that the state-value function can be written as a vector: $$V = \begin{bmatrix}V\left(s_1\right) \\V\left(s_2\right)\\ \vdots \\V\left(s_n\right)\end{bmatrix},$$

Similarly the immediate rewards can also be written as a vector, and $P$ can be written as a $n \times n$ matrix. See lec 1 slide 48.

Finally, you should be able to see how for any given state $s_i$, how $V(s_i)$ can be written as the $i$'th element of the reward vector plus $\gamma$ times a matrix vector product. So it's not that $V(s) = V(s')$ in particular, but $s'$ varies over all the other states for the matrix vector product. See slide 57, and try to see how for example, $V(s_1) = R(s_1) + \gamma \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(s_i | s_1) V(s_i)$. 

Let me know if that makes sense. It's a little tricky to convert from element-wise equations to matrix-vector equations, but thinking deeper about the exact mechanics of matrix-vector multiplication and reasoning about particular indices helps.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I got it but then the equation in the slide can be written as :</paragraph><paragraph>$$V(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum P(s'|s)V(s)$$</paragraph><paragraph>notationally.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
I got it but then the equation in the slide can be written as :

$$V(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum P(s'|s)V(s)$$

notationally.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Sorry, I'm not completely following, could you explain a little bit more about why you think that's the case? If we went with the equation you wrote down, we would get that:</paragraph><paragraph>$$V(s) = R(s) + \gamma V(s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' | s),$$</paragraph><paragraph>which, since $\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' | s) = 1$, would result in</paragraph><paragraph>$$V(s)  = (1 - \gamma)^{-1} R(s).$$</paragraph><paragraph>This is not true. Intuitively, the equation in the slide is saying that the value of a state is the expected immediate reward at that state, followed by the discounted expected value of the <bold>next</bold> state. So $s'$ represents that "next state".</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Sorry, I'm not completely following, could you explain a little bit more about why you think that's the case? If we went with the equation you wrote down, we would get that:

$$V(s) = R(s) + \gamma V(s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' | s),$$

which, since $\sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' | s) = 1$, would result in

$$V(s)  = (1 - \gamma)^{-1} R(s).$$

This is not true. Intuitively, the equation in the slide is saying that the value of a state is the expected immediate reward at that state, followed by the discounted expected value of the next state. So $s'$ represents that "next state".

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Not quite, but thanks for asking. It is actually correct to have $V(s')$ inside of the summation. </paragraph><paragraph>It may be helpful to think of it this way. $V(s)$ is the expected infinite horizon reward you get if you are in state $s$. By the law of total expectation, we can decompose this into two parts:</paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>the <italic>instantaneous reward</italic> you get at time steps: $R(s)$ </paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>the <italic>expected future value you get</italic> in future time steps: This, of course, depends on where you transition to. If you transition to $s'$ you would get $\gamma \cdot V(s')$ in the future, and the probability of transitioning to $s'$ is $P(s' | s)$. So, we sum over $s'$, weight by $P(s'|s)$, and discount by $\gamma$. </paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>If you go through the matrix arithmetic this is exactly what the equation $V = R + \gamma P V$ represents. Indeed, if you look at the $s$-th row of this matrix equation, then note that the $s$-th row of $P$ is a vector whose $s'$-th entry is $P(s' | s)$. Thus, we get $V(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' | s) V(s')$. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Not quite, but thanks for asking. It is actually correct to have $V(s')$ inside of the summation. 

It may be helpful to think of it this way. $V(s)$ is the expected infinite horizon reward you get if you are in state $s$. By the law of total expectation, we can decompose this into two parts:

the instantaneous reward you get at time steps: $R(s)$ 

the expected future value you get in future time steps: This, of course, depends on where you transition to. If you transition to $s'$ you would get $\gamma \cdot V(s')$ in the future, and the probability of transitioning to $s'$ is $P(s' | s)$. So, we sum over $s'$, weight by $P(s'|s)$, and discount by $\gamma$. 

If you go through the matrix arithmetic this is exactly what the equation $V = R + \gamma P V$ represents. Indeed, if you look at the $s$-th row of this matrix equation, then note that the $s$-th row of $P$ is a vector whose $s'$-th entry is $P(s' | s)$. Thus, we get $V(s) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' | s) V(s')$.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Note quite. Note that $V$ is a vector or function. You can think of it as a vector of dimension $|S|$ where $S$ is the state space (and $V(s)$ is the $s$-th entry) or you can think of it as a function over $S$ (in which case $V(s)$ is $V$ evaluated at $s$). <break/><break/>In either case, $V(s)$ and $V(s')$ are <italic>scalars</italic> (not vectors.) In general, $V(s) \neq V(s')$. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Note quite. Note that $V$ is a vector or function. You can think of it as a vector of dimension $|S|$ where $S$ is the state space (and $V(s)$ is the $s$-th entry) or you can think of it as a function over $S$ (in which case $V(s)$ is $V$ evaluated at $s$). 

In either case, $V(s)$ and $V(s')$ are scalars (not vectors.) In general, $V(s) \neq V(s')$.

I am guessing we will see a proof of convergence in the coming weeks in the class.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: I think you are able to factor out the vectors V assuming you have reached steady state (in which the V on the LHS is assumed to be equal to the V on the RHS). I am guessing we will see a proof of convergence in the coming weeks in the class.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This is almost right! However, one important clarification is the <italic>value function</italic> $V$ is <italic>by definition</italic> <break/><break/>$$V(s) = \mathbb{E} \left[\sum_{t \geq 0} \gamma^t R(s_t) | s_0 = s\right], \text{ for all } s$$<break/><break/>where the expectation is taken with respect to the dynamics induced by $P$. This <italic>alone</italic> is actually enough to conclude that <break/><break/>$$V(s) = r(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' | s) V(s'), \text{ for all } s$$ <break/><break/>using the law of total expectation argument I described above. This second equation is what we call the "Bellman equation". There's no steady state assumption used here-- rather, the Bellman equation describes the steady state which the true value function must satisfy. <break/><break/>Regarding the proof you mention, I think you might be thinking about the approximate values $V_1, ..., V_T$ which arise in <italic>value iteration</italic>. You are right that $V_T$ <italic>converges</italic> to satisfying the Bellman equation. However, that is because the value iteration iterates are only <italic>approximations</italic> to the value function $V$. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
This is almost right! However, one important clarification is the value function $V$ is by definition 

$$V(s) = \mathbb{E} \left[\sum_{t \geq 0} \gamma^t R(s_t) | s_0 = s\right], \text{ for all } s$$

where the expectation is taken with respect to the dynamics induced by $P$. This alone is actually enough to conclude that 

$$V(s) = r(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' | s) V(s'), \text{ for all } s$$ 

using the law of total expectation argument I described above. This second equation is what we call the "Bellman equation". There's no steady state assumption used here-- rather, the Bellman equation describes the steady state which the true value function must satisfy. 

Regarding the proof you mention, I think you might be thinking about the approximate values $V_1, ..., V_T$ which arise in value iteration. You are right that $V_T$ converges to satisfying the Bellman equation. However, that is because the value iteration iterates are only approximations to the value function $V$.

title: L1 norm for Policy optimization
content: On Slide 22, I see that the policy iteration continues till the L1-norm of the step-difference between the policies goes to zero. But since the optimal policy might not be unique, the iteration could keep going forever right?
document: On Slide 22, I see that the policy iteration continues till the L1-norm of the step-difference between the policies goes to zero. But since the optimal policy might not be unique, the iteration could keep going forever right?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>That is correct in the case if there are multiple optimal policies and in that case you would also add another check that $|V_{\pi_i} - V_{\pi_{i+1}}|>0$ to make sure that you have reached the optimal value function for each state because we know that if there are multiple optimal policies, then their Value functions must be equal. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: That is correct in the case if there are multiple optimal policies and in that case you would also add another check that $|V_{\pi_i} - V_{\pi_{i+1}}|>0$ to make sure that you have reached the optimal value function for each state because we know that if there are multiple optimal policies, then their Value functions must be equal.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question! If the tie-breaking procedure in the argmax of the policy improvement stage is inconsistent, then yes we could end up with an infinite loop. As Herman suggests, this can be easily remediated by adding a check that the value function has not converged.</paragraph><paragraph>However, I believe that as long as the tie-breaking procedure in the policy improvement sage is consistent, this should not be an issue. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Great question! If the tie-breaking procedure in the argmax of the policy improvement stage is inconsistent, then yes we could end up with an infinite loop. As Herman suggests, this can be easily remediated by adding a check that the value function has not converged.

However, I believe that as long as the tie-breaking procedure in the policy improvement sage is consistent, this should not be an issue.

title: Final Project for Top-tier Papers
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi CS234 classmates,</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>I'm Zhenyu, a CGOE student. The other side is that we may be able to formulate a 24hr team!</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>Let me know if do have the same dream, or even further, you already have an (on-going/planned) idea. Hi CS234 classmates,



I'm Zhenyu, a CGOE student. To introduce myself further, I am proficient in C++, Python and GPU Programming (including Cuda, Triton and Tilelang, or even Trainium). I received an A in CS229/CS149 last semester and am currently self-studying deep learning via Coursera.



I'd like to check if some of you may dream the same dream of mine -- to publish a paper to a top-tier AI conference. I feel with all the dedication, mindset and resources we have, perhaps we can approach some ideas or project that are viable for a paper to a top-tier AI conference (e.g., ICML). The idea could possibly come from a professor or someone close to this field. And we try to build a SOTA in that subdomain (Although LLM is the most likely one, but I'm open to all viable options!)



One caveat is that I live in Singapore (UTC+8), we need to accommodate to find slot for sync-ups and meetings. The other side is that we may be able to formulate a 24hr team!



Let me know if do have the same dream, or even further, you already have an (on-going/planned) idea. Feel free to drop me an email: zhenyuc@stanford.edu



Warm regards,

Zhenyu

title: How much overlap with other RL classes?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi there! I was wondering how much overlap in content there is between CS 234 and CS 224R (Deep Reinforcement Learning) and CS 238 (Decision Making Under Uncertainty)? Thanks so much!</paragraph></document>
document: Hi there! I was wondering how much overlap in content there is between CS 234 and CS 224R (Deep Reinforcement Learning) and CS 238 (Decision Making Under Uncertainty)? Thanks so much!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>#18</paragraph><paragraph>i also think the main difference is 224r is more coding/application based whereas cs234 is more related to the theory and proofs of rl fundamentals from first principles (i think prof brunskill also mentioned some of the material diff in the beginning of lect 2 yesterday)</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
#18

i also think the main difference is 224r is more coding/application based whereas cs234 is more related to the theory and proofs of rl fundamentals from first principles (i think prof brunskill also mentioned some of the material diff in the beginning of lect 2 yesterday)

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Gabriel's answer regarding CS 224R vs CS 234 is accurate from my experiences, too! </paragraph><paragraph>Regarding CS 234 vs CS 238, I would say CS 238 is more about generally using probability to make smart moves when you don't have all the info, so it is broader than RL; think topics such as Bayesian networks, POMDPs, structure learning, and exact solutions. CS 234 is focused on RL, has a bit more of a focus on deep learning, and touches topics such as RLHF/DPO, exploration, imitation learning, etc. </paragraph><paragraph>There is definitely a fundamental overlap between these classes (especially CS 234 and CS 224R), but the class structure/approach and topics differ.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Gabriel's answer regarding CS 224R vs CS 234 is accurate from my experiences, too! 

Regarding CS 234 vs CS 238, I would say CS 238 is more about generally using probability to make smart moves when you don't have all the info, so it is broader than RL; think topics such as Bayesian networks, POMDPs, structure learning, and exact solutions. CS 234 is focused on RL, has a bit more of a focus on deep learning, and touches topics such as RLHF/DPO, exploration, imitation learning, etc. 

There is definitely a fundamental overlap between these classes (especially CS 234 and CS 224R), but the class structure/approach and topics differ.

title: Seeking Teammates - Algorithmic Trading Project
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi everyone! <break/>I’m Siyuan Wu, a second-year MSCS student on the AI track. I’m looking to collaborate on an algorithmic trading project and would love to team up with anyone interested!</paragraph><paragraph>Some relevant resources I’ve been exploring:<break/> 🔗 FinRL: <link href="https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL">https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL</link><break/> 🔗 FinRL-Meta: <link href="https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL-Meta">https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL-Meta</link><break/> 📄 Paper: <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.06753">https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.06753</link></paragraph><paragraph>I’ve already built a codebase that can serve as a trading environment, so we can mainly focus on the research side of things.</paragraph><paragraph>Feel free to reach out at <bold><link href="null">wusiyuan@stanford.edu</link></bold> if you’re interested!</paragraph><paragraph>Looking forward to working together!</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi everyone! 
I’m Siyuan Wu, a second-year MSCS student on the AI track. I’m looking to collaborate on an algorithmic trading project and would love to team up with anyone interested!

Some relevant resources I’ve been exploring:
 🔗 FinRL: https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL
 🔗 FinRL-Meta: https://github.com/AI4Finance-Foundation/FinRL-Meta
 📄 Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.06753

I’ve already built a codebase that can serve as a trading environment, so we can mainly focus on the research side of things.

Feel free to reach out at wusiyuan@stanford.edu if you’re interested!

Looking forward to working together!

title: Optimal Policy for Mars Rover
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For the Mars Rover example, the reward is +10 at S10​ and +1 at S1​. I am a bit confused about the optimal policy in this setting.</paragraph><paragraph>If the agent receives a reward of +1 each time it reaches S1​, then for a sufficiently large horizon H or in the infinite-horizon case, wouldn’t the optimal policy be to go left at S2​ and right at S1​, forming a cycle that yields a reward of +1 repeatedly?</paragraph><paragraph>Is this interpretation correct? If not, could you please help clarify why this policy would or would not be optimal?</paragraph></document>
document:
For the Mars Rover example, the reward is +10 at S10​ and +1 at S1​. I am a bit confused about the optimal policy in this setting.

If the agent receives a reward of +1 each time it reaches S1​, then for a sufficiently large horizon H or in the infinite-horizon case, wouldn’t the optimal policy be to go left at S2​ and right at S1​, forming a cycle that yields a reward of +1 repeatedly?

Is this interpretation correct? If not, could you please help clarify why this policy would or would not be optimal?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question! First, to clarify the setup: in the Mars Rover example, the reward is $+1$ in state $s_1$ and $+10$ in state $s_7$, with zero reward in all other states. Just to make sure we are looking at the same example, not that it particularly affects the concept :) </paragraph><paragraph>In the <bold>finite-horizon case</bold>, the optimal policy depends on the horizon $H$. For example, if $H=1$, then it is optimal to move left at $s_2$, since there is never enough time to reach $s_7$ and collect the larger reward. More generally, for short horizons, policies that favor immediate rewards can be optimal.</paragraph><paragraph>In the <bold>infinite-horizon case</bold> setting, in this class, we typically consider the discounted formulation with discount factor $\gamma \in (0,1)$. While it is true that cycling near $s_1$ yields a positive reward repeatedly, the magnitude of the per-step reward still matters under discounting. A policy that eventually reaches $s_7$ and remains there achieves a discounted return on the order of $\frac{10}{1-\gamma}$, whereas remaining near $s_1$ yields a discounted return on the order of $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$. Thus, despite the delay in reaching $s_7$, the much larger per-step reward makes the "go right" policy optimal for sufficiently large horizons and typical values of $\gamma$.</paragraph><paragraph>Another common infinite-horizon formulation is the <bold>infinite-horizon average-reward</bold> <bold>case</bold>. Although the analysis in that case is different and more mathematically complex, a similar tradeoff between small and large recurring rewards arises. This formulation is beyond the scope of the current course material, but I'm happy to chat about it further if you are curious!<break/><break/>Does this help? </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Great question! First, to clarify the setup: in the Mars Rover example, the reward is $+1$ in state $s_1$ and $+10$ in state $s_7$, with zero reward in all other states. Just to make sure we are looking at the same example, not that it particularly affects the concept :) 

In the finite-horizon case, the optimal policy depends on the horizon $H$. For example, if $H=1$, then it is optimal to move left at $s_2$, since there is never enough time to reach $s_7$ and collect the larger reward. More generally, for short horizons, policies that favor immediate rewards can be optimal.

In the infinite-horizon case setting, in this class, we typically consider the discounted formulation with discount factor $\gamma \in (0,1)$. While it is true that cycling near $s_1$ yields a positive reward repeatedly, the magnitude of the per-step reward still matters under discounting. A policy that eventually reaches $s_7$ and remains there achieves a discounted return on the order of $\frac{10}{1-\gamma}$, whereas remaining near $s_1$ yields a discounted return on the order of $\frac{1}{1-\gamma}$. Thus, despite the delay in reaching $s_7$, the much larger per-step reward makes the "go right" policy optimal for sufficiently large horizons and typical values of $\gamma$.

Another common infinite-horizon formulation is the infinite-horizon average-reward case. Although the analysis in that case is different and more mathematically complex, a similar tradeoff between small and large recurring rewards arises. This formulation is beyond the scope of the current course material, but I'm happy to chat about it further if you are curious!

Does this help?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For the <bold>finite-horizon case</bold>, let us take H=31 and assume the agent starts from s4​. Under this setting, it seems that the optimal policy would be to move left at all states, except at s1​, where the agent moves right, thereby forming a cycle between s1​ and s2​. This cycle yields a reward of +1 each time the agent returns to s1​. Over the horizon, this would result in a total reward of approximately +14.</paragraph><paragraph>In contrast, if the agent always moves right, it eventually reaches the terminal state s7​ and receives a one-time reward of +10, after which no further reward can be collected. Since 14>10, it appears that the cycling policy would be optimal for this finite horizon.</paragraph><paragraph>Is this reasoning correct, or am I missing an important detail in how rewards are accumulated or how terminal states are treated in this example?</paragraph><paragraph>Further, even in the <bold>infinite-horizon case</bold>, if we reason from a practical (undiscounted) perspective rather than a theoretical discounted formulation, it seems that the agent would still prefer to remain in the s1​–s2​ cycle, since it can accumulate arbitrarily large reward over time. Could you please clarify why this intuition does or does not make sense? I would be happy to discuss this in-person if possible.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
For the finite-horizon case, let us take H=31 and assume the agent starts from s4​. Under this setting, it seems that the optimal policy would be to move left at all states, except at s1​, where the agent moves right, thereby forming a cycle between s1​ and s2​. This cycle yields a reward of +1 each time the agent returns to s1​. Over the horizon, this would result in a total reward of approximately +14.

In contrast, if the agent always moves right, it eventually reaches the terminal state s7​ and receives a one-time reward of +10, after which no further reward can be collected. Since 14>10, it appears that the cycling policy would be optimal for this finite horizon.

Is this reasoning correct, or am I missing an important detail in how rewards are accumulated or how terminal states are treated in this example?

Further, even in the infinite-horizon case, if we reason from a practical (undiscounted) perspective rather than a theoretical discounted formulation, it seems that the agent would still prefer to remain in the s1​–s2​ cycle, since it can accumulate arbitrarily large reward over time. Could you please clarify why this intuition does or does not make sense? I would be happy to discuss this in-person if possible.

comments.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>I think this might be a good thing to discuss in person at OH next week! </paragraph><paragraph>But in the mean time, I just want to note that s7 returns to itself with .6 probability, so your statement that the agent doesn’t collect any further reward after reaching s7 is not quite right and might be part of the confusion you describe in the first two paragraphs. </paragraph><paragraph>Additionally, I want to note that undiscounted infinite horizon MDPs are typically infinite horizon average reward MDPs, in which case we do still care about the magnitude of (infinitely accrued reward) due to the limit in the definition of such MDPs. Also, I would like to add that discount factors are not purely a theoretical construct, and are in part designed to avoid the type of situation you described in the last part of your paragraph. </paragraph><paragraph>In all, I think these are all great questions but might just be easier to clarify at OH—feel free to stop by mine or someone else’s and we’d be happy to discuss further :)</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
I think this might be a good thing to discuss in person at OH next week! 

But in the mean time, I just want to note that s7 returns to itself with .6 probability, so your statement that the agent doesn’t collect any further reward after reaching s7 is not quite right and might be part of the confusion you describe in the first two paragraphs. 

Additionally, I want to note that undiscounted infinite horizon MDPs are typically infinite horizon average reward MDPs, in which case we do still care about the magnitude of (infinitely accrued reward) due to the limit in the definition of such MDPs. Also, I would like to add that discount factors are not purely a theoretical construct, and are in part designed to avoid the type of situation you described in the last part of your paragraph. 

In all, I think these are all great questions but might just be easier to clarify at OH—feel free to stop by mine or someone else’s and we’d be happy to discuss further :)

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks, it clears some of my confusion. Let’s consider an example where the agent deterministically moves to the previous state by taking the left action (with probability 1) and to the next state by taking the right action (with probability 1).</paragraph><paragraph>In the infinite-horizon setting, if the agent follows the left action and forms a cycle, it receives a reward of 1−γ1​. On the other hand, if the agent moves right toward the terminal reward, it receives a reward of 100⋅γx, where x is the number of steps required to reach that state.</paragraph><paragraph>In this case, would the infinite-cycle policy be optimal for some values of the discount factor γ?</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Thanks, it clears some of my confusion. Let’s consider an example where the agent deterministically moves to the previous state by taking the left action (with probability 1) and to the next state by taking the right action (with probability 1).

In the infinite-horizon setting, if the agent follows the left action and forms a cycle, it receives a reward of 1−γ1​. On the other hand, if the agent moves right toward the terminal reward, it receives a reward of 100⋅γx, where x is the number of steps required to reach that state.

In this case, would the infinite-cycle policy be optimal for some values of the discount factor γ?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Glad to hear. Just to make sure we are on the same page, is the MDP you are considering something like the following? </paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>Letting action $L$ denote "going left" and $R$ denote "going right", the transitions are deterministic. </paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>For each $i \in \{2,\dots,N-1\}$, $$ P(s_{i-1}\mid s_i,L)=1, \qquad P(s_{i+1}\mid s_i,R)=1,$$ and $r(i)=0$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Edge cases for the first and last states:</paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>For the first state $s_1$, $$P(s_1\mid s_1,L)=1, \qquad P(s_2\mid s_1,R)=1,$$ and $r(1)=1$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>For the last state $s_N$, $$P(s_{N-1}\mid s_N,L)=1, \qquad P(s_N\mid s_N,R)=1,$$ and $r(N)=10$.</paragraph></list-item></list></list-item></list><paragraph>In this MDP, the optimal policy will depend on how large the discount factor $\gamma$ is relative to $N$. First, it should be easy to convince yourself that an optimal policy must be of the form</paragraph><paragraph>$$\pi_m(i)=\begin{cases}L, & i<m \\R, & i\ge m,\end{cases}$$<break/>for some $m \in \{1,\dots,N\}$.</paragraph><paragraph>So, the question really is: how large should $m$ be? Under $\pi_m$, the value function can be computed explicitly. If $i<m$, the agent deterministically reaches $s_1$ in $(i-1)$ steps and then receives reward $1$ forever, so<break/>$$V^{\pi_m}(i)=\frac{\gamma^{\,i-1}}{1-\gamma}, \qquad i<m.$$</paragraph><paragraph>If $i\ge m$, the agent deterministically reaches $s_N$ in $(N-i)$ steps and then receives reward $10$ forever, so</paragraph><paragraph>$$V^{\pi_m}(i)=\frac{10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}}{1-\gamma}, \qquad i\ge m.$$</paragraph><paragraph>In particular,</paragraph><paragraph>$$V^{\pi_m}(1)=\frac{1}{1-\gamma}, \qquad V^{\pi_m}(N)=\frac{10}{1-\gamma}.$$</paragraph><paragraph>Therefore, at state $i$ it is optimal to go left iff the return from heading to $s_1$ is at least the return from heading to $s_N$, i.e.,</paragraph><paragraph>$$\frac{\gamma^{\,i-1}}{1-\gamma} \ge \frac{10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}}{1-\gamma}.$$</paragraph><paragraph>Equivalently,</paragraph><paragraph>$$\gamma^{\,i-1} \ge 10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad</paragraph><paragraph>\gamma^{\,2i-N-1} \ge 10.$$</paragraph><paragraph>Let $i^\star$ be the (real-valued) indifference point defined by equality:</paragraph><paragraph>$$\gamma^{\,2i^\star-N-1}=10 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 2i^\star-N-1=\log_\gamma(10)=\frac{\ln 10}{\ln \gamma}.$$</paragraph><paragraph>Thus,</paragraph><paragraph>$$i^\star=\frac{N+1+\log_\gamma(10)}{2},$$</paragraph><paragraph>and one optimal threshold choice is</paragraph><paragraph>$$m^\star=\left\lceil \frac{N+1+\log_\gamma(10)}{2} \right\rceil.$$</paragraph><paragraph>For $i<m^\star$, the optimal action is $L$ (go left toward $s_1$), while for $i\ge m^\star$, the optimal action is $R$ (go right toward $s_N$).<break/><break/>This is a good example to consider, because it shows you explicitly how the optimal policy depends on the discount factor and $N$. When $\gamma$ is small, more states will opt to go left, to get reward faster. When $\gamma$ is larger, more states will op to go right, to get larger reward even if it takes more time to get there. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Glad to hear. Just to make sure we are on the same page, is the MDP you are considering something like the following? 

Letting action $L$ denote "going left" and $R$ denote "going right", the transitions are deterministic. 

For each $i \in \{2,\dots,N-1\}$, $$ P(s_{i-1}\mid s_i,L)=1, \qquad P(s_{i+1}\mid s_i,R)=1,$$ and $r(i)=0$.

Edge cases for the first and last states:

For the first state $s_1$, $$P(s_1\mid s_1,L)=1, \qquad P(s_2\mid s_1,R)=1,$$ and $r(1)=1$.

For the last state $s_N$, $$P(s_{N-1}\mid s_N,L)=1, \qquad P(s_N\mid s_N,R)=1,$$ and $r(N)=10$.

In this MDP, the optimal policy will depend on how large the discount factor $\gamma$ is relative to $N$. First, it should be easy to convince yourself that an optimal policy must be of the form

$$\pi_m(i)=\begin{cases}L, & i<m \\R, & i\ge m,\end{cases}$$
for some $m \in \{1,\dots,N\}$.

So, the question really is: how large should $m$ be? Under $\pi_m$, the value function can be computed explicitly. If $i<m$, the agent deterministically reaches $s_1$ in $(i-1)$ steps and then receives reward $1$ forever, so
$$V^{\pi_m}(i)=\frac{\gamma^{\,i-1}}{1-\gamma}, \qquad i<m.$$

If $i\ge m$, the agent deterministically reaches $s_N$ in $(N-i)$ steps and then receives reward $10$ forever, so

$$V^{\pi_m}(i)=\frac{10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}}{1-\gamma}, \qquad i\ge m.$$

In particular,

$$V^{\pi_m}(1)=\frac{1}{1-\gamma}, \qquad V^{\pi_m}(N)=\frac{10}{1-\gamma}.$$

Therefore, at state $i$ it is optimal to go left iff the return from heading to $s_1$ is at least the return from heading to $s_N$, i.e.,

$$\frac{\gamma^{\,i-1}}{1-\gamma} \ge \frac{10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}}{1-\gamma}.$$

Equivalently,

$$\gamma^{\,i-1} \ge 10\,\gamma^{\,N-i}\quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad

\gamma^{\,2i-N-1} \ge 10.$$

Let $i^\star$ be the (real-valued) indifference point defined by equality:

$$\gamma^{\,2i^\star-N-1}=10 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad 2i^\star-N-1=\log_\gamma(10)=\frac{\ln 10}{\ln \gamma}.$$

Thus,

$$i^\star=\frac{N+1+\log_\gamma(10)}{2},$$

and one optimal threshold choice is

$$m^\star=\left\lceil \frac{N+1+\log_\gamma(10)}{2} \right\rceil.$$

For $i<m^\star$, the optimal action is $L$ (go left toward $s_1$), while for $i\ge m^\star$, the optimal action is $R$ (go right toward $s_N$).

This is a good example to consider, because it shows you explicitly how the optimal policy depends on the discount factor and $N$. When $\gamma$ is small, more states will opt to go left, to get reward faster. When $\gamma$ is larger, more states will op to go right, to get larger reward even if it takes more time to get there.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>That's really helpful. I am just confused for the states(i > m). Once the agent reaches S7, it stops right? So, shouldn't the reward be only 10 <italic>γ^(N</italic>−<italic>i)?</italic> And for the states with (i < m), shouldn't the reward be only 1 / (1 - <italic>γ</italic>), why there is a <italic>γ</italic> in the numerator?<break/></paragraph></document>
comments.document: That's really helpful. I am just confused for the states(i > m). Once the agent reaches S7, it stops right? So, shouldn't the reward be only 10 γ^(N−i)? And for the states with (i < m), shouldn't the reward be only 1 / (1 - γ), why there is a γ in the numerator?

comments.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>Not quite, notice that in the formulation I wrote above, if the agent goes right at state they return to state N with probability 1. So, state N is an absorbing state, and once you reach it, you collect the reward of 10 (discounted appropriately) forever. Does that make sense? </paragraph></document>
comments.document: Not quite, notice that in the formulation I wrote above, if the agent goes right at state they return to state N with probability 1. So, state N is an absorbing state, and once you reach it, you collect the reward of 10 (discounted appropriately) forever. Does that make sense?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Got it! Thank you so much :)</paragraph></document>

title: A1 Problem 1
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The optimal policy is defined as a mapping from state to action for deterministic policies.</paragraph><paragraph>When we are given the start state, I think it is possible for the optimal policy to not visit certain states in order to get the maximum cumulative reward - so can we say in such a case that the optimal policy function is not defined for the states that are not visited under such a policy?</paragraph></document>
document:
The optimal policy is defined as a mapping from state to action for deterministic policies.

When we are given the start state, I think it is possible for the optimal policy to not visit certain states in order to get the maximum cumulative reward - so can we say in such a case that the optimal policy function is not defined for the states that are not visited under such a policy?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question! An optimal policy $\pi^*$ is defined as maximizing the value starting from any state, not just the initial state. For problem 1, if we consider the example where $H=4$ and we start at state $s=3$, the optimal policy is to go from state 3 to state 2 to state 1 to state 0, so $\pi(i)$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ is sell (we are ignoring the number of steps left until horizon is reached because that doesn't impact the optimal action for $H=4$). However, even though state 4 is not reached in the optimal policy, we can still define $\pi(4) = \text{sell}$ because that would be the optimal action to take at $s=4$ with $H=4$ (no matter how many timesteps we have left).</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Great question! An optimal policy $\pi^*$ is defined as maximizing the value starting from any state, not just the initial state. For problem 1, if we consider the example where $H=4$ and we start at state $s=3$, the optimal policy is to go from state 3 to state 2 to state 1 to state 0, so $\pi(i)$ for $i = 0,1,2,3$ is sell (we are ignoring the number of steps left until horizon is reached because that doesn't impact the optimal action for $H=4$). However, even though state 4 is not reached in the optimal policy, we can still define $\pi(4) = \text{sell}$ because that would be the optimal action to take at $s=4$ with $H=4$ (no matter how many timesteps we have left).

title: HW1 3f
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I see that we have the following definitions</paragraph><math>\pi(s) = \argmax_{a} [{r(s,a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s')}] \\</math><math>(BV)(s) = \max_a \left( r(s, a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s') \right)</math><paragraph>What is the difference between $V^\pi$ and BV in this case?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
I see that we have the following definitions

$$\pi(s) = \argmax_{a} [{r(s,a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s')}] \\$$

$$(BV)(s) = \max_a \left( r(s, a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s') \right)$$

What is the difference between $V^\pi$ and BV in this case?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>$\pi(s) = \argmax_{a} [{r(s,a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s')}]$</paragraph><paragraph>$(BV)(s) = \max_a \left( r(s, a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s') \right)$</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>I think in these equations you have to think of $V$'s as some numerical vectors during a particular iteration of value iteration. One way to think of those is like $\hat{V}_k$ which when applied the Bellman operation on (giving you $\hat{V}_{k+1}$) will eventually converge to the $V^{opt}$ as $k\to\infty$. Also see Ishani's comment on my response on #19. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
$\pi(s) = \argmax_{a} [{r(s,a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s')}]$

$(BV)(s) = \max_a \left( r(s, a) + \gamma\sum_{s' \in S}p(s'|s,a)V(s') \right)$



I think in these equations you have to think of $V$'s as some numerical vectors during a particular iteration of value iteration. One way to think of those is like $\hat{V}_k$ which when applied the Bellman operation on (giving you $\hat{V}_{k+1}$) will eventually converge to the $V^{opt}$ as $k\to\infty$. Also see Ishani's comment on my response on #19.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Herman's response is right! Another way to think about is that $V^\pi$ is the <italic>fixed point</italic> of the Bellman operator, and the equation for $(B^\pi V)(s)$ is a single <italic>fixed point iteration</italic> on $V$. Since $B^\pi$ is $\gamma$-contractive, repeatedly applying the $B^\pi$ to any $V$ is guaranteed to converge to the fixed point $V^\pi$. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: Herman's response is right! Another way to think about is that $V^\pi$ is the fixed point of the Bellman operator, and the equation for $(B^\pi V)(s)$ is a single fixed point iteration on $V$. Since $B^\pi$ is $\gamma$-contractive, repeatedly applying the $B^\pi$ to any $V$ is guaranteed to converge to the fixed point $V^\pi$.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>"Since B is γ-contractive, repeatedly applying the B to any V is guaranteed to converge to the fixed point Vπ"</paragraph><paragraph>I thought V^pi is the fixed point of B^pi, not the fixed point of B? Is it both?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
comments.document:
"Since B is γ-contractive, repeatedly applying the B to any V is guaranteed to converge to the fixed point Vπ"

I thought V^pi is the fixed point of B^pi, not the fixed point of B? Is it both?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>sorry, above I meant $B^\pi$, and edited accordingly </paragraph></document>
comments.document: sorry, above I meant $B^\pi$, and edited accordingly

title: Inifinite-horizon time dependence
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>To clarify, must infinite-horizon policies be time-independent? Some finite horizon policies can be time dependent (i.e. at state $s$ do a different action at time step $t_1$ vs $t_2$), but my understanding is that in an infinite-horizon policy, the action at $s$ will not change based on time?</paragraph></document>
document: To clarify, must infinite-horizon policies be time-independent? Some finite horizon policies can be time dependent (i.e. at state $s$ do a different action at time step $t_1$ vs $t_2$), but my understanding is that in an infinite-horizon policy, the action at $s$ will not change based on time?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I would say there's nothing stopping you from having a non-stationary policy for an infinite-horizon task.<break/>However at this point in class, (for the setup introduced in lecture 2,) can you see why such a policy can potentially be sub-optimal?</paragraph><paragraph>EDIT: Actually, it is technically possible for an infinite-horizon MDP to have a non-stationary optimal policy (example, if two actions have the same optimal value from a given state, we can arbitrarily choose a different one depending on time step). However, it's always true that there will be an optimal stationary policy as well. The key here is that the optimal policy is sometimes not unique, and in some cases we should think about a set of optimal policies, which can be stationary or non-stationary.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
I would say there's nothing stopping you from having a non-stationary policy for an infinite-horizon task.
However at this point in class, (for the setup introduced in lecture 2,) can you see why such a policy can potentially be sub-optimal?

EDIT: Actually, it is technically possible for an infinite-horizon MDP to have a non-stationary optimal policy (example, if two actions have the same optimal value from a given state, we can arbitrarily choose a different one depending on time step). However, it's always true that there will be an optimal stationary policy as well. The key here is that the optimal policy is sometimes not unique, and in some cases we should think about a set of optimal policies, which can be stationary or non-stationary.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think because if the state and future dynamics are identical regardless of when the state is reached, acting differently at different times can’t increase value. Any time-dependent policy can be replaced by a stationary one that is at least as good.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: I think because if the state and future dynamics are identical regardless of when the state is reached, acting differently at different times can’t increase value. Any time-dependent policy can be replaced by a stationary one that is at least as good.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Likely because we will have an explosion of the state space. I.e. we have shown that the state space is |A|^|S|. Now, if we add time dimensions to the state space, we will end up in a exponentially high num. states.<break/>Now, since policy iteration / value iteration algorithms iterate over state space, they will have a horrendous performance and potentially never converge</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Likely because we will have an explosion of the state space. I.e. we have shown that the state space is |A|^|S|. Now, if we add time dimensions to the state space, we will end up in a exponentially high num. states.
Now, since policy iteration / value iteration algorithms iterate over state space, they will have a horrendous performance and potentially never converge

title: Fixed Point
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi,<break/><break/>Is it the case that $V^\pi$ is always the fixed point for any $B$, or only for $B^\pi$?</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi,

Is it the case that $V^\pi$ is always the fixed point for any $B$, or only for $B^\pi$?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think if I'm understanding your question correctly, in general no. I think spending some time to do problem 3 (b) on the homework might help with this.<break/><break/>First, we know that different policies will produce different value functions $V^\pi$. Now, consider two different policies, $\pi$ and $\pi'$ that produce different value functions. We also know that $V^\pi$ and $V^{\pi'}$ are each the fixed point for $B^\pi$ and $B^{\pi'}$ respectively.<break/><break/>However, from the homework, we know that fixed points are unique. Therefore, $V^\pi$ can't be the fixed point for $B^{\pi'}$.<break/><break/>Let me know if this is confusing, and I can elaborate more!</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
I think if I'm understanding your question correctly, in general no. I think spending some time to do problem 3 (b) on the homework might help with this.

First, we know that different policies will produce different value functions $V^\pi$. Now, consider two different policies, $\pi$ and $\pi'$ that produce different value functions. We also know that $V^\pi$ and $V^{\pi'}$ are each the fixed point for $B^\pi$ and $B^{\pi'}$ respectively.

However, from the homework, we know that fixed points are unique. Therefore, $V^\pi$ can't be the fixed point for $B^{\pi'}$.

Let me know if this is confusing, and I can elaborate more!

title: Assignment 1 #1 clarifying questions
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I had a couple of clarifying questions about Assignment 1 problem 1.</paragraph><paragraph>Firstly, in lecture, the policy was defined as a probability distribution dependent only on the state, i.e., pi(a|t) = P(a_t=a|s_t=s). In the problem, if stock (s) entirely defines the state, then this would mean our policy's decision to buy or sell would depend only on the stock, meaning, for example, we would always have to buy when s = 3, regardless of the time step. This seems restrictive, so I was wondering if the state is actually defined both by the stock <italic>and</italic> the time step, which would then allow us to choose different actions at different time steps for the same stock? The problem seems to indicate the latter interpretation since "At any <italic>time</italic>, the agent has two actions..."</paragraph><paragraph>Secondly, subproblems 1c and 1d both mention "the optimal policy" in the finite and infinite time horizon cases. Can we find examples of gamma and H such that <italic>an</italic> optimal policy for the finite horizon case matches <italic>an</italic> optimal policy for the infinite horizon case, or must the matching optimal policy be <italic>the only</italic> optimal policy in both cases for the answer to be valid?</paragraph><paragraph>Thanks so much!</paragraph></document>
document:
I had a couple of clarifying questions about Assignment 1 problem 1.

Firstly, in lecture, the policy was defined as a probability distribution dependent only on the state, i.e., pi(a|t) = P(a_t=a|s_t=s). In the problem, if stock (s) entirely defines the state, then this would mean our policy's decision to buy or sell would depend only on the stock, meaning, for example, we would always have to buy when s = 3, regardless of the time step. This seems restrictive, so I was wondering if the state is actually defined both by the stock and the time step, which would then allow us to choose different actions at different time steps for the same stock? The problem seems to indicate the latter interpretation since "At any time, the agent has two actions..."

Secondly, subproblems 1c and 1d both mention "the optimal policy" in the finite and infinite time horizon cases. Can we find examples of gamma and H such that an optimal policy for the finite horizon case matches an optimal policy for the infinite horizon case, or must the matching optimal policy be the only optimal policy in both cases for the answer to be valid?

Thanks so much!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Yes, in general, for finite-horizon tasks we can have the policy be <italic>non-stationary</italic>, as in, it can depend on the time-step. Prof. Brunskill spoke about this briefly at the end of lecture 2, where you can think of having different value functions depending on how much time you have left, and then the corresponding policy can also vary.<break/><break/>I think I see your point for part (c). Yes, if there's a choice of $\gamma$ and $H$ for which there are multiple optimal policies, I would just check <strike>if the set of such policies is the same</strike> they have any optimal policies in common.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Yes, in general, for finite-horizon tasks we can have the policy be non-stationary, as in, it can depend on the time-step. Prof. Brunskill spoke about this briefly at the end of lecture 2, where you can think of having different value functions depending on how much time you have left, and then the corresponding policy can also vary.

I think I see your point for part (c). Yes, if there's a choice of $\gamma$ and $H$ for which there are multiple optimal policies, I would just check if the set of such policies is the same they have any optimal policies in common.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just to confirm (regarding your second point), if there is a set A of optimal policies for the finite-horizon MDP with horizon H and a set B of optimal policies for the infinite-horizon MDP with discount factor gamma, then we would need to show that A = B, not just that <italic>some policy</italic> is in both A and B?</paragraph><paragraph>Also, I may have misinterpreted 1c to be referring to our specific inventory MDP. When it asks if there is "ever" a pair of values for gamma and H, are we allowed to construct some other inventory MDP that satisfies this condition? (Similarly for 1d, when it asks if there "always" exists gamma and H satisfying the condition, are we considering all possible inventory MDPs?)</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Just to confirm (regarding your second point), if there is a set A of optimal policies for the finite-horizon MDP with horizon H and a set B of optimal policies for the infinite-horizon MDP with discount factor gamma, then we would need to show that A = B, not just that some policy is in both A and B?

Also, I may have misinterpreted 1c to be referring to our specific inventory MDP. When it asks if there is "ever" a pair of values for gamma and H, are we allowed to construct some other inventory MDP that satisfies this condition? (Similarly for 1d, when it asks if there "always" exists gamma and H satisfying the condition, are we considering all possible inventory MDPs?)

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Oh fair point, you can check if they just have any optimal policy in common.</paragraph><paragraph>Hmmm, for 1 c and d, it should be this specific inventory MDP as described in the prompt, and you're comparing (1) a finite horizon version where $\gamma = 1$ (undiscounted) and $H \in \mathbb{N}$, v.s. (2) an infinite horizon version where $\gamma \in [0,1)$.</paragraph><paragraph>For part c), you're allowed to vary $\gamma$ for the infinite horizon version and $H$ for the finite horizon version to see if there ever exists a choice where there exists a shared optimal policy. If you can find one, then list the choice of $\gamma$ and $H$. If you can't, explain why.</paragraph><paragraph>For part d), $H$ is arbitrary but fixed for the finite horizon case, and you're asked if there always exists a corresponding choice of $\gamma$ such that <strike>there exists an optimal policy in the infinite horizon version that matches an optimal policy in the finite horizon version with $H$</strike>. (See edit) So you're not allowed to choose $H$ anymore, but just given any $H$, can you always choose some $\gamma$?</paragraph><paragraph>So again, in both cases, please reason about the specific inventory MDP. Let me know if this makes sense!<break/><break/>EDIT: After talking with Ishani, a better thing to ask might be: For arbitrary $H$, which could have a set of optimal policies $P_H$, does there always exist a choice of $\gamma$ such that the set of optimal policies $P_{\gamma}$ for the infinite horizon version of the task is a subset of $P_H$? This might be a technically more correct question to ask, since there may exist $\gamma$ for which there are multiple (potentially non-stationary) optimal policies.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Oh fair point, you can check if they just have any optimal policy in common.

Hmmm, for 1 c and d, it should be this specific inventory MDP as described in the prompt, and you're comparing (1) a finite horizon version where $\gamma = 1$ (undiscounted) and $H \in \mathbb{N}$, v.s. (2) an infinite horizon version where $\gamma \in [0,1)$.

For part c), you're allowed to vary $\gamma$ for the infinite horizon version and $H$ for the finite horizon version to see if there ever exists a choice where there exists a shared optimal policy. If you can find one, then list the choice of $\gamma$ and $H$. If you can't, explain why.

For part d), $H$ is arbitrary but fixed for the finite horizon case, and you're asked if there always exists a corresponding choice of $\gamma$ such that there exists an optimal policy in the infinite horizon version that matches an optimal policy in the finite horizon version with $H$. (See edit) So you're not allowed to choose $H$ anymore, but just given any $H$, can you always choose some $\gamma$?

So again, in both cases, please reason about the specific inventory MDP. Let me know if this makes sense!

EDIT: After talking with Ishani, a better thing to ask might be: For arbitrary $H$, which could have a set of optimal policies $P_H$, does there always exist a choice of $\gamma$ such that the set of optimal policies $P_{\gamma}$ for the infinite horizon version of the task is a subset of $P_H$? This might be a technically more correct question to ask, since there may exist $\gamma$ for which there are multiple (potentially non-stationary) optimal policies.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Ahh yes, that does make sense. Thank you so much!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Ahh yes, that does make sense. Thank you so much!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For part (d), I'll also clarify that for the set of optimal finite horizon policies for $H$, which we'll call $P_H$, and the set of optimal infinite horizon policies for a $\gamma$, which we'll call $P_{\gamma}$, it's not necessary to show that the two sets are disjoint.<break/><break/>Instead, it may be better to ask if for any $H$, can we always choose a $\gamma$ such that $P_{\gamma} \subseteq P_H$?<break/><break/>See my comment <link href="https://edstem.org/us/courses/91118/discussion/7498416?answer=17401688">here</link> for why this might be a more correct question to ask.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
For part (d), I'll also clarify that for the set of optimal finite horizon policies for $H$, which we'll call $P_H$, and the set of optimal infinite horizon policies for a $\gamma$, which we'll call $P_{\gamma}$, it's not necessary to show that the two sets are disjoint.

Instead, it may be better to ask if for any $H$, can we always choose a $\gamma$ such that $P_{\gamma} \subseteq P_H$?

See my comment here for why this might be a more correct question to ask.

title: About assignment 1 question 3-(i)
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I'm right now struggling with finding the proof for the following equation.</paragraph><math>V^{\pi}(s)\ge V^*(s)-\frac{\epsilon}{1-\gamma}</math><paragraph>If possible, could you give a hint on how to approach the problem? I'm trying to see if I could reuse the inequalities from problem 3-(e), but I'm not sure if that's not the right approach.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
I'm right now struggling with finding the proof for the following equation.

$$V^{\pi}(s)\ge V^*(s)-\frac{\epsilon}{1-\gamma}$$

If possible, could you give a hint on how to approach the problem? I'm trying to see if I could reuse the inequalities from problem 3-(e), but I'm not sure if that's not the right approach.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This should be similar to problem 3(f), but now try to see what the additional assumption of $V^* \le V$ gives you.<break/><break/>If it's hard to reason about entire vectors at once, maybe start with $V^* (s)$ and $V^\pi (s)$ and see what you can say about individual elements first, then see if you can draw any conclusions related to the vectors from that.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
This should be similar to problem 3(f), but now try to see what the additional assumption of $V^* \le V$ gives you.

If it's hard to reason about entire vectors at once, maybe start with $V^* (s)$ and $V^\pi (s)$ and see what you can say about individual elements first, then see if you can draw any conclusions related to the vectors from that.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The problem hint at the bottom of the question is extremely useful. Think about why it's given alongside the additional assumption.</paragraph></document>

title: Pset1, problem3 part (f)
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi,<break/><break/>If $\pi$ is a greedy policy (does that mean that $B^\pi$ = $B$?) and if so, wouldn't <break/>this mean that $V^\pi = V^*$ for arbitrary V - but then how does the lower bound make sense?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Hi,

If $\pi$ is a greedy policy (does that mean that $B^\pi$ = $B$?) and if so, wouldn't 
this mean that $V^\pi = V^*$ for arbitrary V - but then how does the lower bound make sense?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Note that "the greedy policy" must be defined with respect to some value function estimate (or q function estimate). If $\pi$ is a greedy policy with respect to a particular $V$, then $B^\pi V = B V$. However, this does not in general mean that $B^\pi = B$. In order for $B^\pi = B$, we need $B^\pi V' = BV$ for <italic>all</italic> $V'$. <break/><break/>Does this answer your question? </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Note that "the greedy policy" must be defined with respect to some value function estimate (or q function estimate). If $\pi$ is a greedy policy with respect to a particular $V$, then $B^\pi V = B V$. However, this does not in general mean that $B^\pi = B$. In order for $B^\pi = B$, we need $B^\pi V' = BV$ for all $V'$. 

Does this answer your question?

It's just that when considering the <italic>optimal</italic> policy for an infinite-horizon MDP, it is stationary.<break/>Intuitively here, if you're in state $s$ at time $t = 1$ vs $t = 100$, the optimal decision you make should be no different if we have "infinite" steps left, where-as if we know that the episode ends at say, $H = t = 101$, then our optimal policy could vary from $t = 1$ to $t = 100$ for the same state.</paragraph></document>

Intuitively here, if you're in state $s$ at time $t = 1$ vs $t = 100$, the optimal decision you make should be no different if we have "infinite" steps left, where-as if we know that the episode ends at say, $H = t = 101$, then our optimal policy could vary from $t = 1$ to $t = 100$ for the same state.
