title: Policy Evaluation Coding
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>When computing the new value_function at step $k$ (so $V_k$) for every $s$, and for example if we have states $s_1$ and $s_2$ --  after we computed $V_{k}(s_1)$ and move on to computing $V_{k}(s_2)$ , do we use the new $V_{k}(s_1)$ or $V_{k-1}(s_1)$ for its backup inside the summation over all $s'$?<break/><break/><break/>Thanks!</paragraph></document>
document:
When computing the new value_function at step $k$ (so $V_k$) for every $s$, and for example if we have states $s_1$ and $s_2$ --  after we computed $V_{k}(s_1)$ and move on to computing $V_{k}(s_2)$ , do we use the new $V_{k}(s_1)$ or $V_{k-1}(s_1)$ for its backup inside the summation over all $s'$?


Thanks!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In standard value iteration, you would update all states simultaneously at step k. </paragraph><paragraph>(That said, there are many variants that do in-place and/or asynchronous updates, and many of these variants can also converge, though they are not as standard.)</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
In standard value iteration, you would update all states simultaneously at step k. 

(That said, there are many variants that do in-place and/or asynchronous updates, and many of these variants can also converge, though they are not as standard.)

title: How to think about Reinforcement Learning?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>While thinking about a project proposal, I realized that I don't know how to think about RL, it was briefly introduced in lecture 1 that it's making "optimal decisions under uncertainty". But it seems like as you start to build the field, it effectively just means tweaking states in service of maximization/minimization towards a goal. And that starts to sound a lot like optimization. So is the right way of thinking about RL as a subfield of optimization where the "gradient signal" is replaced by a more generalized time delayed gradient signal? Does this question even make sense?</paragraph></document>
document: While thinking about a project proposal, I realized that I don't know how to think about RL, it was briefly introduced in lecture 1 that it's making "optimal decisions under uncertainty". But it seems like as you start to build the field, it effectively just means tweaking states in service of maximization/minimization towards a goal. And that starts to sound a lot like optimization. So is the right way of thinking about RL as a subfield of optimization where the "gradient signal" is replaced by a more generalized time delayed gradient signal? Does this question even make sense?

answers.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>This is a great question! And one which I don’t think admits a “right” or “wrong” answer, though I can offer my own perspective. I’d also encourage you to read the beginning of Sutton & Barto for some high level perspective directly from the people who helped develop this field. </paragraph><paragraph>It is absolutely true that RL often involves optimization, as Prof. Brunskill mentioned in lecture 1. But I personally don’t think of RL as just optimization with a delayed or noisy gradient. One thing that I think characterizes RL compared to other branches of ML is that RL always has some notion of actions, decisions, interventions, etc. This means that the decision you make now impacts the distribution of data you see in the future, and possibly the set of decisions you can make. </paragraph><paragraph>Time delay and credit assignment are certainly a big part of what makes RL hard. But you can also have time delay without any notion of decision making (e.g., forecasting the weather). What I think really distinguishes RL is that you are learning a policy for making good decisions over time in an environment you interact with, rather than optimizing a fixed objective over a static dataset.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
This is a great question! And one which I don’t think admits a “right” or “wrong” answer, though I can offer my own perspective. I’d also encourage you to read the beginning of Sutton & Barto for some high level perspective directly from the people who helped develop this field. 

It is absolutely true that RL often involves optimization, as Prof. Brunskill mentioned in lecture 1. But I personally don’t think of RL as just optimization with a delayed or noisy gradient. One thing that I think characterizes RL compared to other branches of ML is that RL always has some notion of actions, decisions, interventions, etc. This means that the decision you make now impacts the distribution of data you see in the future, and possibly the set of decisions you can make. 

Time delay and credit assignment are certainly a big part of what makes RL hard. But you can also have time delay without any notion of decision making (e.g., forecasting the weather). What I think really distinguishes RL is that you are learning a policy for making good decisions over time in an environment you interact with, rather than optimizing a fixed objective over a static dataset.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for this response, I've been trying to answer this question for the past 6 weeks for myself to see where it fits in my mental map. For now the answer I've arrived at is fancy pants, system in the loop lookahead optimization where both the agent and the environment can be dynamic. Not sure if this is accurate but this is my one line takeaway so far to help me think about the class of problems where I can apply this.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks for this response, I've been trying to answer this question for the past 6 weeks for myself to see where it fits in my mental map. For now the answer I've arrived at is fancy pants, system in the loop lookahead optimization where both the agent and the environment can be dynamic. Not sure if this is accurate but this is my one line takeaway so far to help me think about the class of problems where I can apply this.

title: Question about definitions in question 3
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>"When we use the term V in these expressions as an “arbitrary value function”, we mean that V is an arbitrary |S|-dimensional vector which need not be aligned with the definition of the MDP at all. On the other hand, V π is a value function that is achieved by some policy π in the MDP. For example, say the MDP has 2 states and only negative immediate rewards. V = [1, 1] would be a valid choice for V even though this value function can never be achieved by any policy π, but we can never have a V π = [1, 1]. This distinction between V and V π is important for this question and more broadly in reinforcement learning."</paragraph><paragraph>So does this mean that V is any sample from n-dimensional R and V(pi) is a constrained sample where your constraints make some part of the n-dimensional R infeasible so something similar to how a robotic arm has a constrained configuration space because of joint limits?<break/><break/>Why is this distinction important to RL and why were these definitions introduced in this question?</paragraph></document>
document:
"When we use the term V in these expressions as an “arbitrary value function”, we mean that V is an arbitrary |S|-dimensional vector which need not be aligned with the definition of the MDP at all. On the other hand, V π is a value function that is achieved by some policy π in the MDP. For example, say the MDP has 2 states and only negative immediate rewards. V = [1, 1] would be a valid choice for V even though this value function can never be achieved by any policy π, but we can never have a V π = [1, 1]. This distinction between V and V π is important for this question and more broadly in reinforcement learning."

So does this mean that V is any sample from n-dimensional R and V(pi) is a constrained sample where your constraints make some part of the n-dimensional R infeasible so something similar to how a robotic arm has a constrained configuration space because of joint limits?

Why is this distinction important to RL and why were these definitions introduced in this question?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>First two clarifications/recaps: </paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>An "arbitrary value function" is any vector in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ (completely unconstrained.) </paragraph><paragraph>Meanwhile, for a fixed policy $\pi$, $V^\pi$ is the unique vector in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ such that $V^\pi = B^\pi V^\pi$, where, you should recall that $B^\pi$ is the Bellman backup of $\pi$. Thus, there is a one to one mapping from $\pi$ to $V^\pi$. </paragraph><paragraph>Now, I think what you meant to ask is something like the following (please feel free to correct me if not). Suppose we consider the set of "realizable" value functions that is, let <break/>$$\Gamma = \{V \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|} : V = V^\pi \text{ for some policy } \pi \}.$$</paragraph><paragraph>Then, clearly, $\Gamma$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$, but does $\Gamma$ have any special/important properties or constraints compared to $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$? </paragraph><paragraph>Indeed, it does, and this is relevant to a lot of the theoretical RL literature. To give you some examples, note that unlike arbitrary vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$, if $V' \in \Gamma$, then </paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>$\Vert V' \Vert_\infty \leq \max_{s, a} |R(s,a)|$ </paragraph></list-item></list><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>$V' \leq V^{\pi^\star}$ (entrywise)</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>These types of properties can (1) be helpful for proving various statistical guarantees, which would not be true of arbitrary $|S|$-dimensional vectors and (2) can also be helpful in developing geometric intuition about certain linear programming (interior point methods, simplex method) approaches for solving MDPs.</paragraph><paragraph>These definitions are introduced in this question to get you thinking about values of policies versus arbitrary vectors as well as so that the problem sub-parts are clear.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
First two clarifications/recaps: 



An "arbitrary value function" is any vector in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ (completely unconstrained.) 

Meanwhile, for a fixed policy $\pi$, $V^\pi$ is the unique vector in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$ such that $V^\pi = B^\pi V^\pi$, where, you should recall that $B^\pi$ is the Bellman backup of $\pi$. Thus, there is a one to one mapping from $\pi$ to $V^\pi$. 

Now, I think what you meant to ask is something like the following (please feel free to correct me if not). Suppose we consider the set of "realizable" value functions that is, let 
$$\Gamma = \{V \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|} : V = V^\pi \text{ for some policy } \pi \}.$$

Then, clearly, $\Gamma$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$, but does $\Gamma$ have any special/important properties or constraints compared to $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$? 

Indeed, it does, and this is relevant to a lot of the theoretical RL literature. To give you some examples, note that unlike arbitrary vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{|S|}$, if $V' \in \Gamma$, then 

$\Vert V' \Vert_\infty \leq \max_{s, a} |R(s,a)|$ 

$V' \leq V^{\pi^\star}$ (entrywise)

These types of properties can (1) be helpful for proving various statistical guarantees, which would not be true of arbitrary $|S|$-dimensional vectors and (2) can also be helpful in developing geometric intuition about certain linear programming (interior point methods, simplex method) approaches for solving MDPs.

These definitions are introduced in this question to get you thinking about values of policies versus arbitrary vectors as well as so that the problem sub-parts are clear.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This finally made sense. Thanks!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: This finally made sense. Thanks!

title: Contraction Operator definition
content: In the slides from the 2nd lecture, isn't the contraction definition missing a \gamma?
document: In the slides from the 2nd lecture, isn't the contraction definition missing a \gamma?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In general, we say that $O: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{X}$ is a <italic>contraction operator</italic> over the set $\mathcal{X}$ under the norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ if for any $X, X' \in \mathcal{X}$, $\Vert OX - OX' \Vert <  \Vert X - X' \Vert$. <break/><break/>Further, for any $c \in [0, 1)$, we say that $O: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{X}$ is a $c$-<italic>contraction operator</italic> over the set $\mathcal{X}$ under the norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ if for any $X, X' \in \mathcal{X}$, $\Vert OX - OX' \Vert \leq  c \Vert X - X' \Vert$. <break/><break/>So, I believe the slide is almost correct, except that the $\leq$ should technically be a $<$. <break/><break/>However, you are right that in general, in this class we will often work with $\gamma$-contractions. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
In general, we say that $O: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{X}$ is a contraction operator over the set $\mathcal{X}$ under the norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ if for any $X, X' \in \mathcal{X}$, $\Vert OX - OX' \Vert <  \Vert X - X' \Vert$. 

Further, for any $c \in [0, 1)$, we say that $O: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{X}$ is a $c$-contraction operator over the set $\mathcal{X}$ under the norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ if for any $X, X' \in \mathcal{X}$, $\Vert OX - OX' \Vert \leq  c \Vert X - X' \Vert$. 

So, I believe the slide is almost correct, except that the $\leq$ should technically be a $<$. 

However, you are right that in general, in this class we will often work with $\gamma$-contractions.

title: Team up for RL project: Aiming for a paper with clever, feasible ideas
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi everyone,</paragraph><paragraph>I’m Jacky, a <bold>AI Software Engineer at NVIDIA</bold> working on VLA/LLMs for autonomous driving. I’m looking for 1-2 teammates for the RL final project.</paragraph><paragraph>While my day job involves large-scale models, for this course, I want to focus on a <bold>feasible yet high-impact research topic</bold> that can be completed within the quarter and potentially extended into a workshop or conference paper (e.g., CoRL, ICRA, or RLC).</paragraph><paragraph><bold>My Philosophy for this Project:</bold> Instead of brute-force training, let’s focus on <bold>algorithmic innovation</bold> where we can get results on a few GPUs. Possible directions:</paragraph><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>LLM-as-a-Teacher:</bold> Using LLMs to provide structured reward signals or "reasoning traces" to speed up RL training (Reward Design).</paragraph></list-item></list><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>Sample Efficient RL:</bold> Improving RL exploration using prior knowledge from pre-trained encoders (Representation Learning).</paragraph></list-item></list><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>RL for Fine-tuning:</bold> Applying RLHF-like techniques to smaller, specialized models for specific control tasks.</paragraph></list-item></list><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>Your Idea:</bold> I’m open to any "clever" idea in <bold>Robotics, AI4S, or Finance</bold> that solves a bottleneck in RL.</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><bold>What I bring:</bold></paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph>Professional engineering experience (NVIDIA) to ensure our codebase is solid.</paragraph></list-item></list><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph>Deep understanding of LLM/VLM capabilities and how to "distill" them into RL.</paragraph></list-item></list><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph>Strong interest in the research process and paper writing.</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><bold>Looking for:</bold> Teammates who are proactive, have a solid understanding of RL basics, and want to do more than just a "re-implementation" project.</paragraph><paragraph>If you have a potential idea or want to brainstorm together, please reach out at jackyyin@stanford.edu or jackyyin@nvidia.com. Let's build something neat!</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi everyone,

I’m Jacky, a AI Software Engineer at NVIDIA working on VLA/LLMs for autonomous driving. I’m looking for 1-2 teammates for the RL final project.

While my day job involves large-scale models, for this course, I want to focus on a feasible yet high-impact research topic that can be completed within the quarter and potentially extended into a workshop or conference paper (e.g., CoRL, ICRA, or RLC).

My Philosophy for this Project: Instead of brute-force training, let’s focus on algorithmic innovation where we can get results on a few GPUs. Possible directions:

LLM-as-a-Teacher: Using LLMs to provide structured reward signals or "reasoning traces" to speed up RL training (Reward Design).

Sample Efficient RL: Improving RL exploration using prior knowledge from pre-trained encoders (Representation Learning).

RL for Fine-tuning: Applying RLHF-like techniques to smaller, specialized models for specific control tasks.

Your Idea: I’m open to any "clever" idea in Robotics, AI4S, or Finance that solves a bottleneck in RL.

What I bring:

Professional engineering experience (NVIDIA) to ensure our codebase is solid.

Deep understanding of LLM/VLM capabilities and how to "distill" them into RL.

Strong interest in the research process and paper writing.

Looking for: Teammates who are proactive, have a solid understanding of RL basics, and want to do more than just a "re-implementation" project.

If you have a potential idea or want to brainstorm together, please reach out at jackyyin@stanford.edu or jackyyin@nvidia.com. Let's build something neat!

title: Bellman Backup Confirmation
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>As a followup to #80 I just wanted to confirm that the Bellman Backup is</paragraph><paragraph>$$BV(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S}p(s' \mid s, a) V(s')]$$</paragraph><paragraph>I see the function header for bellman_backup includes action, so does this mean our function should just return the value inside of $\max_a[...]$? As a semantic thing, would $R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S}p(s' \mid s, a) V(s')]$ then be the actual backup value and the bellman backup process is taking the max over a?</paragraph></document>
document:
As a followup to #80 I just wanted to confirm that the Bellman Backup is

$$BV(s) = \max_a [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S}p(s' \mid s, a) V(s')]$$

I see the function header for bellman_backup includes action, so does this mean our function should just return the value inside of $\max_a[...]$? As a semantic thing, would $R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'\in S}p(s' \mid s, a) V(s')]$ then be the actual backup value and the bellman backup process is taking the max over a?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You are right that the Bellman Backup is </paragraph><math>BV(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \mid s,a)\, V(s') \right].</math><paragraph>In the programming assignment, the "bellman_backup" function takes both a state and an action as input and outputs the backup value for that specific state, action pair, without taking the max. We designed the function in this way because it maximizes the reusability of this function in later calculations such as policy evaluation and policy improvement.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
You are right that the Bellman Backup is 

$$BV(s) \;=\; \max_{a} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} p(s' \mid s,a)\, V(s') \right].$$

In the programming assignment, the "bellman_backup" function takes both a state and an action as input and outputs the backup value for that specific state, action pair, without taking the max. We designed the function in this way because it maximizes the reusability of this function in later calculations such as policy evaluation and policy improvement.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Perfect, thanks!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Perfect, thanks!

title: assignment1 question
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In question 1 is it important to think we have to use both actions buy and sell or it can be use only one of action to find optimal policy? </paragraph></document>
document: In question 1 is it important to think we have to use both actions buy and sell or it can be use only one of action to find optimal policy?

answers.content:
The question stated:
...[is] it possible to a choose a value of H that results in theoptimal policy taking both buy and sell steps during its execution?
My understanding is that policy must include at least one buy action and one sell action to determine the value of H. Hopefully teaching team can clarify further if I'm incorrect about this.Sidenote, the grammar on this question made it a little confusing to read in the latex template.
answers.document:
The question stated:

...[is] it possible to a choose a value of H that results in the
optimal policy taking both buy and sell steps during its execution?

My understanding is that policy must include at least one buy action and one sell action to determine the value of H. Hopefully teaching team can clarify further if I'm incorrect about this.

title: HW1 1d
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph><italic>"Does there always exist a discount factor γ such that the optimal policy for the infinite-horizon MDP matches the optimal policy for the finite-horizon MDP for any H?"</italic></paragraph><paragraph>I want to make sure I'm understanding what this question is asking.</paragraph><paragraph>To reword, is it asking if:</paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>Option 1: For every discount factor G, we can find a finite undiscounted H-horizon MDP whose optimal policy matches the optimal policy of the G-discounted infinite-horizon MDP <bold>OR</bold></paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Option 2: For every finite natural number H, we can find a discount factor G such that the optimal policy for the G-discounted infinite-horizon MDP is the same as the optimal policy for the undiscounted finite H-horizon MDP <bold>OR</bold></paragraph></list-item></list><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>Option 3: Something else?</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>It's Option 2, right?</paragraph></document>
document:
"Does there always exist a discount factor γ such that the optimal policy for the infinite-horizon MDP matches the optimal policy for the finite-horizon MDP for any H?"

I want to make sure I'm understanding what this question is asking.

To reword, is it asking if:

Option 1: For every discount factor G, we can find a finite undiscounted H-horizon MDP whose optimal policy matches the optimal policy of the G-discounted infinite-horizon MDP OR

Option 2: For every finite natural number H, we can find a discount factor G such that the optimal policy for the G-discounted infinite-horizon MDP is the same as the optimal policy for the undiscounted finite H-horizon MDP OR

Option 3: Something else?

It's Option 2, right?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Yes. Option 2.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Yes. Option 2.

title: Question from Lecture 3
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Why every visit Monte Carlo is a biased estimator? Could you please provide some mathematical details? As per my understanding, it's expected value should be equal to V which makes it unbiased.</paragraph></document>
document: Why every visit Monte Carlo is a biased estimator? Could you please provide some mathematical details? As per my understanding, it's expected value should be equal to V which makes it unbiased.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question, I had to look it as well to find a good reference, but intuitively I think this is because the returns are correlated with the number of visits. I believe the paper "Reinforcement Learning with Replacing Eligibility Traces" by Singh and Barto (with Matt kindly pointed me to) has a counterexample and explanation. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: Great question, I had to look it as well to find a good reference, but intuitively I think this is because the returns are correlated with the number of visits. I believe the paper "Reinforcement Learning with Replacing Eligibility Traces" by Singh and Barto (with Matt kindly pointed me to) has a counterexample and explanation.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>As a more concrete example, consider a two state MDP with two states $s$ and $t$, where $t$ is terminal, and you loop from $s$ back to $s$ with probability $p$ of receiving +1 reward, and you transition from $s$ to $t$ with probability  $1-p$, receiving 0 and ending the episode. </paragraph><paragraph>Note that $V(s)$ is a geometric series with expected value $p/(1-p)$. <break/><break/>Now, consider the number of episodes to be $m = 1$. Suppose the episode visits $s$ exactly $N$ times before terminating. Then, the every-visit MC estimate is $1/N \sum_{i=1}^N i-1 = (N-1)/2$ (since every time-step collects a reward of 1, except for the last time-step.) The expected value of N is $1/(1-p)$, meaning that the expected value of every-visit MC is $p/2(1-p) ≠ V(s)$. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
As a more concrete example, consider a two state MDP with two states $s$ and $t$, where $t$ is terminal, and you loop from $s$ back to $s$ with probability $p$ of receiving +1 reward, and you transition from $s$ to $t$ with probability  $1-p$, receiving 0 and ending the episode. 

Note that $V(s)$ is a geometric series with expected value $p/(1-p)$. 

Now, consider the number of episodes to be $m = 1$. Suppose the episode visits $s$ exactly $N$ times before terminating. Then, the every-visit MC estimate is $1/N \sum_{i=1}^N i-1 = (N-1)/2$ (since every time-step collects a reward of 1, except for the last time-step.) The expected value of N is $1/(1-p)$, meaning that the expected value of every-visit MC is $p/2(1-p) ≠ V(s)$.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>If R(s,a) = +1, shouldn't the V(s) be 1 / (1 - p)? And the every-visit MC estimate be N(N + 1) / 2 as if there are N visits to state s, then we have already calculated a reward of  +N? But otherwise this makes sense. Thanks for sharing this example. I would appreciate if you could share a more generalized proof as well.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: If R(s,a) = +1, shouldn't the V(s) be 1 / (1 - p)? And the every-visit MC estimate be N(N + 1) / 2 as if there are N visits to state s, then we have already calculated a reward of  +N? But otherwise this makes sense. Thanks for sharing this example. I would appreciate if you could share a more generalized proof as well.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just to clarify your second point: what do you mean by a more generalized proof? This example is meant as a counterexample, which already suffices to show that the every-visit MC estimator can be biased (and thus is a complete proof of the claim). Please let me know if I’m misunderstanding what you’re asking for.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Just to clarify your second point: what do you mean by a more generalized proof? This example is meant as a counterexample, which already suffices to show that the every-visit MC estimator can be biased (and thus is a complete proof of the claim). Please let me know if I’m misunderstanding what you’re asking for.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>What I was wondering about is whether there exists a more general, formula-based derivation using the standard definitions of the estimator (e.g., expectations over returns and visit counts) that directly shows the bias analytically.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: What I was wondering about is whether there exists a more general, formula-based derivation using the standard definitions of the estimator (e.g., expectations over returns and visit counts) that directly shows the bias analytically.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I see, thanks for clarifying. <break/><break/>I’m not aware of a general closed-form, formula-based derivation of the bias that applies across all MDPs, though if I wanted to fine one or derive one myself I would probably start with the Singh and Barto paper above or the textbook by Sutton and Barto.</paragraph><paragraph>The every-visit MC estimator can be written as a ratio of random quantities (a sum of returns divided by a random number of visits), and I think bias should depend on the joint distribution of these terms, which I believe depends on the specific MDP dynamics. That said, one would need to check all of this more carefully, and these are just my initial thoughts.  </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
I see, thanks for clarifying. 

I’m not aware of a general closed-form, formula-based derivation of the bias that applies across all MDPs, though if I wanted to fine one or derive one myself I would probably start with the Singh and Barto paper above or the textbook by Sutton and Barto.

The every-visit MC estimator can be written as a ratio of random quantities (a sum of returns divided by a random number of visits), and I think bias should depend on the joint distribution of these terms, which I believe depends on the specific MDP dynamics. That said, one would need to check all of this more carefully, and these are just my initial thoughts.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks!!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks!!

title: Tutorials: Additional Information
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>As a friendly reminder, tutorials start tomorrow! Everyone who signed up by the end of Friday should have received a date/time/CA/location via email. All on campus students who signed up late — hang tight, we are working to schedule things.</paragraph><paragraph>See below for additional reminder/background on why we think tutorials are important.</paragraph><paragraph>Each tutorial will consist of both already covered and new material. This week the already covered material will be on the Bellman contraction proof from week 1 lecture 2. Your tutorial lead will ask students to help rederive this. In the second part of the tutorial, you will cover new material. </paragraph><paragraph>0/4: no attendance </paragraph><paragraph>1/4: not prepared to discuss material already covered / not participating </paragraph><paragraph>4/4: prepared to discuss covered material, actively try new material. Note: you do not have to get this right </paragraph><paragraph>In rare cases, we will mark a bonus if a student does very well.</paragraph><paragraph>We expect all students can get 4/4 if they attempt the material and are familiar with the covered material. You do not have to get the answers right to get full credit.</paragraph><paragraph>Your top 6 of 8 tutorials will count for your tutorial grade.</paragraph><paragraph>— </paragraph><paragraph>Background on Why We Are Running Tutorials for CS234</paragraph><paragraph>As you all know, large language models are now very good at solving standard homework problems.</paragraph><paragraph>But the goal of learning was never to simply get problems correct. You’ll work in small groups with a staff facilitator to explain ideas, reason through problems, and wrestle with the material together.</paragraph><paragraph>One gap we’ve consistently seen is that students get very little practice articulating technical ideas—yet this is exactly what you’ll be expected to do in research, interviews, and industry. No laptops, phones or prior notes will be allowed, and problems will be worked out on a whiteboard.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
As a friendly reminder, tutorials start tomorrow! Everyone who signed up by the end of Friday should have received a date/time/CA/location via email. All on campus students who signed up late — hang tight, we are working to schedule things.

See below for additional reminder/background on why we think tutorials are important.

Each tutorial will consist of both already covered and new material. This week the already covered material will be on the Bellman contraction proof from week 1 lecture 2. Your tutorial lead will ask students to help rederive this. In the second part of the tutorial, you will cover new material. 

0/4: no attendance 

1/4: not prepared to discuss material already covered / not participating 

4/4: prepared to discuss covered material, actively try new material. Note: you do not have to get this right 

In rare cases, we will mark a bonus if a student does very well.

We expect all students can get 4/4 if they attempt the material and are familiar with the covered material. You do not have to get the answers right to get full credit.

Your top 6 of 8 tutorials will count for your tutorial grade.

— 

Background on Why We Are Running Tutorials for CS234

As you all know, large language models are now very good at solving standard homework problems.

But the goal of learning was never to simply get problems correct. The real goal is to build ways of thinking—frameworks you can use to tackle new problems, explain your ideas to others, and advance a field.

That’s why, alongside homework, we’re introducing weekly tutorials this term.

Tutorials are small, structured, discussion-based sessions inspired by the Oxford and Cambridge system. You’ll work in small groups with a staff facilitator to explain ideas, reason through problems, and wrestle with the material together.

One gap we’ve consistently seen is that students get very little practice articulating technical ideas—yet this is exactly what you’ll be expected to do in research, interviews, and industry. Tutorials are designed to directly address that gap.

These sessions will be mandatory for on-campus students. You’ll have a 30-minute slot that stays the same each week, and there will be 8 total tutorials. Content will be a mix of explaining (already submitted) homework problems or lecture material, and new problems. No laptops, phones or prior notes will be allowed, and problems will be worked out on a whiteboard.

title: Notational confusion between 3.(b) and 3.(c) assignment 1
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Question 3(b) talks about proving there is only one fixed point per policy B(pi) but 3(c) talks about proving that two different bellman backups for different value functions corresponding to the same policy have different values. Aren't these two things contradictory?</paragraph></document>
document: Question 3(b) talks about proving there is only one fixed point per policy B(pi) but 3(c) talks about proving that two different bellman backups for different value functions corresponding to the same policy have different values. Aren't these two things contradictory?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Essentially, 3c says: "If you start with two different guesses where one is 'higher' than the other, after one update, the first one will still be 'higher' (or equal) than the second".<break/><break/>3b says: "There is only one vector that satisfies $V = B^\pi V$". </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
Essentially, 3c says: "If you start with two different guesses where one is 'higher' than the other, after one update, the first one will still be 'higher' (or equal) than the second".

3b says: "There is only one vector that satisfies $V = B^\pi V$".

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I guess you mean in part (b) that you want to do policy evaluation while in part(c) it's just one iteration of that policy evaluation?(using terminology from slides)</paragraph></document>
comments.document: I guess you mean in part (b) that you want to do policy evaluation while in part(c) it's just one iteration of that policy evaluation?(using terminology from slides)

title: Assignment 1 part 3f
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>What's the significance of calling it a greedy policy since it's possible to solve this question purely mechanically? What does the word greedy policy imply in context of this question?</paragraph></document>
document: What's the significance of calling it a greedy policy since it's possible to solve this question purely mechanically? What does the word greedy policy imply in context of this question?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Look at the definition of $B^*$. It uses a $\max$ to find the best action $a$. Now look at $B^\pi$. It is given an action by the policy $\pi$. If we want these two results to be identical, what must be true about the action $\pi$ chooses?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: Look at the definition of $B^*$. It uses a $\max$ to find the best action $a$. Now look at $B^\pi$. It is given an action by the policy $\pi$. If we want these two results to be identical, what must be true about the action $\pi$ chooses?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>It must be the action corresponding to max value, aka greedy.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: It must be the action corresponding to max value, aka greedy.

One more question on exam monitoring for CGOE student. Do I need to schedule two exam monitoring sessions: (1) mid-term and (2) in-class quiz?

comments.document: Yes. You need proctors for both.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>AFAIK, as long as the proctor sends the exam before the 24h mark you should be fine.<break/><break/>For example, if exam officially starts at a Wednesday at 3pm, your proctor has to send it before Thursday at 3pm. So if they give us, for example, 2 hours to finish it, you would have to start your exam at the latest at 12:00-ish on Thursday so you are done by 2:00pm and the proctor has at least 1 hour to scan the pages and email them back to Stanford. But you can start your 2 exam hours any time during that window. <break/>(Haven't checked how much time we actually have, in CS229 we had 3 hours)</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
AFAIK, as long as the proctor sends the exam before the 24h mark you should be fine.

For example, if exam officially starts at a Wednesday at 3pm, your proctor has to send it before Thursday at 3pm. So if they give us, for example, 2 hours to finish it, you would have to start your exam at the latest at 12:00-ish on Thursday so you are done by 2:00pm and the proctor has at least 1 hour to scan the pages and email them back to Stanford. But you can start your 2 exam hours any time during that window. 
(Haven't checked how much time we actually have, in CS229 we had 3 hours)

title: 1b Buying
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi,</paragraph><paragraph>Is the r(9, buy, 10) = 100 only for the end of the day? For example, if I finish restocking, sell 1, and buy 1, do I get 100 added to my return twice? And what happens if I buy when s=10?</paragraph><paragraph>Thank you.</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi,

Is the r(9, buy, 10) = 100 only for the end of the day? For example, if I finish restocking, sell 1, and buy 1, do I get 100 added to my return twice? And what happens if I buy when s=10?

Thank you.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think it says that the day ends when $s = 10$, meaning if your agent ever gets to 10, the day will end, and no other actions can be taken afterwards. As stated in the problem description, " $s=10$ is also a terminal state and the problem ends if it is reached."</paragraph></document>
answers.document: I think it says that the day ends when $s = 10$, meaning if your agent ever gets to 10, the day will end, and no other actions can be taken afterwards. As stated in the problem description, " $s=10$ is also a terminal state and the problem ends if it is reached."

title: Project Partner Search
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hello everyone,<break/>I’m looking for a partner who would be interested in pursuing a research project on robust autonomous driving through population-based reinforcement learning. The core idea is to train an autonomous vehicle against distributions of human driving models, rather than an “average” driver. Human behaviors would be modeled along dimensions such as aggressiveness, reaction time, risk tolerance, and rule compliance. </paragraph><paragraph>I'm a second-year Master's student in the Aero/Astro department, with an undergraduate degree in Automotive Engineering. I have taken a 'Decision making under uncertainty course', and while this will be my first formal reinforcement learning class, I am very excited to dive into the topic and work on a research-oriented project.</paragraph><paragraph>If this sounds interesting to you and you’d like to collaborate, please feel free to reach out to me via email at <link href="f1adeeye@stanford.edu">f1adeeye@stanford.edu</link></paragraph><paragraph>Thanks.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Hello everyone,
I’m looking for a partner who would be interested in pursuing a research project on robust autonomous driving through population-based reinforcement learning. The core idea is to train an autonomous vehicle against distributions of human driving models, rather than an “average” driver. Human behaviors would be modeled along dimensions such as aggressiveness, reaction time, risk tolerance, and rule compliance. 

I'm a second-year Master's student in the Aero/Astro department, with an undergraduate degree in Automotive Engineering. I have taken a 'Decision making under uncertainty course', and while this will be my first formal reinforcement learning class, I am very excited to dive into the topic and work on a research-oriented project.

If this sounds interesting to you and you’d like to collaborate, please feel free to reach out to me via email at f1adeeye@stanford.edu

Thanks.

comments.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>Resolved✅<break/>Thanks.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Resolved✅
Thanks.

Hi, I'm confused by what exactly question 4d is asking for. Is it asking us to do all of the following:

Find the largest discount factor under weak current and interpret why that makes sense

Find the largest discount factor under medium current and interpret why that makes sense

Find the largest discount factor under strong current and interpret why that makes sense

Describe and explain the changes in optimal values and discount factors you obtain both quantitatively and qualitatively

What does it mean to "describe and explain the changes in optimal values and discount factors you obtain both quantitatively and qualitatively"?

title: Questions from Lecture-4
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Why are we using neural networks to approximate the value functions when we can just calculate these values using experience? Do we only fit the values for some initial iterations and then use this trained model to approximate the value function until convergence?<break/><break/>Why is replay important, is it used just to handle data correlation and stability of the model? Is there something else that the replay is doing?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Why are we using neural networks to approximate the value functions when we can just calculate these values using experience? Do we only fit the values for some initial iterations and then use this trained model to approximate the value function until convergence?

Why is replay important, is it used just to handle data correlation and stability of the model? Is there something else that the replay is doing?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph><bold>Why are we using neural networks to approximate value functions?</bold></paragraph><paragraph>In tabular settings with small state spaces, it is totally possible to just calculate the values from experiences:</paragraph><paragraph>$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \big( (r_t + \text{max}_{a'}Q(s_{t+1}, a')) - Q(s_t, a_t) \big).$$</paragraph><paragraph>Here, you would build a table for $Q(s_t, a_t)$ and their values can be computed without any function approximation.</paragraph><paragraph>However, in real-world scenarios, the state space might be high-dimensional. For example, in a robot learning setup, a state space might include images, point clouds, robot joint poses, etc. In this scenario, it's not possible to visit every state in the state space and building a table for the values is not possible. Therefore, if we can assume that $Q(s, a)$ is a smooth function wrt. states and actions, then we can use neural networks to approximate $Q$ for a compact and generalizable representation that can learn from experiences that only cover limited parts of the state space.</paragraph><paragraph><bold>Do we only fit the values for some initial iterations?</bold></paragraph><paragraph>No. We update the neural networks iteratively until convergence.</paragraph><paragraph><bold>Why is replay important?</bold></paragraph><paragraph>You are right that replay can break the temporal correlation issue in the on-policy setting. In addition to handling data correlation, having a replay buffer also helps store and reuse transitions that are rare. Without replay, the policy might visit certain scenarios once or twice and won't learn from them much. But with replay, such transitions will be stored in the replay buffer and sampled repeatedly during the learning process.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Why are we using neural networks to approximate value functions?

In tabular settings with small state spaces, it is totally possible to just calculate the values from experiences:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \big( (r_t + \text{max}_{a'}Q(s_{t+1}, a')) - Q(s_t, a_t) \big).$$

Here, you would build a table for $Q(s_t, a_t)$ and their values can be computed without any function approximation.

However, in real-world scenarios, the state space might be high-dimensional. For example, in a robot learning setup, a state space might include images, point clouds, robot joint poses, etc. In this scenario, it's not possible to visit every state in the state space and building a table for the values is not possible. Therefore, if we can assume that $Q(s, a)$ is a smooth function wrt. states and actions, then we can use neural networks to approximate $Q$ for a compact and generalizable representation that can learn from experiences that only cover limited parts of the state space.

Do we only fit the values for some initial iterations?

No. We update the neural networks iteratively until convergence.

Why is replay important?

You are right that replay can break the temporal correlation issue in the on-policy setting. In addition to handling data correlation, having a replay buffer also helps store and reuse transitions that are rare. Without replay, the policy might visit certain scenarios once or twice and won't learn from them much. But with replay, such transitions will be stored in the replay buffer and sampled repeatedly during the learning process.

title: MDP + Policy
content: Hello Teaching Team, From the lecture slide i kind of understood that the MDP + Policy can we equated to an MRP. MRP as we know is something which gives Immediate Reward with some selected action probabilities. Intuitively it is still not clear on how we can deduce the MDP + Policy to be an Markov Reward Process. Can i get some help on this please?
document:
Hello Teaching Team, 

From the lecture slide i kind of understood that the MDP + Policy can we equated to an MRP. MRP as we know is something which gives Immediate Reward with some selected action probabilities. 

Intuitively it is still not clear on how we can deduce the MDP + Policy to be an Markov Reward Process. Can i get some help on this please?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>An MDP is composed of $(S, A, R, P, \gamma)$ where $S$ is the state space, $A$ is the action space, $R(s, a, s')$ is the reward function, $P(s'|s,a)$ is the transition dynamics, and $\gamma$ is a discount factor.</paragraph><paragraph>Given a policy $\pi(a|s)$, we can collapse the action part of the MDP into the environment dynamics to induce an MRP: we keep the same state space $S$ and discount factor $\gamma$, but transition dynamics and rewards that no longer depend on actions. Instead, they are given by expectations over actions selected according to the policy:</paragraph><paragraph>$$P_\pi(s' \mid s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ P(s' \mid s, a) \right], \quad R_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s),\; s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s,a)} \left[ R(s, a, s') \right].$$</paragraph><paragraph>Thus, once the policy is fixed, action selection becomes part of the environment dynamics, and the resulting process is a Markov reward process.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
An MDP is composed of $(S, A, R, P, \gamma)$ where $S$ is the state space, $A$ is the action space, $R(s, a, s')$ is the reward function, $P(s'|s,a)$ is the transition dynamics, and $\gamma$ is a discount factor.

Given a policy $\pi(a|s)$, we can collapse the action part of the MDP into the environment dynamics to induce an MRP: we keep the same state space $S$ and discount factor $\gamma$, but transition dynamics and rewards that no longer depend on actions. Instead, they are given by expectations over actions selected according to the policy:

$$P_\pi(s' \mid s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)} \left[ P(s' \mid s, a) \right], \quad R_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s),\; s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s,a)} \left[ R(s, a, s') \right].$$

Thus, once the policy is fixed, action selection becomes part of the environment dynamics, and the resulting process is a Markov reward process.

title: Lecture -2 : Poll2
content: I was able to get the #deterministic policies part but am not sure on how there may be two policies with the same value function. Is this because we have two possible actions left or right?
document: I was able to get the #deterministic policies part but am not sure on how there may be two policies with the same value function. Is this because we have two possible actions left or right?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In general, I think its because multiple policies can achieve the same maximal value function by choosing different, but equally rewarding/beneficial actions. </paragraph><paragraph>Contrast this to a unique optimal value function: Even if there are many different ways to achieve it, the best score you can get remains the same and does not change. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
In general, I think its because multiple policies can achieve the same maximal value function by choosing different, but equally rewarding/beneficial actions. 

Contrast this to a unique optimal value function: Even if there are many different ways to achieve it, the best score you can get remains the same and does not change.

Note that tutorials start on week 2.

document:

title: OAE and Accommodations Announcement
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi everyone!</paragraph><paragraph>This post contains some important information for anyone who has or believes they will need special accommodations. </paragraph><paragraph>We are happy to support anyone who has special accommodations from OAE for the course. To help us do so most effectively we ask that if you have an OAE accommodation letter and have not done so already, please send us your OAE letter as soon as possible, so we can be sure to accommodate your needs.</paragraph><paragraph>If you require midterm or quiz-related accommodations: by end of day <underline>Wednesday January 28</underline>, please send us an email describing what accommodations you expect to need for the midterm and quiz. Please do this even if you have already sent us an OAE letter separately :) </paragraph><paragraph>Thanks,<break/>Ishani</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi everyone!

This post contains some important information for anyone who has or believes they will need special accommodations. 

We are happy to support anyone who has special accommodations from OAE for the course. To help us do so most effectively we ask that if you have an OAE accommodation letter and have not done so already, please send us your OAE letter as soon as possible, so we can be sure to accommodate your needs.

If you require midterm or quiz-related accommodations: by end of day Wednesday January 28, please send us an email describing what accommodations you expect to need for the midterm and quiz. Please do this even if you have already sent us an OAE letter separately :) 

Thanks,
Ishani

title: Homework 2 Posted!
content:
Assignment 2 has been released! It is linked below and is updated on the website. It is due on Sunday, Feb 1, at 6 pm PT. This homework covers Q-learning with function approximation (DQN), policy gradient methods including PPO, and distribution shift across policies. The material will be covered in lectures during weeks 3 and 4; therefore, the deadline is Sunday, February 1 at 6:00 pm.Submission Instructions (Please follow these exactly)Please typeset the written portion of your homework using the LaTeX template we have provided, and do not modify the template itself. If you are new to LaTex, here is an Overleaf tutorial so that you can more easily edit LaTeX files online. In particular, the template contains \texttt{tcolorbox} environments. You should enter your answers only in the designated sections (e.g., within the provided boxes).
 \begin{tcolorbox}[breakable]

 %%%%% Start of 1(a) %%%%%

Put your answer to 1(a) here.

 %%%%% End of 1(a) %%%%%

 \end{tcolorbox}
Please feel free to post LaTeX questions on Ed!You will submit the homework to Gradescope using your sunid Stanford account in three separate parts:Assignment 2 Written (PDF file): submit a pdf version of your LaTex file Assignment 2 Written (.zip file): submit a .zip file, following the structure and format listed in the instructions (see q2d). Assignment 2 Coding (.zip file): submit your coding solutions here. There is a Makefile provided that will help you submit this portion. Please run make clean followed by make submit in the terminal and submit the resulting zip file on Gradescope.Package Installation / Debugging TipsIf PyBullet is not working for you (for some Mac users), try running the following:
conda create -n cs234_a2 python=3.9 pip=23.0
conda activate cs234_a2
conda install -c conda-forge pybullet -y
python -m pip install -U gymnasium pybullet-envs-gymnasium
pip install -r requirements.txt  // Comment out pybullet
document:
Assignment 2 has been released! It is linked below and is updated on the website. It is due on Sunday, Feb 1, at 6 pm PT. 

This homework covers Q-learning with function approximation (DQN), policy gradient methods including PPO, and distribution shift across policies. The material will be covered in lectures during weeks 3 and 4; therefore, the deadline is Sunday, February 1 at 6:00 pm.


Submission Instructions (Please follow these exactly)


Please typeset the written portion of your homework using the LaTeX template we have provided, and do not modify the template itself. If you are new to LaTex, here is an Overleaf tutorial so that you can more easily edit LaTeX files online. 

In particular, the template contains \texttt{tcolorbox} environments. You should enter your answers only in the designated sections (e.g., within the provided boxes).

 \begin{tcolorbox}[breakable]

 %%%%% Start of 1(a) %%%%%

Put your answer to 1(a) here.

 %%%%% End of 1(a) %%%%%

 \end{tcolorbox}


Please feel free to post LaTeX questions on Ed!

You will submit the homework to Gradescope using your sunid Stanford account in three separate parts:

Assignment 2 Written (PDF file): submit a pdf version of your LaTex file 

Assignment 2 Written (.zip file): submit a .zip file, following the structure and format listed in the instructions (see q2d). 

Assignment 2 Coding (.zip file): submit your coding solutions here. There is a Makefile provided that will help you submit this portion. Please run make clean followed by make submit in the terminal and submit the resulting zip file on Gradescope.

Package Installation / Debugging Tips
If PyBullet is not working for you (for some Mac users), try running the following:

conda create -n cs234_a2 python=3.9 pip=23.0
conda activate cs234_a2
conda install -c conda-forge pybullet -y
python -m pip install -U gymnasium pybullet-envs-gymnasium
pip install -r requirements.txt  // Comment out pybullet

title: Project Team Search Thread
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hello!</paragraph><paragraph>Project information can be found on the <link href="https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs234/project.html">website</link>. This thread is for students who are looking to form a project group (1–3 members). <italic>While groups of any size within this range are allowed, we strongly encourage forming groups of <bold>three</bold> whenever possible.</italic></paragraph><paragraph>If you are searching for a group or additional partners, please leave a comment below. To help others connect with you more easily, include the following information in your post:</paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>Name and Background:</bold> Share your name and any relevant details, such as your <bold>degree program (e.g., PhD/MS)</bold> and <bold>field of study</bold>.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Availability:</bold> Specify the <bold>times or days</bold> you are available to work together.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Team Size:</bold> Indicate your <bold>preferred team size</bold>.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Project Interests/Proposed Ideas.</bold></paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Contact Information:</bold> Provide the <bold>best way for others to reach you</bold>.</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>Additional logistics will be shared later in the quarter, so be sure to stay updated. </paragraph><paragraph>Once you've successfully formed a group, <bold><italic>please mark your comment as resolved so others know you're no longer available</italic></bold>. Thanks! </paragraph></document>
document:
Hello!

Project information can be found on the website. This thread is for students who are looking to form a project group (1–3 members). While groups of any size within this range are allowed, we strongly encourage forming groups of three whenever possible.

If you are searching for a group or additional partners, please leave a comment below. To help others connect with you more easily, include the following information in your post:

Name and Background: Share your name and any relevant details, such as your degree program (e.g., PhD/MS) and field of study.

Availability: Specify the times or days you are available to work together.

Team Size: Indicate your preferred team size.

Project Interests/Proposed Ideas.

Contact Information: Provide the best way for others to reach you.

Additional logistics will be shared later in the quarter, so be sure to stay updated. 

Once you've successfully formed a group, please mark your comment as resolved so others know you're no longer available. Thanks!

Hi, I'm Joey! I'm an coterm / MS in Computer Science (AI track).

Availability: Weekdays (Tuesday–Friday) mornings and afternoons, preferably before 5pm. Can also work Saturdays if needed.

[Edit]
Team Status: We currently have a team of 2 and are looking for one more person to join us!

Project Focus: I am interested in developing Cybersecurity RL Environments that frame security tasks—such as vulnerability exploitation and patching—as POMDP-style reinforcement learning problems.

The core goal for this class would be to build an environment that generates high-quality trajectories for training autonomous cybersecurity agents. I’m particularly excited about exploring adversarial self-play: setting up an "exploit agent" and a "patch agent" to compete against one another. This approach creates a natural curriculum and allows us to generate diverse training data without the need for manual annotation.

Open To: While this is the primary direction, I’m also open to discussing related ideas in agentic security. If you're interested in RL, security, or building complex environments, let's chat!

Contact info: junyiji3@stanford.edu

Hi everyone! My name is Maxim Gorshkov, me and my project partner Ruslan Sharifullin are looking for a third team member to work on the following project idea:

Title: Safe Offline RL with Learned Preferences for Adaptive Medical RAG

Summary: This project develops an intelligent retrieval system for medical question answering that learns when to retrieve documents, what to retrieve, and when to stop, optimizing both answer quality and retrieval efficiency. Unlike traditional RAG systems with fixed retrieval strategies, we formulate retrieval as a sequential decision problem and use reinforcement learning to learn optimal policies. The reward function is learned from physician preferences rather than hand-coded, enabling the system to align with clinical judgment. We use offline RL to ensure safe learning from logged data without risky online experimentation in a medical context.

Background:

title: assignment 1 q1a
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In this question is a state equivalent to the current number of stocks, meaning each stock number maps to only one action? Or is the state (stock_number, time_step) meaning that a stock number can map to different actions in different time steps?</paragraph><paragraph>For example, if I am in s=3 and I choose to sell, I will then be in s=2. If I then buy and end up back in s=3, can I now buy again if I am using the same policy even though the last time I was in s=3 I sold?</paragraph></document>
document:
In this question is a state equivalent to the current number of stocks, meaning each stock number maps to only one action? Or is the state (stock_number, time_step) meaning that a stock number can map to different actions in different time steps?

For example, if I am in s=3 and I choose to sell, I will then be in s=2. If I then buy and end up back in s=3, can I now buy again if I am using the same policy even though the last time I was in s=3 I sold?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question: for finite-horizon, the state is technically both your stock and time step, which is what allows you to have differerent actions for the same stock number (as it depends on time step).</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Great question: for finite-horizon, the state is technically both your stock and time step, which is what allows you to have differerent actions for the same stock number (as it depends on time step).

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Got it, so just to clarify when they introduce the infinite horizon MDP the state is just the stock level and there we have to choose the same action every time we are in say s=3.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Got it, so just to clarify when they introduce the infinite horizon MDP the state is just the stock level and there we have to choose the same action every time we are in say s=3.

title: Conditions for monotonic improvement
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I'm hoping to get a clearer understanding of the conditions for policy iteration's monotonic improvement. </paragraph><paragraph>In the original algorithm, we begin by initializing pi_0 randomly and getting its corresponding V values given the MDP. What if we start instead with an arbitrary series of V values, and then proceed the exact same way afterwards? Are we still guaranteed monotonic improvement?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
I'm hoping to get a clearer understanding of the conditions for policy iteration's monotonic improvement. 

In the original algorithm, we begin by initializing pi_0 randomly and getting its corresponding V values given the MDP. What if we start instead with an arbitrary series of V values, and then proceed the exact same way afterwards? Are we still guaranteed monotonic improvement?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think you are suggesting that what if instead of starting from a random $\pi_{0}$, we start from a random $V_{0}$, thereby eliminating the step of  getting $V_{\pi_{0}}$ from the random $\pi_{0}$. <break/><break/>Then we can just get the greedy $\pi_{1}$ from $V_{0}$ and perform policy iteration from this. (getting $V_{\pi_1}$ and then performing greedy step to get $\pi_{1}$ and so on)<break/><break/>We can prove that the successive improvements in policies would be monotonic. That is, $V_{\pi_2} \geq V_{\pi_1} \forall s \in S$, but I am not sure about $V_{\pi_1} \geq V_{0}$. Let me get back to you. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
I think you are suggesting that what if instead of starting from a random $\pi_{0}$, we start from a random $V_{0}$, thereby eliminating the step of  getting $V_{\pi_{0}}$ from the random $\pi_{0}$. 

Then we can just get the greedy $\pi_{1}$ from $V_{0}$ and perform policy iteration from this. (getting $V_{\pi_1}$ and then performing greedy step to get $\pi_{1}$ and so on)

We can prove that the successive improvements in policies would be monotonic. That is, $V_{\pi_2} \geq V_{\pi_1} \forall s \in S$, but I am not sure about $V_{\pi_1} \geq V_{0}$. Let me get back to you.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>By contradiction we can show that $V_{\pi_1} \leq V_{0}$. </paragraph></document>
comments.document: By contradiction we can show that $V_{\pi_1} \leq V_{0}$.

title: HW1 Q3e
content: My first question: Is it true that if we have an arbitrary V, B^{\pi}V\left(s\right)\ \le BV\left(s\right)\ \ \ \forall s?This seems true to me, based on how B^pi and B are defined, but I want to make sure I'm not overlooking some nuance or edge case. If it's true, can I use this fact without justification in 3e?Follow-up question: If the above is true, then does it necessarily lead to the following being true for arbitrary V? If so, can I use this without justification in 3e?V^{\pi}\left(s\right)\le V\left(s\right)\ \forall sThird question:Refers to the screenshot below. I understand the highlighted line in isolation. However, if we use a Bellman Operator on an "arbitrary value function" V, aren't we necessarily assuming that this V is aligned with some MDP? Otherwise, we wouldn't have a r(s, a) or p(s'|s, a) that w could use in calculating BV, right?
document:
My first question: Is it true that if we have an arbitrary V, 

$$B^{\pi}V\left(s\right)\ \le BV\left(s\right)\ \ \ \forall s?$$

This seems true to me, based on how B^pi and B are defined, but I want to make sure I'm not overlooking some nuance or edge case. If it's true, can I use this fact without justification in 3e?

Follow-up question: If the above is true, then does it necessarily lead to the following being true for arbitrary V? If so, can I use this without justification in 3e?

$$V^{\pi}\left(s\right)\le V\left(s\right)\ \forall s$$

Third question:

Refers to the screenshot below. I understand the highlighted line in isolation. However, if we use a Bellman Operator on an "arbitrary value function" V, aren't we necessarily assuming that this V is aligned with some MDP? Otherwise, we wouldn't have a r(s, a) or p(s'|s, a) that w could use in calculating BV, right?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>1. If you use this statement in your answer for 3e, you should provide a short justification. </paragraph><paragraph>2. Again, you should provide a justification for this statement if you make use of it. I would question whether this statement is true for an <italic>arbitrary</italic> V, since you can always find a V such that it is element wise less than V^π</paragraph><paragraph>3. What the highlighted text is saying that the values of V can be completely arbitrary and not necessarily achievable in the MDP. For instance, suppose that for a given MDP, max returns are bounded by 1, but V could be a vector of all +100. However, you are correct in that V must be defined over the same set of states as the reward and transition function.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
1. If you use this statement in your answer for 3e, you should provide a short justification. 

2. Again, you should provide a justification for this statement if you make use of it. I would question whether this statement is true for an arbitrary V, since you can always find a V such that it is element wise less than V^π

3. What the highlighted text is saying that the values of V can be completely arbitrary and not necessarily achievable in the MDP. For instance, suppose that for a given MDP, max returns are bounded by 1, but V could be a vector of all +100. However, you are correct in that V must be defined over the same set of states as the reward and transition function.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I have another question regarding the nuances - It seems, if my proof is correct, that the bound applies to any policy, not necessarily only to greedy.  What are we supposed to do with the information that the policy is greedy?</paragraph></document>
answers.document: I have another question regarding the nuances - It seems, if my proof is correct, that the bound applies to any policy, not necessarily only to greedy.  What are we supposed to do with the information that the policy is greedy?

title: Latex question
content: Is Latex available for the slides?If not, what are the latex commands I would need to get this nice hanging indentation? (Lec 2) I couldn't figure out how with \begin{align} or \begin{flalign} Also, how do you do in-line math symbols in Ed on the same line as normal text?

answers.content:
Using & in the right places is the key to getting the right indentation. Here's the code: 
\begin{align*}
    V^{\pi_i}(s) &\leq \max_a Q^{\pi_i}(s, a) \\
    &= \max_a R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, a) V^{\pi_i}(s') \\
    &= R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) V^{\pi_i}(s') \quad \text{// by the definition of } \pi_{i+1} \\
    &\leq R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) \left( \max_{a'} Q^{\pi_i}(s', a') \right) \\
    &= R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) \\
    &\quad \left( R(s', \pi_{i+1}(s')) + \gamma \sum_{s'' \in S} P(s'' | s', \pi_{i+1}(s')) V^{\pi_i}(s'') \right) \\
    &\vdots \\
    &= V^{\pi_{i+1}}(s)
\end{align*}
I haven't checked the accuracy of the equations, but it should be good enough to get an idea of how to format things correctly.

    V^{\pi_i}(s) &\leq \max_a Q^{\pi_i}(s, a) \\
    &= \max_a R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, a) V^{\pi_i}(s') \\
    &= R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) V^{\pi_i}(s') \quad \text{// by the definition of } \pi_{i+1} \\
    &\leq R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) \left( \max_{a'} Q^{\pi_i}(s', a') \right) \\
    &= R(s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' | s, \pi_{i+1}(s)) \\
    &\quad \left( R(s', \pi_{i+1}(s')) + \gamma \sum_{s'' \in S} P(s'' | s', \pi_{i+1}(s')) V^{\pi_i}(s'') \right) \\
    &\vdots \\
    &= V^{\pi_{i+1}}(s)
\end{align*}


I haven't checked the accuracy of the equations, but it should be good enough to get an idea of how to format things correctly.

title: HW 1, 3e
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I'm trying to understand why 3e is framed as two separate statements - if we are proving the first statement holds for any policy, then do we actually need a separate proof for the optimal policy?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: I'm trying to understand why 3e is framed as two separate statements - if we are proving the first statement holds for any policy, then do we actually need a separate proof for the optimal policy?

answers.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>They are framed as two separate statements because both statements are used in follow up questions. </paragraph><paragraph>You make a great observation, though I will note that the RHS of the first statement uses B^pi while the second uses B, so the second does not follow immediately from the first</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
They are framed as two separate statements because both statements are used in follow up questions. 

You make a great observation, though I will note that the RHS of the first statement uses B^pi while the second uses B, so the second does not follow immediately from the first

title: Lecture 2 question for PI
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Since learning the optimal deterministic policy is much simpler than stochastic policy, can we first assume the actions are deterministic to train the policy optimization, then relax the assumption that the actions are stochastic, and compute the optimal stochastic policy faster?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: Since learning the optimal deterministic policy is much simpler than stochastic policy, can we first assume the actions are deterministic to train the policy optimization, then relax the assumption that the actions are stochastic, and compute the optimal stochastic policy faster?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Note that every deterministic policy is actually also stochastic (it just places all probability on a single action.) Thus, in a strong sense, computing an optimal deterministic policy <italic>is not</italic> easier than computing an optimal stochastic policy because once you have an optimal deterministic policy, you already have an optimal stochastic policy. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: Note that every deterministic policy is actually also stochastic (it just places all probability on a single action.) Thus, in a strong sense, computing an optimal deterministic policy is not easier than computing an optimal stochastic policy because once you have an optimal deterministic policy, you already have an optimal stochastic policy.

title: "Greedy policy"
content: My intuition from previous classes was that a "greedy policy" is whatever maximizes the immediate reward without looking at future rewards. If this is true, then how do I square it with the equation here (from HW1 Q3), which looks like it considers discounted future steps, not just r(s, a)?What makes this "greedy" and what is an example of a situation where this would not be optimal?
document:
My intuition from previous classes was that a "greedy policy" is whatever maximizes the immediate reward without looking at future rewards. If this is true, then how do I square it with the equation here (from HW1 Q3), which looks like it considers discounted future steps, not just r(s, a)?

What makes this "greedy" and what is an example of a situation where this would not be optimal?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Not quite. The greedy policy with respect to a value function $V$ is the one that maximizes the immediate reward <italic>plus</italic> the expected future reward, assuming that $V$ is correct (this is a type of bootstrapping.) This may be suboptimal, for example, if the estimate $V$ is grossly incorrect. Does this help? </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: Not quite. The greedy policy with respect to a value function $V$ is the one that maximizes the immediate reward plus the expected future reward, assuming that $V$ is correct (this is a type of bootstrapping.) This may be suboptimal, for example, if the estimate $V$ is grossly incorrect. Does this help?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>So as long as our estimate V is correct, our greedy policy will be optimal?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: So as long as our estimate V is correct, our greedy policy will be optimal?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>yes, it's a good idea to convince yourself of this</paragraph></document>

title: How is MC more data efficient than TD?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This was mentioned in lecture briefly, but I don't understand why.</paragraph></document>
document: This was mentioned in lecture briefly, but I don't understand why.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I believe the specific kind of data inefficiency Prof. Brunskill was talking about can likely be explained with the following example:</paragraph><paragraph>Suppose in your MDP, you need to travel through a chain of ~100 distinct states, receiving 0 reward, and only receiving reward of +1 when you reach an end goal state.<break/><break/>Consider what happens in MC if we take in one episode. All state value estimates would be updated based on the final return of 1. You can see, it only takes one episode for the value to propagate along the whole chain of states.<break/><break/>Now consider what happens in TD(0). After going through one episode in order, we would have propagated the value just one state back. After another episode, it would be propagated two states back. Essentially, for any state $k$ steps away from another state, if updating forward in this way, it could take $k$ episodes before we incorporate any information about reward or value from that other state. So, in this small example above, it could take 100 episodes to make the same update to the value function compared to MC.<break/><break/>If you're interested, incorporating "eligibility traces" aims to address this specific inefficiency.<break/></paragraph></document>
answers.document:
I believe the specific kind of data inefficiency Prof. Brunskill was talking about can likely be explained with the following example:

Suppose in your MDP, you need to travel through a chain of ~100 distinct states, receiving 0 reward, and only receiving reward of +1 when you reach an end goal state.

Consider what happens in MC if we take in one episode. All state value estimates would be updated based on the final return of 1. You can see, it only takes one episode for the value to propagate along the whole chain of states.

Now consider what happens in TD(0). After going through one episode in order, we would have propagated the value just one state back. After another episode, it would be propagated two states back. Essentially, for any state $k$ steps away from another state, if updating forward in this way, it could take $k$ episodes before we incorporate any information about reward or value from that other state. So, in this small example above, it could take 100 episodes to make the same update to the value function compared to MC.

If you're interested, incorporating "eligibility traces" aims to address this specific inefficiency.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Is MC generally more data efficient than TD, or is it only in special cases like this with this specific inefficiency?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
comments.document: Is MC generally more data efficient than TD, or is it only in special cases like this with this specific inefficiency?
