title: choice of alpha
content: In first-visit MC, updates are done with a step size of 1/n, which comes from computing the exact sample average and normalizes based on how often a state is visited. In incremental Monte Carlo, we replace 1/n with alpha. Since 1/n originally had a clear statistical meaning, why is it valid to replace it with an arbitrary alpha given that alpha is in these bounds (see below)?I understand that 1/n satisfies the properties below, but why would you want to choose an alpha other than 1/n?
document:
In first-visit MC, updates are done with a step size of 1/n, which comes from computing the exact sample average and normalizes based on how often a state is visited. In incremental Monte Carlo, we replace 1/n with alpha. Since 1/n originally had a clear statistical meaning, why is it valid to replace it with an arbitrary alpha given that alpha is in these bounds (see below)?
I understand that 1/n satisfies the properties below, but why would you want to choose an alpha other than 1/n?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You are right that setting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ has a clear statistical meaning. However, in a practical setting, setting $\alpha$ in this way is not always optimal. Consider an online RL setting, where the learned policy is iteratively updated based on the value function approximation. In the early stages of learning, the value function and policy are both suboptimal, and thus the policy has a high probability of visiting bad states (eg. various failures). By setting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$, earlier bad samples are weighted highly and can lead to poor exploration, where the policy has trouble finding alternative strategies that lead to better states. Alternative ways to set $\alpha$ (eg. making $\alpha$ a constant) can help our approximated $V$ track the latest $V^\pi$ better and thus help improve stability in the learning process.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: You are right that setting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$ has a clear statistical meaning. However, in a practical setting, setting $\alpha$ in this way is not always optimal. Consider an online RL setting, where the learned policy is iteratively updated based on the value function approximation. In the early stages of learning, the value function and policy are both suboptimal, and thus the policy has a high probability of visiting bad states (eg. various failures). By setting $\alpha_n = \frac{1}{n}$, earlier bad samples are weighted highly and can lead to poor exploration, where the policy has trouble finding alternative strategies that lead to better states. Alternative ways to set $\alpha$ (eg. making $\alpha$ a constant) can help our approximated $V$ track the latest $V^\pi$ better and thus help improve stability in the learning process.

title: lecture 3: Is incremental Monte-Carlo policy evaluation first-visit or every-visit?
content: For the Monte-Carlo policy evaluator, does incremental Monte-Carlo update the states for first visits or every visit of a state in an episode? Or can it be both? In the lecture notes, it seems like it is every-visit but I'm not sure
document:
For the Monte-Carlo policy evaluator, does incremental Monte-Carlo update the states for first visits or every visit of a state in an episode? Or can it be both? 

In the lecture notes, it seems like it is every-visit but I'm not sure

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Incremental MC can be either first-visit or every visit. This is because the "incremental" term refers to how the update is applied, while "first-visit" or "every-visit" specifies which state visits should be counted. When referring to a policy evaluation process, you can combine two terms, like "incremental every-visit MC" on slide 32 of lecture 3.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Incremental MC can be either first-visit or every visit. This is because the "incremental" term refers to how the update is applied, while "first-visit" or "every-visit" specifies which state visits should be counted. When referring to a policy evaluation process, you can combine two terms, like "incremental every-visit MC" on slide 32 of lecture 3.

title: Lecture 3 optional check understanding
content: Why is Question 3 true? (Is there also a proper definition of "stationary")
document: Why is Question 3 true? (Is there also a proper definition of "stationary")

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>First-visit MC, for each state $s$, only considers the first time $s$ is visited in an episode to compute value estimates. The scenario described in "incremental MC with $\alpha = 1$", however, is equivalent to using the last visit to $s$ in an episode for computing $V^\pi$.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: First-visit MC, for each state $s$, only considers the first time $s$ is visited in an episode to compute value estimates. The scenario described in "incremental MC with $\alpha = 1$", however, is equivalent to using the last visit to $s$ in an episode for computing $V^\pi$.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for the response, but my confusion is about Question 3 "why incremental MC with </paragraph><math>\alpha\ >\ \frac{1}{N\left(s_{it}\right)}</math><paragraph>could help in non-stationary domains" </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Thanks for the response, but my confusion is about Question 3 "why incremental MC with 

$$\alpha\ >\ \frac{1}{N\left(s_{it}\right)}$$

could help in non-stationary domains"

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In non-stationary domains, we assume that our environment and rewards can change over time.</paragraph><paragraph>This means that our value for being in a given state can also change over time (for example, a state that might be really good to visit in the past can change to be really bad in the future).<break/><break/>In this setting, don't want our value estimates to converge once and then have trouble updating to the new true values. We want our $\alpha$ to be big enough so that we have the ability to update our estimates to the changing true values over time.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
In non-stationary domains, we assume that our environment and rewards can change over time.

This means that our value for being in a given state can also change over time (for example, a state that might be really good to visit in the past can change to be really bad in the future).

In this setting, don't want our value estimates to converge once and then have trouble updating to the new true values. We want our $\alpha$ to be big enough so that we have the ability to update our estimates to the changing true values over time.

title: Tabular MDP vs MDP
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Can i know like what's the difference between the Tabular MDP and a regular MDP ?</paragraph><paragraph>We know that MDP is a MRP (Immediate Reward + no actions) + actions. But was wondering how this plays out in the case of Tabular MDP ?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Can i know like what's the difference between the Tabular MDP and a regular MDP ?

We know that MDP is a MRP (Immediate Reward + no actions) + actions. But was wondering how this plays out in the case of Tabular MDP ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>A tabular MDP is an MDP that can be represented in a tabular format (with finite states and actions that can be enumerated in a table).</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: A tabular MDP is an MDP that can be represented in a tabular format (with finite states and actions that can be enumerated in a table).

These quantities can't be represented in a tabular format.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>

title: L3N1: Value Iteration vs Policy Iteration
content: Hello,I was able to get the policy iteration part on why we have distinct policies for the case of Policy Iteration, but am not sure on how the Value Iteration will take more than one policy to reach V*. I know we assume the V_0(S) = 0 always, but am not sure on why the V_1(S) = R(S,a) and how the V* will take more iterations and the notation of the V^pi (S) = 1/1-gamma. Can i get some help on this please.
document:
Hello,

I was able to get the policy iteration part on why we have distinct policies for the case of Policy Iteration, but am not sure on how the Value Iteration will take more than one policy to reach V*. I know we assume the V_0(S) = 0 always, but am not sure on why the V_1(S) = R(S,a) and how the V* will take more iterations and the notation of the V^pi (S) = 1/1-gamma. Can i get some help on this please.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>1. Value iteration will take more than one policy to reach V*: when we do value iteration we are not explicitly choosing a policy. We can extract a greedy policy from a specific value function, but this greedy policy does not need to be used in value iteration. <break/>2. V_1(S) = R(S,a) follows from the Bellman backup, where if we initialize V_0(S) = 0 then the summation term is 0. <break/>3. V* will take more than |A|^|S| iterations because at every iteration we are increasing the horizon by 1. To get "close enough" to the optimal value function in the infinite horizon setting we may need to have the horizon k arbitrarily large (potentially much larger than |A|^|S|). </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
1. Value iteration will take more than one policy to reach V*: when we do value iteration we are not explicitly choosing a policy. We can extract a greedy policy from a specific value function, but this greedy policy does not need to be used in value iteration. 
2. V_1(S) = R(S,a) follows from the Bellman backup, where if we initialize V_0(S) = 0 then the summation term is 0. 
3. V* will take more than |A|^|S| iterations because at every iteration we are increasing the horizon by 1. To get "close enough" to the optimal value function in the infinite horizon setting we may need to have the horizon k arbitrarily large (potentially much larger than |A|^|S|).

Are there any problems, that we can solve to better solidify the concepts on how we apply the Policy Iteration and Value Iteration ?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Hello Teaching Team,

I find the notations and some times the concepts the bellman backup,  Q value function little confusing especially when we do the Policy Iteration. Are there any problems, that we can solve to better solidify the concepts on how we apply the Policy Iteration and Value Iteration ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In general, computing by hand policy iteration will be difficult because the policy evaluation step will require computing Bellman backup for a particular policy until convergence. As an example, you can try applying policy/value iteration to the Mars Rover example (lecture slide 46)</paragraph><paragraph/></document>

title: clarification about on/off policy
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>If both SARSA and Q-learning collect data using an epsilon-greedy policy derived from the current Q-values, and both update those Q-values and then update the policy accordingly, in what precise sense is SARSA on-policy and Q-learning off-policy?</paragraph><paragraph>If the policy is updated after every Q update, aren’t both algorithms always learning about the same policy they are executing?</paragraph><paragraph>Analogously, is value iteration off policy and policy iteration on policy? </paragraph></document>
document:
If both SARSA and Q-learning collect data using an epsilon-greedy policy derived from the current Q-values, and both update those Q-values and then update the policy accordingly, in what precise sense is SARSA on-policy and Q-learning off-policy?

If the policy is updated after every Q update, aren’t both algorithms always learning about the same policy they are executing?

Analogously, is value iteration off policy and policy iteration on policy?

answers.content:
<document version="2.0"><paragraph>For SARSA update, we have:</paragraph><math>Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t)
+ \alpha \bigl( r_t + \gamma Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) - Q(s_t, a_t) \bigr)</math><paragraph>and for Q-learning update we have:</paragraph><math>Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t)
+ \alpha \bigl( r_t + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \bigr)</math><paragraph>Notice that Q-learning uses </paragraph><math>\max_aQ(s_{t+1},a)</math><paragraph>which means we are evaluating an action 'a' that maximizes the Q value i.e. estimating Q*, and that action may not be part of the policy that the agent takes, it may be "off-policy".<break/><break/>On the other hand, with SARSA we use:</paragraph><math>Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})</math><paragraph>where a_{t+1}​ is the action actually taken by the agent under the current policy. Therefore, SARSA evaluates and improves the same policy that is used to generate data, making it on-policy.</paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph><link href="https://stanford-pilot.hosted.panopto.com/Panopto/Pages/Viewer.aspx?id=fe13eb05-5464-45bc-baf9-b3a70002123f">CS234 on 1/14/2026 (Wed)</link> (1:03:50 for Q-learning and 1:07:00 for SARSA)</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><link href="https://stanford-pilot.hosted.panopto.com/Panopto/Pages/Viewer.aspx?id=c2cf111c-5739-471c-b964-b3a70002126f">CS234 on 1/21/2026 (Wed)</link> (14:08 Recap of off-policy for Q-learning)</paragraph></list-item></list></document>
answers.document:
For SARSA update, we have:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t)
+ \alpha \bigl( r_t + \gamma Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) - Q(s_t, a_t) \bigr)$$

and for Q-learning update we have:

$$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t)
+ \alpha \bigl( r_t + \gamma \max_a Q(s_{t+1}, a) - Q(s_t, a_t) \bigr)$$

Notice that Q-learning uses 

$$\max_aQ(s_{t+1},a)$$

which means we are evaluating an action 'a' that maximizes the Q value i.e. estimating Q*, and that action may not be part of the policy that the agent takes, it may be "off-policy".

On the other hand, with SARSA we use:

$$Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1})$$

where a_{t+1}​ is the action actually taken by the agent under the current policy. Therefore, SARSA evaluates and improves the same policy that is used to generate data, making it on-policy.

CS234 on 1/14/2026 (Wed) (1:03:50 for Q-learning and 1:07:00 for SARSA)

CS234 on 1/21/2026 (Wed) (14:08 Recap of off-policy for Q-learning)

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Pranav provided a great answer regarding SARSA vs Q-Learning. The on-policy versus off-policy difference is primarily applied to RL (sampling) algorithms, such as SARSA and Q-Learning. It is not really applied to Dynamic Programming (planning) algorithms, such as Value Iteration and Policy Iteration, because you aren't really collecting data as you would in SARSA / Q-Learning; you are basically calculating everything without needing to do rollouts.</paragraph><paragraph>However, in some sense, Value Iteration does not use a policy for its Bellman backups (it takes max over actions similar to Q-Learning) while Policy Iteration does use a policy. So value iteration is kind of off-policy and policy iteration is kind of on-policy. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Pranav provided a great answer regarding SARSA vs Q-Learning. The on-policy versus off-policy difference is primarily applied to RL (sampling) algorithms, such as SARSA and Q-Learning. It is not really applied to Dynamic Programming (planning) algorithms, such as Value Iteration and Policy Iteration, because you aren't really collecting data as you would in SARSA / Q-Learning; you are basically calculating everything without needing to do rollouts.

However, in some sense, Value Iteration does not use a policy for its Bellman backups (it takes max over actions similar to Q-Learning) while Policy Iteration does use a policy. So value iteration is kind of off-policy and policy iteration is kind of on-policy.

title: Unable to create environment for Mac (M1)
content:
Hi, I use an Apple Silicon Mac (M1) and I'm unable to meet all the dependency requirements for assignment 2 after trying a couple of different things. I first followed the provided installation directions in the README, then tried the fix in #175 and #210, and finally removed all the version specifications in requirements.txt, but I'm still getting the following error:
note: This error originates from a subprocess, and is likely not a problem with pip. error: legacy-install-failure

× Encountered error while trying to install package. ╰─> pybullet

note: This is an issue with the package mentioned above, not pip. hint: See above for output from the failure.
I think this is because the package versions are very old. Is there any fix for this? Perhaps a more updated requirements.txt?
document:
Hi, I use an Apple Silicon Mac (M1) and I'm unable to meet all the dependency requirements for assignment 2 after trying a couple of different things. I first followed the provided installation directions in the README, then tried the fix in #175 and #210, and finally removed all the version specifications in requirements.txt, but I'm still getting the following error:

× Encountered error while trying to install package. ╰─> pybullet

note: This is an issue with the package mentioned above, not pip. hint: See above for output from the failure.


I think this is because the package versions are very old. Is there any fix for this? Perhaps a more updated requirements.txt?

answers.content:
My recommendation is to install the prebuilt Apple Silicon build from conda-forge:
conda create -n bullet python=3.11 -y
conda activate bullet
conda install -c conda-forge pybullet -y
or you can change the Xcode toolchain bc it's a build failure usually but honestly I don't recommend especially and it may take longer and doesn't always work

comments.document: Hmm, seems like pybullet is still not installed correctly

answers.content:
Here’s a flow that worked for me on M3 Max (Apple Silicon):
# 1) Create env (conda-forge only)
conda create -n cs234_a2 python=3.9 pip=23.0 -y -c conda-forge --override-channels

# 2) Activate
conda activate cs234_a2

# 3) Install pybullet + numpy from conda-forge (prebuilt ARM64)
conda install -c conda-forge pybullet "numpy=1.23" -y --override-channels

# 4) Pin build tools
pip install pip==23.0 setuptools==65.5.0 "wheel<0.40.0"

# 5) Install remaining packages
pip install torch gym==0.21 matplotlib scipy

# 6) Sanity check
python -c "import gym, pybullet_envs, torch; print('OK', torch.backends.mps.is_available())"
If conda ever hits the default Anaconda channel (repo.anaconda.com), run once:
conda config --remove channels defaults
conda config --set channel_priority strict
Note that there are some slight differences from the official README versioning. For instance, I'm using numpy 1.23.5 since that's a patched version of 1.23.0. However, the bigger deal is with the pybullet package. It looks like pybullet 3.2.6 won't compile on ARM without workarounds. The default conda-forge version is 3.25. Is that okay to use?

title: Lecture 3: Monte Carlo vs Every Time Monte Carlo
content: In a given robot navigation i might enter a particular state only once during an episode. Here we don't have room to average out that particular state related return. But if we have to go via that a particular state 3 times our overall return will be something like (averaged return at state 3 + all the other state returns)/(Incremental counter of total first visits). Is my understanding correct ?
document: In a given robot navigation i might enter a particular state only once during an episode. Here we don't have room to average out that particular state related return. But if we have to go via that a particular state 3 times our overall return will be something like (averaged return at state 3 + all the other state returns)/(Incremental counter of total first visits). Is my understanding correct ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just to make sure I understand, what do you mean when you say "returns" in this case? For a given time-step $t$ in an episode, the return is the discounted sum of all remaining rewards in the episode.<break/><break/>So maybe a clearer way to say it would be <bold>(total returns across all episodes from the first times we see $s$) / (total first visits to $s$)</bold><break/><break/>For first-visit MC, if a state $s$ appears three times in the episode at $t_1 < t_2 < t_3$, then we update $V^\pi (s)$ in the following way:<break/><break/>We first add to our count of <italic>total</italic> return (across all episodes) from $s$, adding the return starting from $t_1$. Then we divide that total return by the number of times we have done a <italic>first visit</italic> to $s$. We then do nothing for the times we visit $s$ at $t_2$ and $t_3$.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Just to make sure I understand, what do you mean when you say "returns" in this case? For a given time-step $t$ in an episode, the return is the discounted sum of all remaining rewards in the episode.

So maybe a clearer way to say it would be (total returns across all episodes from the first times we see $s$) / (total first visits to $s$)

For first-visit MC, if a state $s$ appears three times in the episode at $t_1 < t_2 < t_3$, then we update $V^\pi (s)$ in the following way:

We first add to our count of total return (across all episodes) from $s$, adding the return starting from $t_1$. Then we divide that total return by the number of times we have done a first visit to $s$. We then do nothing for the times we visit $s$ at $t_2$ and $t_3$.

title: Does Monte Carlo converge in the value function approximation setting?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>We know that Q-Learning might not converge because of too many approximations/instability issues, but will Monte Carlo value function approximation (and control) also have the same problems? or will it converge? I am a bit confused</paragraph></document>
document: We know that Q-Learning might not converge because of too many approximations/instability issues, but will Monte Carlo value function approximation (and control) also have the same problems? or will it converge? I am a bit confused

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This is a good question. To my knowledge, in theory, there are no general convergence guarantees to the optimal policy when using arbitrary function approximation. I think this will depend on the model being used (i.e., expressivity) as well as the richness of the features in addition to properties of the algorithm. There's a rich literature on this, but I think it's generally outside the scope of class.</paragraph><paragraph>Practically speaking, note that Monte Carlo methods avoid some aspects of the deadly triad (bootstrapping and off-policy) so they might be more stable than q learning with function approximation in practice. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
This is a good question. To my knowledge, in theory, there are no general convergence guarantees to the optimal policy when using arbitrary function approximation. I think this will depend on the model being used (i.e., expressivity) as well as the richness of the features in addition to properties of the algorithm. There's a rich literature on this, but I think it's generally outside the scope of class.

Practically speaking, note that Monte Carlo methods avoid some aspects of the deadly triad (bootstrapping and off-policy) so they might be more stable than q learning with function approximation in practice.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks. Does this apply for the GLIE condition too (i.e., if the Monte-Carlo approximated policy is GLIE)</paragraph></document>

title: Lecture 4: Confusion about policies used in Q-learning with epsilon-greedy exploration
content: I am a bit confused about how pi_b is initialized. Based on this pseudocode, it seems like pi_b just stays the same for the whole Q-learning process, based on the initialized Q function. Is that understanding correct, or does pi_b update every time Q is updated? (follow-up question if pi_b is updated every iteration: if pi_b is updated every iteration, then what is the difference between pi_b and our learned policy pi, as they are both epsilon-greedy w.r.t. the same Q?)
document: I am a bit confused about how pi_b is initialized. Based on this pseudocode, it seems like pi_b just stays the same for the whole Q-learning process, based on the initialized Q function. Is that understanding correct, or does pi_b update every time Q is updated? (follow-up question if pi_b is updated every iteration: if pi_b is updated every iteration, then what is the difference between pi_b and our learned policy pi, as they are both epsilon-greedy w.r.t. the same Q?)

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>My understanding is that this pseudocode is demonstrating "synchronous Q-learning" (which is the standard version). I think $\pi_b$ is defined <italic>functionally</italic>, so at any given time, it is the $\epsilon$-greedy policy wrt $Q$. This means that it's basically updated every time $Q$ is updated in line 6. You're right that $\pi_b$ and $\pi$ are essentially equivalent here. </paragraph><paragraph>That said, there are variants of Q-learning which operate in the "asynchronous setting" where the policy used to collect data $\pi_b$ is different from the $\epsilon$-greedy policy with respect to $Q$. You can imagine this might be useful in settings where you need to run an entire episode at once with a fixed policy and cannot afford to update your policy in the middle of the episode (e.g., societal applications of RL to education or healthcare). In these cases, there are works which prove guarantees when $\pi_b$ is different from $\pi$. This might be why the pseudocode is written in generality, to emphasize that $\pi_b$ usually is the same as $\pi$ but technically needn't be. </paragraph><paragraph>Does this help? </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
My understanding is that this pseudocode is demonstrating "synchronous Q-learning" (which is the standard version). I think $\pi_b$ is defined functionally, so at any given time, it is the $\epsilon$-greedy policy wrt $Q$. This means that it's basically updated every time $Q$ is updated in line 6. You're right that $\pi_b$ and $\pi$ are essentially equivalent here. 

That said, there are variants of Q-learning which operate in the "asynchronous setting" where the policy used to collect data $\pi_b$ is different from the $\epsilon$-greedy policy with respect to $Q$. You can imagine this might be useful in settings where you need to run an entire episode at once with a fixed policy and cannot afford to update your policy in the middle of the episode (e.g., societal applications of RL to education or healthcare). In these cases, there are works which prove guarantees when $\pi_b$ is different from $\pi$. This might be why the pseudocode is written in generality, to emphasize that $\pi_b$ usually is the same as $\pi$ but technically needn't be. 

Does this help?

title: Difference between Q function vs Value Function.
content: How different is the Q function when compared with the value function ? Want to know as I'm little confused on how we get to do the Monte Carlo policy evaluation using the exploration and exploitation approach.
document: How different is the Q function when compared with the value function ? Want to know as I'm little confused on how we get to do the Monte Carlo policy evaluation using the exploration and exploitation approach.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You can think of the value function at $s$ (that is, $V(s)$) as (an estimate of) the expected reward you would obtain, given that you start in $s$. </paragraph><paragraph>You can think of the q-function at $(s,a)$ (that is, $Q(s, a)$) as (an estimate of) the reward you would obtain if: </paragraph><list style="bullet"><list-item><paragraph>you start at state $s$ and take action $a$ at the first time step </paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>and then bootsrap your expected future rewards</paragraph></list-item></list></document>
answers.document:
You can think of the value function at $s$ (that is, $V(s)$) as (an estimate of) the expected reward you would obtain, given that you start in $s$. 

You can think of the q-function at $(s,a)$ (that is, $Q(s, a)$) as (an estimate of) the reward you would obtain if: 

you start at state $s$ and take action $a$ at the first time step 

and then bootsrap your expected future rewards

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Aaah that makes sense, so you just plug in the previous state related value function to further progress in your value iteration using value function right?</paragraph><paragraph>But with Q-Learning we need to perform an action to get the reward at a particular state S and then use that reward, to boot strap further and get your expected future rewards, correct ?</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Aaah that makes sense, so you just plug in the previous state related value function to further progress in your value iteration using value function right?

But with Q-Learning we need to perform an action to get the reward at a particular state S and then use that reward, to boot strap further and get your expected future rewards, correct ?

content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Based on the definition of TD(0) and Monte Carlo in this case and how TD error is calculated, does it mean that TD(n) is equal to monte carlo where the horizon is n itself?</paragraph></document>
document: Based on the definition of TD(0) and Monte Carlo in this case and how TD error is calculated, does it mean that TD(n) is equal to monte carlo where the horizon is n itself?

I'm not sure if this is discussed in the lecture, so just want to make sure I understand what you're looking at.</paragraph><paragraph>In general, I am more familiar with TD(\lambda), as opposed to TD(n), so I'm curious if that is what you mean to refer to. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Just to make sure I understand the question, may I ask what you are referring to when you say TD(n)? I'm not sure if this is discussed in the lecture, so just want to make sure I understand what you're looking at.

title: Lecture Question
content: Why does it cause correlations?
document: Why does it cause correlations?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Because during Q-learning with value function approximation, the data comes from trajectories generated by the agent interacting with the environment. Transitions sampled along the same trajectory are correlated.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: Because during Q-learning with value function approximation, the data comes from trajectories generated by the agent interacting with the environment. Transitions sampled along the same trajectory are correlated.

title: Score Function
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi,<break/><break/>I am looking for a further explanation of the score function and why it works and it is valid even if f (x) is discontinuous, and unknown, or sample space (containing x) is a discrete set</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi,

I am looking for a further explanation of the score function and why it works and it is valid even if f (x) is discontinuous, and unknown, or sample space (containing x) is a discrete set

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Not sure if this is the answer you seek but the score function is exactly equivalent to the gradient update in gradient descent if multiplied by a learning rate. It gives you the direction/slope of your loss/error's ascent.<break/><break/></paragraph></document>
answers.document: Not sure if this is the answer you seek but the score function is exactly equivalent to the gradient update in gradient descent if multiplied by a learning rate. It gives you the direction/slope of your loss/error's ascent.

title: DQN vs SuperVised Learning
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Unlike the Supervised Learning we will have an true value estimate in the case of the DQN settings. Wondering if when we perform the backprop to learn the weights, we might see some unstable training and the hyperparameters learned might not be giving us the best prediction results. Can i get some help in understanding this differentiation ?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: Unlike the Supervised Learning we will have an true value estimate in the case of the DQN settings. Wondering if when we perform the backprop to learn the weights, we might see some unstable training and the hyperparameters learned might not be giving us the best prediction results. Can i get some help in understanding this differentiation ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Unlike supervised learning, where the loss is computed between the prediction of the model and the ground-truth, in DQN, we <italic>do not</italic> have a true target in the supervised-learning sense. What we have is a <italic>bootstrapped target</italic>, not ground truth.</paragraph><paragraph>More specifically, in DQN, our target is $y = r + \gamma \text{max}_{a'} Q_{w^-}(s', a')$, where $y$ is not the ground-truth and is an estimate produced by another network $Q_{w^-}$. In other words, DQN is learning from its own predictions. This is one of the reasons learning with DQN is unstable. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Unlike supervised learning, where the loss is computed between the prediction of the model and the ground-truth, in DQN, we do not have a true target in the supervised-learning sense. What we have is a bootstrapped target, not ground truth.

More specifically, in DQN, our target is $y = r + \gamma \text{max}_{a'} Q_{w^-}(s', a')$, where $y$ is not the ground-truth and is an estimate produced by another network $Q_{w^-}$. In other words, DQN is learning from its own predictions. This is one of the reasons learning with DQN is unstable.

answers.document:
Bootstrapping is basically when you have updates to your agent by guessing or estimating about the value of a state/action based on its other current guesses or states/actions; it is different than waiting to see the final outcome

i usually associate bootstrapping with estimation

so here in slide 37 you update the value function using the estimates from past value functions and V(s_t+1)

title: Check your Understanding: Fixed Targets
content: Hello Teaching Team,I get the part that having separate set of weights for the target and we do update these old weights with the interval of C as per the DQN Pseudocode, Given we have to keep computing the rewards and have to update them often, doesn't this necessarily double the computation time at some time along with doubling the memory requirements ?
document:
Hello Teaching Team,

I get the part that having separate set of weights for the target and we do update these old weights with the interval of C as per the DQN Pseudocode, Given we have to keep computing the rewards and have to update them often, doesn't this necessarily double the computation time at some time along with doubling the memory requirements ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You are right that the target weights are updated with an interval of C, but target network updates are usually done by either directly copying the online network weights to the target network ($w^- \leftarrow w$) or through Polyak averaging ($w^- \leftarrow \tau w + (1-\tau)w^-$ for some hyperparameter $\tau$). Such an assignment process does not require forward/backward passes in the Q networks and does not include optimizer updates. Therefore, the extra computation required to keep a target network does not incur doubled computation time.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: You are right that the target weights are updated with an interval of C, but target network updates are usually done by either directly copying the online network weights to the target network ($w^- \leftarrow w$) or through Polyak averaging ($w^- \leftarrow \tau w + (1-\tau)w^-$ for some hyperparameter $\tau$). Such an assignment process does not require forward/backward passes in the Q networks and does not include optimizer updates. Therefore, the extra computation required to keep a target network does not incur doubled computation time.

title: Policy Performance Identity Clarification
content: I'm lost as to what we are summing over on the second and third line. Are the actions we are summing over from \pi^{'} or is it from just \pi? In the first line it seems to be from pi prime but I'm lost as to how the switch happens.
document: I'm lost as to what we are summing over on the second and third line. Are the actions we are summing over from \pi^{'} or is it from just \pi? In the first line it seems to be from pi prime but I'm lost as to how the switch happens.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for the question. In the first line, actions and states are from policy $\pi$.</paragraph><paragraph>In the second line, we re-express the expectation over actions in $\pi'$: </paragraph><math>E_{s\sim d^{\pi'},a\sim\pi'}\left[...\right]\ =E_{\left[s\sim d^{\pi'}\right]}E_{a\sim\pi'}\ =E_{\left[s\sim d^{\pi'}\right]}\sum_{_{a\ }}^{ }\pi'(a|s)</math><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
Thanks for the question. In the first line, actions and states are from policy $\pi$.

In the second line, we re-express the expectation over actions in $\pi'$: 

$$E_{s\sim d^{\pi'},a\sim\pi'}\left[...\right]\ =E_{\left[s\sim d^{\pi'}\right]}E_{a\sim\pi'}\ =E_{\left[s\sim d^{\pi'}\right]}\sum_{_{a\ }}^{ }\pi'(a|s)$$

title: Doubt in actor-critic Method
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Why are we using two separate models for Q-learning and baseline? Baseline is just calculating the value function which is the expected value of Q over the actions space. So, isn't the Q-learning sufficient and baseline is not required?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: Why are we using two separate models for Q-learning and baseline? Baseline is just calculating the value function which is the expected value of Q over the actions space. So, isn't the Q-learning sufficient and baseline is not required?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>(Deep) Q-learning is a value-based method where we only learn Q(s,a) and extract a policy from it (e.g., greedy).</paragraph><paragraph>Policy gradient methods like REINFORCE do something else. They directly learn a policy π(a|s) without learning a value.</paragraph><paragraph>Actor-critic methods combine these ideas: the actor is a representation of the policy π(a | s), while the critic is usually a representation of either V^π(s) or Q^π(s, a). There are algorithms that learn both V and Q, but learning one or the other is sufficient.</paragraph><paragraph>As shown in Lecture 6, adding a baseline to policy gradient methods (thus making them actor-critic) reduces the variance. Intuitively, the baseline causes the policy to be updated based only on whether an action was better or worse than expected, rather than on how good or bad the return was in absolute terms.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
(Deep) Q-learning is a value-based method where we only learn Q(s,a) and extract a policy from it (e.g., greedy).

Policy gradient methods like REINFORCE do something else. They directly learn a policy π(a|s) without learning a value.

Actor-critic methods combine these ideas: the actor is a representation of the policy π(a | s), while the critic is usually a representation of either V^π(s) or Q^π(s, a). There are algorithms that learn both V and Q, but learning one or the other is sufficient.

As shown in Lecture 6, adding a baseline to policy gradient methods (thus making them actor-critic) reduces the variance. Intuitively, the baseline causes the policy to be updated based only on whether an action was better or worse than expected, rather than on how good or bad the return was in absolute terms.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Right, so in the case of actor-critic, we are explicitly calculating learning Q values and baseline is just an estimate of the value function. Why can't we use the Q values to get the value function instead of using a separate model for baseline?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Right, so in the case of actor-critic, we are explicitly calculating learning Q values and baseline is just an estimate of the value function. Why can't we use the Q values to get the value function instead of using a separate model for baseline?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>There exist actor-critic algorithms that learn V directly (e.g., PPO, TRPO), there exist algorithms that learn Q directly (e.g., DDPG) and then extract V from Q, and there exist algorithms that learn both V and Q (SAC). In all cases, the policy update uses an estimate of a value function as a baseline.</paragraph><paragraph>This is a modeling choice that depends on your setting. Often (but not always), learning V directly is more efficient and stable than learning Q(s,a) for all a and extracting V. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
There exist actor-critic algorithms that learn V directly (e.g., PPO, TRPO), there exist algorithms that learn Q directly (e.g., DDPG) and then extract V from Q, and there exist algorithms that learn both V and Q (SAC). In all cases, the policy update uses an estimate of a value function as a baseline.

This is a modeling choice that depends on your setting. Often (but not always), learning V directly is more efficient and stable than learning Q(s,a) for all a and extracting V.

title: what is the value of \alpha in lecture 4, page 10?
content: As titled.
document: As titled.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This problem is asking what TD converges to after infinitely many iterations over the data, and there are thus many values of alpha that could lead to convergence. If it were asking what the TD estimates would equal after just one iteration, the precise value of alpha would matter.</paragraph><paragraph>Specifically, any sequence of alpha_t satisfying the Robbins-Munro conditions (slide 37) will converge</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
This problem is asking what TD converges to after infinitely many iterations over the data, and there are thus many values of alpha that could lead to convergence. If it were asking what the TD estimates would equal after just one iteration, the precise value of alpha would matter.

Specifically, any sequence of alpha_t satisfying the Robbins-Munro conditions (slide 37) will converge

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>So, in the slide, V(B) = 0.75 means, after infinitely many iterations, V(B) would converges to 0.75 , as long as alpha_t satisfying the Robbins-Munro conditions. Is my understanding correct? </paragraph></document>

title: Score functions: past state and actions
content: For the log P(s_t+1|s_t, a_t), I was wondering how does the score function work when it has to depend on the past state and actions i.e history unlike the next state, current state and action as mentioned in the equation. Like if there's any example for this case it will be super helpful for understanding.
document: For the log P(s_t+1|s_t, a_t), I was wondering how does the score function work when it has to depend on the past state and actions i.e history unlike the next state, current state and action as mentioned in the equation. Like if there's any example for this case it will be super helpful for understanding.

answers.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>The score function is not log P(s_t+1|s_t, a_t). It is the gradient of the log policy with respect to the parameters (last line of your screen shot). It will not depend on previous states and actions due to the Markov property. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: The score function is not log P(s_t+1|s_t, a_t). It is the gradient of the log policy with respect to the parameters (last line of your screen shot). It will not depend on previous states and actions due to the Markov property.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just looking at your conf and not having any access to your code/env, you have some potential problems, which if you're lucky won't manifest. For e.g. the shared libs corresponding to bullet-cpp will be linked to both pybullet-envs(pybullet) and pybullet-envs-gymnasium but if those shared libs are not what the individual packages were compiled with you're going to get segfaults.     </paragraph></document>
answers.document: Just looking at your conf and not having any access to your code/env, you have some potential problems, which if you're lucky won't manifest. For e.g. the shared libs corresponding to bullet-cpp will be linked to both pybullet-envs(pybullet) and pybullet-envs-gymnasium but if those shared libs are not what the individual packages were compiled with you're going to get segfaults.

title: Lecture3 slides understanding check
content: Hi CS234 stuff,I've got a 2 questions on lecture 3's slides. Can you help me check if I'm understanding this correctly?Q1. In page 26, the slides says 1. First-visit MC is also consistent and can be proved2. Given $n \ne \infty$, we usually have $Var(\theta^{\text{Every Visit EC}}) < Var(\theta^{\text{First Visit EC}})$ ?Am I understanding this correctly?Q2.In page 43, the slides saysThe MLE MDP Models will converge to right estimate for Markov models and it is consistent. I'd like to check that heremodel-based is referring to models like in ML and DL (NN for example?)the consistent also requires the model used should be able to represent the distribution of P (transition distribution) and r (reward distribution)?the environment itself (P) is Markov? BTW, also want to check if proving one sampling estimator is consistent or unbiased is required for the midterm?
document:
Hi CS234 stuff,

I've got a 2 questions on lecture 3's slides. Can you help me check if I'm understanding this correctly?

Q1. 

In page 26, the slides says 

1. First-visit MC is also consistent and can be proved

2. Given $n \ne \infty$, we usually have $Var(\theta^{\text{Every Visit EC}}) < Var(\theta^{\text{First Visit EC}})$ ?
Am I understanding this correctly?


Q2.

In page 43, the slides says

The MLE MDP Models will converge to right estimate for Markov models and it is consistent. I'd like to check that here

model-based is referring to models like in ML and DL (NN for example?)

the consistent also requires 

the model used should be able to represent the distribution of P (transition distribution) and r (reward distribution)?

the environment itself (P) is Markov? 


BTW, also want to check if proving one sampling estimator is consistent or unbiased is required for the midterm?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Q1<break/>1. First-visit MC is also consistent and can be proved. </paragraph><paragraph>This is right.</paragraph><paragraph>2. We usually have <italic>Var</italic>(<italic>θ</italic>Every Visit MC)<<italic>Var</italic>(<italic>θ</italic>First Visit MC)</paragraph><paragraph>This is not guaranteed. The variance of every visit MC is dependent on how correlated the visits are within an episode.</paragraph><paragraph>Q2<break/><break/>1. model-based is referring to models like in ML and DL (NN for example?)</paragraph><paragraph>Yes</paragraph><paragraph>2. the consistent also requires </paragraph><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph>the model used should be able to represent the distribution of P (transition distribution) and r (reward distribution)?</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>Yes, the model needs to be expressive enough.</paragraph><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph>the environment itself (P) is Markov? </paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>Yes, the MLE equation assumes the markov property.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
Q1
1. First-visit MC is also consistent and can be proved. 

This is right.

2. We usually have Var(θEvery Visit MC)<Var(θFirst Visit MC)

This is not guaranteed. The variance of every visit MC is dependent on how correlated the visits are within an episode.

Q2

1. model-based is referring to models like in ML and DL (NN for example?)

Yes

2. the consistent also requires 

the model used should be able to represent the distribution of P (transition distribution) and r (reward distribution)?

Yes, the model needs to be expressive enough.

the environment itself (P) is Markov? 

Yes, the MLE equation assumes the markov property.

title: certainty equivalence "very data efficient"
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>On slide 43 of the Lecture 3 pre-class slides, it says certainty equivalence is "very data efficient"</paragraph><paragraph>Does this just mean more data efficient than Monte Carlo, since we don't need to keep track of a full episode before calculating?</paragraph><paragraph>Certainty Equivalence is less data efficient than Temporal Difference, right? Because in both we can discard tuples once we've used them to update our value function, but in Certainty Equivalence we'll need to save an additional reward and maximum likelihood MDP model for every state and action on top?</paragraph></document>
document:
On slide 43 of the Lecture 3 pre-class slides, it says certainty equivalence is "very data efficient"

Does this just mean more data efficient than Monte Carlo, since we don't need to keep track of a full episode before calculating?

Certainty Equivalence is less data efficient than Temporal Difference, right? Because in both we can discard tuples once we've used them to update our value function, but in Certainty Equivalence we'll need to save an additional reward and maximum likelihood MDP model for every state and action on top?

answers.content:
From the Book (2nd edition), P154
Finally, it is worth noting that although the certainty-equivalence estimateis in some sense an optimal solution, it is almost never feasible to computeit directly. If N is the number of states, then just forming the maximum-likelihood estimate of the process may require N2 memory, and computing thecorresponding value function requires on the order of N3 computational stepsif done conventionally. In these terms it is indeed striking that TD methodscan approximate the same solution using memory no more than N and re-peated computations over the training set. On tasks with large state spaces,TD methods may be the only feasible way of approximating the certainty-equivalence solution.
I think it basically states that Certainty Equivalence is more data efficient but requires more memory ($O(N^2)$ or $O(N^3)$.
answers.document:
From the Book (2nd edition), P154

Finally, it is worth noting that although the certainty-equivalence estimate
is in some sense an optimal solution, it is almost never feasible to compute
it directly. If N is the number of states, then just forming the maximum-
likelihood estimate of the process may require N2 memory, and computing the
corresponding value function requires on the order of N3 computational steps
if done conventionally. In these terms it is indeed striking that TD methods
can approximate the same solution using memory no more than N and re-
peated computations over the training set. On tasks with large state spaces,
TD methods may be the only feasible way of approximating the certainty-
equivalence solution.

I think it basically states that Certainty Equivalence is more data efficient but requires more memory ($O(N^2)$ or $O(N^3)$.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Data efficiency is a different concept from memory efficiency. Data efficiency of an algorithm is: how much performance improvement does more training data buy me using this algorithm. Memory efficiency is: how much memory do I need to store all the vectors/matrices for this algorithm.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Data efficiency is a different concept from memory efficiency. Data efficiency of an algorithm is: how much performance improvement does more training data buy me using this algorithm. Memory efficiency is: how much memory do I need to store all the vectors/matrices for this algorithm.

title: Why do we call the score function a likelihood ratio?
content: "Likelihood ratio" has a very specific meaning in statistics as the ratio of two likelihoods, and it typically arises in the context of hypothesis testing. Is there an intuition as to why we equate it to the score function in RL?
document: "Likelihood ratio" has a very specific meaning in statistics as the ratio of two likelihoods, and it typically arises in the context of hypothesis testing. Is there an intuition as to why we equate it to the score function in RL?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi Suchir, the "likelihood ratio" referred to in the slides is different from the likelihood ratio in other contexts you referred to e.g. importance sampling or hypothesis testing.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Hi Suchir, the "likelihood ratio" referred to in the slides is different from the likelihood ratio in other contexts you referred to e.g. importance sampling or hypothesis testing.

title: A2 Q2 plots
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I saw a few threads related to the plots, but just wanted to check my understanding regarding correctness.</paragraph><paragraph>For cartpole, all methods converge to 200 (some faster or noisier). Similar for pendulum converging to 1000, however PPO and no baseline sometimes drop dramatically from 1000 to ~500 and back up after convergence. I think this is fine behavior.</paragraph><paragraph>For cheetah, all 3 methods get up to ~500 reward for a few iterations then converge around -1000. No baseline dramatically drops down to -60k then back up to -1000 at iteration 150. The writeup says cheetah should reach at least 200, which this does, but this convergence behavior seems suspicious. Is this something to be worried about, or is this okay because it still reaches 200?</paragraph></document>
document:
I saw a few threads related to the plots, but just wanted to check my understanding regarding correctness.

For cartpole, all methods converge to 200 (some faster or noisier). Similar for pendulum converging to 1000, however PPO and no baseline sometimes drop dramatically from 1000 to ~500 and back up after convergence. I think this is fine behavior.

For cheetah, all 3 methods get up to ~500 reward for a few iterations then converge around -1000. No baseline dramatically drops down to -60k then back up to -1000 at iteration 150. The writeup says cheetah should reach at least 200, which this does, but this convergence behavior seems suspicious. Is this something to be worried about, or is this okay because it still reaches 200?

title: Definition of Reward
content: Sorry for asking a naive question....I am quite confused about reward and states.Sometimes, the reward is a single attribute with state. For example, in the Mars Rover example in the following image, reward of r2 = 0. The reward value is a fixed constant of a given stateHowever, on another slide, R is a function of both s and a.Here, if a = left which always make the agent turn left, then r(s_2, a) = 1, not 0. Therefore, I am wondering, does reward in MDP has two different definitions? In the lecture, professor mentioned that mathematically, these differences (influding r(s, a, s')) dosn't matter, but I cannot understand the reason.
document:
Sorry for asking a naive question....

I am quite confused about reward and states.

Sometimes, the reward is a single attribute with state. For example, in the Mars Rover example in the following image, reward of r2 = 0. The reward value is a fixed constant of a given state





However, on another slide, R is a function of both s and a.



Here, if a = left which always make the agent turn left, then r(s_2, a) = 1, not 0. 

Therefore, I am wondering, does reward in MDP has two different definitions? In the lecture, professor mentioned that mathematically, these differences (influding r(s, a, s')) dosn't matter, but I cannot understand the reason.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>These expressions of reward are largely equivalent. In the Mars Rover example, we used $R(s)$ because in this specific example, the reward only depends on the current state. In a more general situation though, the reward depends on both the current state and action: $R(s,a)$.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: These expressions of reward are largely equivalent. In the Mars Rover example, we used $R(s)$ because in this specific example, the reward only depends on the current state. In a more general situation though, the reward depends on both the current state and action: $R(s,a)$.

title: Different policy notation in poligy-gradient methods
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Sometimes, in the policy gradient notes, we see $\pi_{\theta}(a_t, s_t)$ to represent the policy parameterized by $\theta$ of $s_t$ and $a_t$. Other times, I've seen $\pi(a_t, s_t; \theta)$, where this also means a policy parameterized by $\theta$. The second one comes up in PG with baseline--is there any difference between the two?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: Sometimes, in the policy gradient notes, we see $\pi_{\theta}(a_t, s_t)$ to represent the policy parameterized by $\theta$ of $s_t$ and $a_t$. Other times, I've seen $\pi(a_t, s_t; \theta)$, where this also means a policy parameterized by $\theta$. The second one comes up in PG with baseline--is there any difference between the two?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I don't think there's a difference, but note that it's usually a conditional probability, i.e. $\pi_\theta (a_t | s_t)$ not a joint probability</paragraph></document>
answers.document: I don't think there's a difference, but note that it's usually a conditional probability, i.e. $\pi_\theta (a_t | s_t)$ not a joint probability

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Right, thanks for catching that!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Right, thanks for catching that!

title: Are optimal policies always deterministic in fully observable states?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>See title for question</paragraph></document>
document: See title for question

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For an infinite horizon MDP, there always exists an stationary optimal policy which is deterministic, but there might be some stationary stochastic policies which have an equal value of $V^{*}$ for every state s and are optimal as well. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: For an infinite horizon MDP, there always exists an stationary optimal policy which is deterministic, but there might be some stationary stochastic policies which have an equal value of $V^{*}$ for every state s and are optimal as well.

title: Q2.7b how to interpret
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi, I am a bit unsure of whether my interpretation of Q2.7b is correct. We can define </paragraph><math>L(\Theta)=\mathbb{E}{\tau \sim \pi_k}[\sum_{t=0}^T L_t(\Theta)]</math><paragraph>where L_t(\Theta) is the objective for a single t. So </paragraph><math>\nabla L(\Theta)=\mathbb{E}{\tau \sim \pi_k}[\sum_{t=0}^T \nabla L_t(\Theta)]</math><paragraph>Wouldn't it then be the case that there are many cases where L(theta) as can be 0? i.e. when some L_t(theta)'s are positive and others are negative, and they cancel out? Or do we need to reason about each individual L_t(theta) only?</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi, I am a bit unsure of whether my interpretation of Q2.7b is correct. We can define 

$$L(\Theta)=\mathbb{E}{\tau \sim \pi_k}[\sum_{t=0}^T L_t(\Theta)]$$

where L_t(\Theta) is the objective for a single t. So 

$$\nabla L(\Theta)=\mathbb{E}{\tau \sim \pi_k}[\sum_{t=0}^T \nabla L_t(\Theta)]$$

Wouldn't it then be the case that there are many cases where L(theta) as can be 0? i.e. when some L_t(theta)'s are positive and others are negative, and they cancel out? Or do we need to reason about each individual L_t(theta) only?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You need to reason about each individual $L_{t}(\theta)$ only. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: You need to reason about each individual $L_{t}(\theta)$ only.

title: PPO Ratio
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just to confirm, we can assume the PPO ratio $z_{\theta} = \frac{\pi_{\theta}}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}} > 0$?</paragraph></document>
document: Just to confirm, we can assume the PPO ratio $z_{\theta} = \frac{\pi_{\theta}}{\pi_{\theta_{\text{old}}}} > 0$?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Yes. $\pi_\theta$ and $\pi_{\theta_old}$ are both probabilities and are non-negative. Therefore, $z_\theta \geq 0$.</paragraph><paragraph>In principle, if $\pi_\theta = 0$, then $z_\theta = 0$. In practice though, policies are typically parameterized as smooth distributions, such as Gaussians for continuous actions or softmax-parameterized categorical distributions for discrete actions, which assign non-zero probability to all actions in their support. As a result, $z_\theta$ is almost always strictly positive in practice.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Yes. $\pi_\theta$ and $\pi_{\theta_old}$ are both probabilities and are non-negative. Therefore, $z_\theta \geq 0$.

In principle, if $\pi_\theta = 0$, then $z_\theta = 0$. In practice though, policies are typically parameterized as smooth distributions, such as Gaussians for continuous actions or softmax-parameterized categorical distributions for discrete actions, which assign non-zero probability to all actions in their support. As a result, $z_\theta$ is almost always strictly positive in practice.

comments.document:
Looking at the expression for $\nabla{\textbf{w}} J(w)$, we have a factor of 2 in the expression.
The -1/2$\alpha$ is there such that the update equation doesn't have this constant factor being multiplied in order to get a simple update equation. 

$$

\Delta \mathbf{w} = -\frac{1}{2}\alpha \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w})

$$

$$

\Delta \mathbf{w} = -\frac{1}{2}\alpha \left( -2\mathbb{E}{\pi}[(Q^\pi(s, a) - \hat{Q}(s, a; \mathbf{w}))\nabla{\mathbf{w}} \hat{Q}(s, a; \mathbf{w})] \right)

$$
this leads to a cleaner expression

title: Practice midterm 4b
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi! I'm a bit confused on the practice midterm question 4b. Specifically, the solution states that "since $B_s$ only modifies one entry, we must have $\|B_sV' - B_sV''\|_\infty = 1$." I'm a little confused as to why this is true, and why the difference of the modified entry's bellman backup $B_sV'(s) - B_sV''(s)$ is unable to exceed 1 in this case?</paragraph><paragraph>Would it also be sufficient to make the argument that if $i\neq s$ (where $i$ is the index of $\max_i |B_sV'(s) - B_sV''(s)|$, then $\|B_sV'-B_sV''\| = \|V'-V''\| > \gamma \|V'-V''\|$ (since $0 < \gamma < 1$)?</paragraph><paragraph>Thank you!</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi! I'm a bit confused on the practice midterm question 4b. Specifically, the solution states that "since $B_s$ only modifies one entry, we must have $\|B_sV' - B_sV''\|_\infty = 1$." I'm a little confused as to why this is true, and why the difference of the modified entry's bellman backup $B_sV'(s) - B_sV''(s)$ is unable to exceed 1 in this case?

Would it also be sufficient to make the argument that if $i\neq s$ (where $i$ is the index of $\max_i |B_sV'(s) - B_sV''(s)|$, then $\|B_sV'-B_sV''\| = \|V'-V''\| > \gamma \|V'-V''\|$ (since $0 < \gamma < 1$)?

Thank you!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Let's say that we modify entry i with B_s. If applying the update resulted in</paragraph><math>\left|B_sV'\left(i\right)-B_sV''\left(i\right)\right|>1</math><paragraph>then this would contradict the synchronous Bellman backup B^π being a contraction. And for the remaining entries j ≠ i, the norm is unchanged. </paragraph><paragraph>To answer your second question, yes I think this is sufficient.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Let's say that we modify entry i with B_s. If applying the update resulted in

$$\left|B_sV'\left(i\right)-B_sV''\left(i\right)\right|>1$$

then this would contradict the synchronous Bellman backup B^π being a contraction. And for the remaining entries j ≠ i, the norm is unchanged. 

To answer your second question, yes I think this is sufficient.

title: DNQ on non-Markov
content: From what I understand: since DNQ is an extension of Q-Learning, which is an extension of TD algorithms which assume our reward/dynamics model is Markov, DNQ will only work on Markov problems. It's not supposed to be used when our problem is non-MarkovIs this correct?I tried Googling and the AI overview seems to suggest that DNQ can actually work on non-Markovian problems even if normal Q-learning fails on them. Should I trust this? Or should I treat it as a Markov-only algorithm for now?
document:
From what I understand: since DNQ is an extension of Q-Learning, which is an extension of TD algorithms which assume our reward/dynamics model is Markov, DNQ will only work on Markov problems. It's not supposed to be used when our problem is non-Markov

Is this correct?

I tried Googling and the AI overview seems to suggest that DNQ can actually work on non-Markovian problems even if normal Q-learning fails on them. Should I trust this? Or should I treat it as a Markov-only algorithm for now?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In cases where we have to find a policy for a non-Markovian MDP, we generally change the state space to include some history of states, which works well as an input when we are using a function approximator. Hence in practice these methods work well for non-Markovian MDPS when the dynamics can be represented by a short history of states. <break/><break/></paragraph></document>
answers.document: In cases where we have to find a policy for a non-Markovian MDP, we generally change the state space to include some history of states, which works well as an input when we are using a function approximator. Hence in practice these methods work well for non-Markovian MDPS when the dynamics can be represented by a short history of states.

title: Lecture 4, slide 48 and 49
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Should the Qπ (st ,at ) be Vπ (st ), since it's the value function prediction for policy evaluation not control on action-value function?<break/><break/>Thank you.</paragraph></document>
document:
Should the Qπ (st ,at ) be Vπ (st ), since it's the value function prediction for policy evaluation not control on action-value function?

Thank you.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Remember that we are trying to do model-free policy evaluation. If we do get a estimate for $V^{\pi}(s_t)$, we still wouldn't be able to obtain the optimal policy as we do not have the dynamics model.<break/><break/>Hence we try to learn $Q^{\pi}(s_{t}, a_{t})$, as we can try to uncover the best action using the argmax operator. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Remember that we are trying to do model-free policy evaluation. If we do get a estimate for $V^{\pi}(s_t)$, we still wouldn't be able to obtain the optimal policy as we do not have the dynamics model.

Hence we try to learn $Q^{\pi}(s_{t}, a_{t})$, as we can try to uncover the best action using the argmax operator.

title: Lecture 5, slide 12: Why can we break up expectations?
content: Why can we break the expectation in the first line into nested expectations? Is there some rule of expectations I should be familiar with? Or some assumption that is being made here that allows us to do this?
document:
Why can we break the expectation in the first line into nested expectations? 

Is there some rule of expectations I should be familiar with? Or some assumption that is being made here that allows us to do this?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This is a result of the <link href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_total_expectation">tower property/law of total expectation</link>:</paragraph><math>\mathbb{E}_{X, Y}[f(X, Y)] = \mathbb{E}_{X}[ \mathbb{E}_{Y}[f(X, Y) | X]]</math><paragraph>where X is the trajectory up until time t and Y is the trajectory starting at time t</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
This is a result of the tower property/law of total expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}_{X, Y}[f(X, Y)] = \mathbb{E}_{X}[ \mathbb{E}_{Y}[f(X, Y) | X]]$$

where X is the trajectory up until time t and Y is the trajectory starting at time t

title: Policy gradient theorem
content:
<document version="2.0"><paragraph>The policy gradient theorem states $
abla_{	heta} J(	heta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{theta}} \left[ 
abla_{	heta} \log \pi_{	heta}(s, a) Q^{\pi_{	heta}}(s,a)  ight]$. However, I also see (lecture 6) $
abla_{	heta} V(	heta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{theta}} \left[ 
abla_{	heta} \log \pi_{	heta}(s, a) Q^{\pi_{	heta}}(s,a)  ight]$.</paragraph><paragraph>Are $V(	heta)$ and $J(	heta)$ both the same thing (expected return of a policy)?</paragraph></document>
document:
The policy gradient theorem states $
abla_{	heta} J(	heta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{theta}} \left[ 
abla_{	heta} \log \pi_{	heta}(s, a) Q^{\pi_{	heta}}(s,a)  ight]$. However, I also see (lecture 6) $
abla_{	heta} V(	heta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_{theta}} \left[ 
abla_{	heta} \log \pi_{	heta}(s, a) Q^{\pi_{	heta}}(s,a)  ight]$.

Are $V(	heta)$ and $J(	heta)$ both the same thing (expected return of a policy)?

title: Clarification on Reward Invariance and Adding Constants
content: During lecture it was stated that you can add any constant to the reward and it will not change the optimal policy. I'm struggling to see why this is true. Here is the example I am thinking about:Consider a simple navigation task with Start State $A$ and Terminal State $C$:Path 1 (Long): $A \to B \to C$ (2 steps).$R(A,B) = 1$$R(B,C) = 1$Total Reward: 2Path 2 (Short): $A \to C$ (1 step).$R(A,C) = 5$Total Reward: 5Current Optimal Policy: Take Path 2.However, if we add a constant $c = 4$ to every step's reward:Path 1 (Long): $(1+4) + (1+4) = 10$Path 2 (Short): $(5+4) = 9$
document:
During lecture it was stated that you can add any constant to the reward and it will not change the optimal policy. 

I'm struggling to see why this is true. Here is the example I am thinking about:

Consider a simple navigation task with Start State $A$ and Terminal State $C$:

Path 1 (Long): $A \to B \to C$ (2 steps).

$R(A,B) = 1$

$R(B,C) = 1$

Total Reward: 2

Path 2 (Short): $A \to C$ (1 step).

$R(A,C) = 5$

Total Reward: 5

Current Optimal Policy: Take Path 2.

However, if we add a constant $c = 4$ to every step's reward:

Path 1 (Long): $(1+4) + (1+4) = 10$

Path 2 (Short): $(5+4) = 9$

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question. I think the slide assumed an infinite-horizon situation with $\gamma < 1$. For an infinite horizon, if we add all rewards by a constant $c$, then our returns become<break/>$$(r_t + c) + \gamma(r_{t+1} + c) + ... = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} (r_{t+k} + c) = G_t + c \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t = G_t + \frac{c}{1-\gamma}$$, so all returns are increased by the same constant $\frac{c}{1-\gamma}$, which will not change the optimal policy.</paragraph><paragraph>In your case, you have an episodic task with variable length, so the constant $c$ is kind of like a "survival" reward that changes the optimal policy.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
Great question. I think the slide assumed an infinite-horizon situation with $\gamma < 1$. For an infinite horizon, if we add all rewards by a constant $c$, then our returns become
$$(r_t + c) + \gamma(r_{t+1} + c) + ... = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} (r_{t+k} + c) = G_t + c \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t = G_t + \frac{c}{1-\gamma}$$, so all returns are increased by the same constant $\frac{c}{1-\gamma}$, which will not change the optimal policy.

In your case, you have an episodic task with variable length, so the constant $c$ is kind of like a "survival" reward that changes the optimal policy.

title: REINFORCE vs Vanilla PG Update
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Are REINFORCE and vanilla PG essentially the same algorithm (REINFORCE is the simplest PG algorithm)? I noticed in the slides that the update rule for REINFORCE is </paragraph><paragraph>$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta} (s_t, a_t) G_t$$</paragraph><paragraph>however Vanilla PG uses</paragraph><paragraph>$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta} (a_t \mid a_t) G_t$$ (this is in the practice exam 2a as well). Is there a reason REINFORCE uses the joint distribution but vanilla PG uses the conditional?</paragraph><paragraph>I was thinking this is essentially notational and both work since we are optimizing, so by taking a log we are just off by an added constant.</paragraph></document>
document:
Are REINFORCE and vanilla PG essentially the same algorithm (REINFORCE is the simplest PG algorithm)? I noticed in the slides that the update rule for REINFORCE is 

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta} (s_t, a_t) G_t$$

however Vanilla PG uses

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_{\theta} \log \pi_{\theta} (a_t \mid a_t) G_t$$ (this is in the practice exam 2a as well). Is there a reason REINFORCE uses the joint distribution but vanilla PG uses the conditional?

I was thinking this is essentially notational and both work since we are optimizing, so by taking a log we are just off by an added constant.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Are REINFORCE and vanilla PG essentially the same algorithm (REINFORCE is the simplest PG algorithm)?<break/>Yes.</paragraph><paragraph>Is there a reason REINFORCE uses the joint distribution but vanilla PG uses the conditional?<break/>These two notations refer to the same probability distribution, though I believe $\pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)$ is more commonly used.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Are REINFORCE and vanilla PG essentially the same algorithm (REINFORCE is the simplest PG algorithm)?
Yes.

Is there a reason REINFORCE uses the joint distribution but vanilla PG uses the conditional?
These two notations refer to the same probability distribution, though I believe $\pi_\theta(a_t|s_t)$ is more commonly used.

title: lecture 4, page 51, why r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') is a biased estimate of V^{\pi}(s)?
content: lecture 4, page 51, why r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') is a biased estimate of V^{\pi}(s)?
document: lecture 4, page 51, why r + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') is a biased estimate of V^{\pi}(s)?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The TD target $r + \gamma V^\pi(s')$ is a biased estimate of the true value $V^\pi(s)$ because it relies on bootstrapping, using an imperfect, approximate value function $V(s')$ rather than the actual, complete future rewards to estimate the next state's value. While $r$ is accurate, the approximated $V(s')$ introduces errors that skew the update, particularly in early learning stages.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: The TD target $r + \gamma V^\pi(s')$ is a biased estimate of the true value $V^\pi(s)$ because it relies on bootstrapping, using an imperfect, approximate value function $V(s')$ rather than the actual, complete future rewards to estimate the next state's value. While $r$ is accurate, the approximated $V(s')$ introduces errors that skew the update, particularly in early learning stages.

title: lecture 4, page 54, why the grad only apply to V(s; w) but not V(s'; w)?
content: lecture 4, page 54, why the grad only apply to V(s; w) but not V(s'; w)?
document: lecture 4, page 54, why the grad only apply to V(s; w) but not V(s'; w)?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>As an exercise, you can define the loss function as: $$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ (\text{Target} - \hat{V}(s; w))^2 \right]$$<break/><break/>Then, with TD(0) as our target, you'll get: $$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} (r + \gamma \hat{V}(s'; w) - \hat{V}(s; w))^2$$<break/><break/>If you take the gradient, you'll end up getting $$\nabla_w J(w) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{V}(s; w)} \cdot \nabla_w \hat{V}(s; w)$$<break/><break/>The 1/2 is simply to remove the factor of 2 to make the final expression clean. In the slides, it was omitted, but both are valid. <break/><break/>Applying this, you'll get the same result as in the slides. </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
As an exercise, you can define the loss function as: $$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ (\text{Target} - \hat{V}(s; w))^2 \right]$$

Then, with TD(0) as our target, you'll get: $$J(w) = \frac{1}{2} (r + \gamma \hat{V}(s'; w) - \hat{V}(s; w))^2$$

If you take the gradient, you'll end up getting $$\nabla_w J(w) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \hat{V}(s; w)} \cdot \nabla_w \hat{V}(s; w)$$

The 1/2 is simply to remove the factor of 2 to make the final expression clean. In the slides, it was omitted, but both are valid. 

Applying this, you'll get the same result as in the slides.

title: Distribution over states visited by pi
content: How does this represent the distribution over states visited by pi? Specifically, why do we include the discount factor?
document: How does this represent the distribution over states visited by pi? Specifically, why do we include the discount factor?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>This is just defining a new distribution over the states; i.e. probability that you will visit a certain state $s$, no matter what time-step (use the law of total probability - so that's why the sum, the $\gamma$ multiplier discounts the probability for later timesteps - so it is really just defining a made-up distribution - which would be useful for the proof). The $\gamma$ discounting is done to match the expression we are taking expectation over in the problem so it is about mathematical usefulness for the proof.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: This is just defining a new distribution over the states; i.e. probability that you will visit a certain state $s$, no matter what time-step (use the law of total probability - so that's why the sum, the $\gamma$ multiplier discounts the probability for later timesteps - so it is really just defining a made-up distribution - which would be useful for the proof). The $\gamma$ discounting is done to match the expression we are taking expectation over in the problem so it is about mathematical usefulness for the proof.
