title: policy gradient update
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi, <break/><break/>for the policy gradient updates, do we need the entire trajectory ? or can we update just after a few episodes. <break/><break/>thanks</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi, 

for the policy gradient updates, do we need the entire trajectory ? or can we update just after a few episodes. 

thanks

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For vanilla policy gradient (REINFORCE), we need episodes to finish before performing an update, because the return $G_t$​ requires summing rewards until the end of the episode. In contrast, when using policy gradient with GAE (or more generally actor–critic methods), returns are bootstrapped using a value function, so updates can be made using partial episodes (fixed-length rollouts) without waiting for termination.</paragraph><paragraph>In both cases, it is common and valid to perform updates using multiple episodes or trajectories collected in a batch, rather than updating after every single episode.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
For vanilla policy gradient (REINFORCE), we need episodes to finish before performing an update, because the return $G_t$​ requires summing rewards until the end of the episode. In contrast, when using policy gradient with GAE (or more generally actor–critic methods), returns are bootstrapped using a value function, so updates can be made using partial episodes (fixed-length rollouts) without waiting for termination.

In both cases, it is common and valid to perform updates using multiple episodes or trajectories collected in a batch, rather than updating after every single episode.

title: PPO Clipping Objective Plots
content: Hello! I was wondering if someone could help explain these plots. I understand the clipping objective at a high level here:When $\hat{A}_t > 0$ (good action): We want to increase $r_t$ (take this action more often). But once $r_t > 1 + \epsilon$, the clipped term $(1+\epsilon)\hat{A}_t$ becomes smaller than the unclipped term $r_t \hat{A}_t$. The $\min$ selects the clipped term, which is constant w.r.t. $\theta$—so the gradient vanishes and there's no further incentive to increase $r_t$.When $\hat{A}_t < 0$ (bad action): We want to decrease $r_t$ (take this action less often). But once $r_t < 1 - \epsilon$, the clipped term $(1-\epsilon)\hat{A}_t$ is more negative than the unclipped term $r_t \hat{A}_t$. The $\min$ selects the clipped term, which is again constant—so the gradient vanishes and there's no further incentive to decrease $r_t$.But I'm a bit confused by how this is presented in the plots. In particular:What is the Y axis here and what is the value at the 'flat line' on both plots?The updates on the A>0 plot appear to be bounded such that 0 < r(theta) < 1 + e, but the updates on the right side do not appear to have a bound on the r value. Is this the case?
document:
Hello! I was wondering if someone could help explain these plots. I understand the clipping objective at a high level here:

When $\hat{A}_t > 0$ (good action): We want to increase $r_t$ (take this action more often). But once $r_t > 1 + \epsilon$, the clipped term $(1+\epsilon)\hat{A}_t$ becomes smaller than the unclipped term $r_t \hat{A}_t$. The $\min$ selects the clipped term, which is constant w.r.t. $\theta$—so the gradient vanishes and there's no further incentive to increase $r_t$.

When $\hat{A}_t < 0$ (bad action): We want to decrease $r_t$ (take this action less often). But once $r_t < 1 - \epsilon$, the clipped term $(1-\epsilon)\hat{A}_t$ is more negative than the unclipped term $r_t \hat{A}_t$. The $\min$ selects the clipped term, which is again constant—so the gradient vanishes and there's no further incentive to decrease $r_t$.



But I'm a bit confused by how this is presented in the plots. In particular:

What is the Y axis here and what is the value at the 'flat line' on both plots?

The updates on the A>0 plot appear to be bounded such that 0 < r(theta) < 1 + e, but the updates on the right side do not appear to have a bound on the r value. Is this the case?

answers.content: Y axis is referring to the value of the following function L (not to be confused with Loss). This value remains constant after clipping hence the flat line, the value itself is less important.When you have A < 0, the new policy is performing worse than your reference policy, as a result you would want your r value to be as less as possible to maximize the overall L (since L is negative).One observation from the graph is that as r increases, L becomes more negative (unbounded as r increases). We are trying to maximize the objective (L) so we will try to find the smallest r value which in this case is 1 - e due to the lower bound for the clipping function.
answers.document:
Y axis is referring to the value of the following function L (not to be confused with Loss). This value remains constant after clipping hence the flat line, the value itself is less important.

When you have A < 0, the new policy is performing worse than your reference policy, as a result you would want your r value to be as less as possible to maximize the overall L (since L is negative).
One observation from the graph is that as r increases, L becomes more negative (unbounded as r increases). We are trying to maximize the objective (L) so we will try to find the smallest r value which in this case is 1 - e due to the lower bound for the clipping function.

title: REINFORCE
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I’m sorry this is a trivial and not urgent question, but why was the name REINFORCE accepted? It feels a bit confusing—like having an algorithm called DEEP or CONVEX inside the field of Deep Learning or Convex Optimization. REINFORCE is a good algorithm as the ancestor of policy gradient methods, but the name can be somewhat misleading.</paragraph></document>
document: I’m sorry this is a trivial and not urgent question, but why was the name REINFORCE accepted? It feels a bit confusing—like having an algorithm called DEEP or CONVEX inside the field of Deep Learning or Convex Optimization. REINFORCE is a good algorithm as the ancestor of policy gradient methods, but the name can be somewhat misleading.

answers.content: Haha that's a good question. I did some quick digging, and the person who coined it REINFORCE seemed to have done it as an acronym to represent the update rule:I agree it can be a confusing name to accept, but the algorithm does somewhat hold up to its name because we are explicitly reinforcing actions that outperform a baseline (positive advantage).source: Simple statistical gradient-following algorithms for connectionist reinforcement learning | Machine Learning | Springer Nature Link
answers.document:
Haha that's a good question. I did some quick digging, and the person who coined it REINFORCE seemed to have done it as an acronym to represent the update rule:

I agree it can be a confusing name to accept, but the algorithm does somewhat hold up to its name because we are explicitly reinforcing actions that outperform a baseline (positive advantage).

source: Simple statistical gradient-following algorithms for connectionist reinforcement learning | Machine Learning | Springer Nature Link

title: Practice Midterm: 3c
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Experience Replay [2pts]</paragraph><paragraph> If the materials are expensive, would experience replay be useful during the process and why or why not? </paragraph><paragraph>Solution: Definitely, experience replay is helpful especially when data is expensive, as it improves sample efficiency.</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>My question: Isnt the idea of experience replay that we store more data so if the data is expensive isn't it also expensive to do experience replay too?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Experience Replay [2pts]

 If the materials are expensive, would experience replay be useful during the process and why or why not? 

Solution: Definitely, experience replay is helpful especially when data is expensive, as it improves sample efficiency.



My question: Isnt the idea of experience replay that we store more data so if the data is expensive isn't it also expensive to do experience replay too?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Experience Replay does not necessitate storage of more data, instead it allows us to learn from the same data multiple times (by random sampling multiple times from a replay buffer). Given that the same data is being reused to improve learning we are consequentially increasing the sample efficiency.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: Experience Replay does not necessitate storage of more data, instead it allows us to learn from the same data multiple times (by random sampling multiple times from a replay buffer). Given that the same data is being reused to improve learning we are consequentially increasing the sample efficiency.

document:
Assignment 3 has been released! It is due on Friday, February 20, 6pm PT. This homework covers preference learning (RLHF, DPO), bandits, and more.

Starter code: https://drive.google.com/file/d/18HwwLiMIN9XSdK7QXqQjGyhyb_86Iz_Y/view?usp=drive_link

Changes in grading

For this homework, each question in the writeup is graded on a 3-level rubric (percentages are subject to change):

Complete (100%): You demonstrated correct reasoning and answer (full credit).

Partial (90%): You showed solid progress but had notable errors (partial credit).

Limited (60%): Work shows major conceptual errors or very limited progress (low partial credit).

We grade against clear criteria for each question, not on a curve. Most strong submissions should be in Complete or Partial; Limited is reserved for major misunderstandings.

The coding portion judged by the autograder will still be graded based on correctness on our provided test cases on Gradescope.

Submission Instructions (Please follow these exactly)

Please typeset the written portion of your homework using the LaTeX template we have provided, and do not modify the template itself. If you are new to LaTex, here is an Overleaf tutorial so that you can more easily edit LaTeX files online. 

In particular, the template contains \answer{} environments. You should enter your answers only in the designated sections.

 \answer{
 %%%%% Start of 1(a) %%%%%

Put your answer to 1(a) here.

 %%%%% End of 1(a) %%%%%
 }


For additional submission instructions, please refer to the submission guidelines on the PDF/Latex template!
Updates
- 2/10/26: For question 2, changed trajectories from $\sigma$ to $\tau$ to prevent confusion with sigmoid function. If possible, please use the new template!
- 2/11/26: Per #509 , there was a small bug in the render.py code related to question 2.2c. If possible, please use the new starter code! Since this is an error on our end, if you already submitted/wrote an answer using the old code, we will accept those answers as well as long as you explain your reasoning. However, the labels may make more sense if you use the newest code.
- 2/17/26: Clarification for Q3.1d (DPO vs RLHF comparison): please run the commands in the handout with default settings for consistency across submissions. Note that run_rlhf.py --rl-steps (PPO environment timesteps) and run_dpo.py --num-dpo-steps (DPO optimizer updates) are different units, so this is not a strictly budget-matched comparison. In your discussion, briefly mention this fairness caveat. Optional extra matched-budget analysis (e.g., fixed wall-clock) is welcome but not required.

title: [A3Q2.1a] Can the assignment template be changed to avoid overloading $\sigma$?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Q2.1(a) defines the sigmoid function using $\sigma$ as part of its proof suggestion. However, this comes after a preface that extensively defines and uses $\sigma$ to denote trajectories. Also, the $\sigma$ looks like the $o$ used to denote observations. I think this notational overload will cause confusion on the part of both students and graders (in my opinion unnecessary confusion, as $\tau$ is common RL notation for denoting a trajectory). Why not replace the sigmas in the problem preface with $\tau^{i}$?</paragraph></document>
document: Q2.1(a) defines the sigmoid function using $\sigma$ as part of its proof suggestion. However, this comes after a preface that extensively defines and uses $\sigma$ to denote trajectories. Also, the $\sigma$ looks like the $o$ used to denote observations. I think this notational overload will cause confusion on the part of both students and graders (in my opinion unnecessary confusion, as $\tau$ is common RL notation for denoting a trajectory). Why not replace the sigmas in the problem preface with $\tau^{i}$?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi, thanks for the feedback! I definitely see the confusion. The pdf and template have been updated on the original HW3 release post!</paragraph></document>

title: HW3 2.2c visualizations
content:
<document version="2.0"><paragraph>The rendering code to compare pairwise trajectories seems to always label the left side as preferred over the right side, is that intended? It doesn't seem that the videos obs_1 and obs_2 are correspondingly rearranged to match. Are we supposed to assume the annotator always labels the left side as preferred, or use the actual annotations of 0, 0.5, and 1?</paragraph><snippet language="py" runnable="false" line-numbers="false"><snippet-file id="code">if label == 0.5:
    labels = ["Equally Preferred", "Equally Preferred"]
else:
    labels = ["Preferred", "Not Preferred"]</snippet-file></snippet><paragraph/></document>

answers.content:
Hi Dhruba, wow good catch! No, that was not intended; we should not be always labelling the left one as preferred if label != 0.5. I have updated the starter code accordingly in the starter code .zip file in the original HW 3 release post (#468) like so (the label to ordering mapping is based on how we generated the data):
if label == 0.5:
        labels = ["Equally Preferred", "Equally Preferred"]
elif label == 1:
    labels = ["Not Preferred", "Preferred"]
else:
    labels = ["Preferred", "Not Preferred"]
Note: Since this is an error on our end, if you already submitted/wrote an answer using the old code, we will accept those answers as well as long as you explain your reasoning. However, the labels may make more sense if you use the newest code.

title: RL + LLM & Robotics Session For Projects
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph><bold>UPDATE:</bold> Based on the responses we will have the session on <bold>Tuesday 4-5 PM</bold> both in-person & zoom, and also release the recording. Though, I still recommend attending in-person if you can. Moreover, for students interested in Friday session, you can attend the 6-7 PM OH session </paragraph><paragraph> Will update the room no. where session takes place shortly</paragraph><paragraph>---</paragraph><paragraph>Hi everyone,<break/>We’re planning a conduct a session for students working on LLM or robotics (simulation) projects. The goal is to walk through commonly used RL+LLM and robotics libraries, their setup, run a small training example, and discuss best practices.<break/><break/><bold>RL + LLM</bold></paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>TRL</bold> (Hugging Face) – RL fine-tuning for LLMs, aligned with HF tutorials and examples</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Prime-RL</bold> – Better suited for environment-based setups (e.g., coding tasks, tool use, tool calling)</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Tinker</bold></paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><bold>Robotics</bold></paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>Mujoco / Issac-Gym</bold> – Setting up simulation for robotics task</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><strike>For in person we’re considering:</strike></paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph><bold>Friday (02/13)</bold>: 5-6 PM ❌</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph><bold>Tuesday (02/16)</bold>: 4-5 PM ✅</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><break/>If you’re interested, please reply with:</paragraph><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph>Whether you’d attend</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Your preferred time slot</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph><break/>If there’s enough interest, we’ll confirm the final time and share logistics.</paragraph></document>
document:
UPDATE: Based on the responses we will have the session on Tuesday 4-5 PM both in-person & zoom, and also release the recording. Though, I still recommend attending in-person if you can. Moreover, for students interested in Friday session, you can attend the 6-7 PM OH session 

 Will update the room no. where session takes place shortly

---

Hi everyone,
We’re planning a conduct a session for students working on LLM or robotics (simulation) projects. The goal is to walk through commonly used RL+LLM and robotics libraries, their setup, run a small training example, and discuss best practices.

RL + LLM

title: Homework 4 1.2 Automation script
content:
Hi all,I decided to write this short script to automate PPO hopper for question 1.2 so that I can leave my computer running overnight to complete the necessary runs.
import subprocess

def run_jobs(seeds = [1], additional_args = [""]):
    for s in seeds:
        for a in additional_args:
            cmd = f"python3 ppo_hopper.py --seed {s} {a}"
            print(f"Running: {cmd}")
            subprocess.run(cmd, shell = True)
    print("Process Completed.")
    
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_jobs(seeds = [1,2,3], additional_args = ["--early-termination", ""])
Hope some of you find this helpful.

comments.content:
Update: I just realized that I need to leave my computer on overnight again... Here's an updated script with problems 2 and 3 runs:
import subprocess
import argparse

def exec(cmd):
    print(f"Running: {cmd}")
    subprocess.run(cmd, shell = True)
    
def run_ppo(seeds = [1], additional_args = [""]):
    for s in seeds:
        for a in additional_args:
            exec(f"python3 ppo_hopper.py --seed {s} {a}")
    print("Process Completed.")
    
def run_rlhf(seeds = [1]):
    for s in seeds:
        exec(f"python3 run_rlhf.py --seed {s}")
    print("Process Completed.")
    
def run_dpo(seeds = [1], algorithms = ["dpo"]):
    for a in algorithms:
        for s in seeds:
            exec(f"python3 run_dpo.py --seed {s} --algo {a}")
    print("Process Completed.")    
    
if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--ppo", help="[Q1] Run PPO", action="store_true")
    parser.add_argument("--rlhf", help="[Q2] Run RLHF", action="store_true")
    parser.add_argument("--dpo", help="[Q3] Run DPO", action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    
    if not(args.ppo or args.rlhf or args.dpo):
        print("Please tell me which script(s) to run.")
        parser.print_help()
    
    if args.ppo:
        run_ppo(seeds = [1,2,3], additional_args = ["--early-termination", ""])
    if args.rlhf:
        run_rlhf(seeds = [1,2,3])
    if args.dpo:
        run_dpo(seeds = [1,2,3], algorithms=["sft", "dpo"])

title: Cross Entropy in HW3 Q2 Coding
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For update in run_rlhf I see hints for using functional.cross_entropy (both the documentation and stack overflow). However, since self.net already guarantees outputs in [0,1], would it make more sense to use binary_cross_entropy_with_logits (<link href="https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits.html">https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits.html</link>)? This expects logits as input, rather than re-applying a softmax to already normalized outputs?</paragraph></document>
document: For update in run_rlhf I see hints for using functional.cross_entropy (both the documentation and stack overflow). However, since self.net already guarantees outputs in [0,1], would it make more sense to use binary_cross_entropy_with_logits (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.binary_cross_entropy_with_logits.html)? This expects logits as input, rather than re-applying a softmax to already normalized outputs?

answers.content: self.net returns reward values between 0 and 1 to prevent trajectory sums from exploding, but they are not probabilities. This is why we still need to apply a softmax. I believe using either cross_entropy or binary_cross_entropy_with_logits would be correct since we are in the binary case, but both of them automatically apply a probability normalization (i.e. using softmax or sigmoid) to trajectory logits / rewards.
answers.document: self.net returns reward values between 0 and 1 to prevent trajectory sums from exploding, but they are not probabilities. This is why we still need to apply a softmax. I believe using either cross_entropy or binary_cross_entropy_with_logits would be correct since we are in the binary case, but both of them automatically apply a probability normalization (i.e. using softmax or sigmoid) to trajectory logits / rewards.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Ah I see! I applied softmax to normalize reward values between 0 and 1 in self.net. I think this still works but just making sure this could also be a viable approach?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Ah I see! I applied softmax to normalize reward values between 0 and 1 in self.net. I think this still works but just making sure this could also be a viable approach?

comments.content: If you are using something like nn.Softmax(), that might not work because it expects a vector of logits, but for our self.net the last layer should take in a scalar.
comments.document: If you are using something like nn.Softmax(), that might not work because it expects a vector of logits, but for our self.net the last layer should take in a scalar.

title: Question regarding Problem 1(c), 3rd statement in the midterm exam
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>"In a tabular domain, given a finite set of on-policy samples from π, run MC policy evaluation updates an infinite number of times over the finite set. Then the resulting estimate VπMC​ will have the minimum MSE possible relative to the true Vπ​."<break/><break/>My instinct is that MC is unbiased but high variance, and MSE is calculated to be variance, but the relationship to true Vπ​ is questionable.<break/><break/>The calcualtion:<break/> <break/>MSE=Bias2+Variance, since it's infinite sample, converge with no bias.<break/>then MSE = Var(V^), and we can prove this is minimized among unbiased estimators too. <break/><break/>Consider Vπ= E[V^],</paragraph><paragraph>then MSE= Var(V^)=E[V^2]−(E[V^])2 = E[V^2] -Vπ^2</paragraph><paragraph>So we cannot derive the MSE directly from Vπ​ unless we also know E[V^2], right? In that case, I think the relationship between MSE and the true Vπ​ is questionable.</paragraph><paragraph>Therefore, I thought I could be correct if the 3rd statement was not selected.</paragraph><paragraph>I am not sure whether this understanding is correct or not, as this confused me during the exam and when I saw the grade.<break/><break/>Thank you!</paragraph></document>
document:
"In a tabular domain, given a finite set of on-policy samples from π, run MC policy evaluation updates an infinite number of times over the finite set. Then the resulting estimate VπMC​ will have the minimum MSE possible relative to the true Vπ​."

My instinct is that MC is unbiased but high variance, and MSE is calculated to be variance, but the relationship to true Vπ​ is questionable.

The calcualtion:
 
MSE=Bias2+Variance, since it's infinite sample, converge with no bias.
then MSE = Var(V^), and we can prove this is minimized among unbiased estimators too. 

Consider Vπ= E[V^],

then MSE= Var(V^)=E[V^2]−(E[V^])2 = E[V^2] -Vπ^2

So we cannot derive the MSE directly from Vπ​ unless we also know E[V^2], right? In that case, I think the relationship between MSE and the true Vπ​ is questionable.

Therefore, I thought I could be correct if the 3rd statement was not selected.

I am not sure whether this understanding is correct or not, as this confused me during the exam and when I saw the grade.

Thank you!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I would think in a simpler way, I would just write the MSE in terms of an estimator V^.</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>MSE = E[(V^ - V^\pi)^2] where V^\pi is the true value and just minimize this over all possible V^. You would get the minimum MSE at V\pi = E[V^], which is what MC sampling does.<break/><break/>We do not need to derive the MSE value as we do not know the ground truth but we can try to minimize it. </paragraph><paragraph> </paragraph></document>
answers.document:
I would think in a simpler way, I would just write the MSE in terms of an estimator V^.



MSE = E[(V^ - V^\pi)^2] where V^\pi is the true value and just minimize this over all possible V^. You would get the minimum MSE at V\pi = E[V^], which is what MC sampling does.

We do not need to derive the MSE value as we do not know the ground truth but we can try to minimize it.

title: MidTerm 1a
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For MidTerm 1a, Option c is false because the number of time steps may not be large enough to find an optimal policy, right?</paragraph><paragraph>..and why is option b correct because can an optimal policy not be stochastic?</paragraph></document>
document:
For MidTerm 1a, Option c is false because the number of time steps may not be large enough to find an optimal policy, right?

..and why is option b correct because can an optimal policy not be stochastic?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For option c: The time steps is not referring to the computation the optimal policy, it's referring to whether the optimal policy can choose different actions at a single state s depending on how many time steps are remaining in the finite horizon. Problem 1 from HW 1 had a good example of a non-stationary optimal policy, I think.</paragraph><paragraph/><paragraph>For option b: I believe there can be stochastic policies that are optimal in some MDPs, but there is always a deterministic policy that is at least as good. The expected return under the optimal policy from an action a at state s is $Q^*(s, a)$, so you can always just choose the argmax over a to maximize expected return.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
For option c: The time steps is not referring to the computation the optimal policy, it's referring to whether the optimal policy can choose different actions at a single state s depending on how many time steps are remaining in the finite horizon. Problem 1 from HW 1 had a good example of a non-stationary optimal policy, I think.



For option b: I believe there can be stochastic policies that are optimal in some MDPs, but there is always a deterministic policy that is at least as good. The expected return under the optimal policy from an action a at state s is $Q^*(s, a)$, so you can always just choose the argmax over a to maximize expected return.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For option c, in my statement, the assumption is that the optimal policy is always stationary, meaning learning has stopped and convergence is achieved but in case of MDP with finite horizon,  there may not be enough time steps to achieve convergence.</paragraph><paragraph>I agree with your answer for b. Thank you!</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
For option c, in my statement, the assumption is that the optimal policy is always stationary, meaning learning has stopped and convergence is achieved but in case of MDP with finite horizon,  there may not be enough time steps to achieve convergence.

I agree with your answer for b. Thank you!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>"Stationary" in this context just means the policy is independent of the trajectory time step, it doesn't relate to policy learning at all. The key point is that the optimal action to take from a given state might be dependent on the time step in the trajectory (e.g. near the end of the horizon you can optimize for short term gain), hence the optimal policy can be non-stationary.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: "Stationary" in this context just means the policy is independent of the trajectory time step, it doesn't relate to policy learning at all. The key point is that the optimal action to take from a given state might be dependent on the time step in the trajectory (e.g. near the end of the horizon you can optimize for short term gain), hence the optimal policy can be non-stationary.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The assumption that optimal policy for finite horizon MDPs would be stationary is incorrect, but for infinite horizon MDPs this assumption is correct. <break/><break/>For a short finite horizon MDP, if you have lot of experience or data then you can converge to an optimal policy which would depend on the number of time steps left in the horizon. </paragraph></document>
comments.document:
The assumption that optimal policy for finite horizon MDPs would be stationary is incorrect, but for infinite horizon MDPs this assumption is correct. 

For a short finite horizon MDP, if you have lot of experience or data then you can converge to an optimal policy which would depend on the number of time steps left in the horizon.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Rohan (and Dhurba), that can only mean that in finite horizon, in most cases, we are not converging and then we can have many different (optimal) policies depending on the current time step.</paragraph><paragraph>These are good insights that I learned just now.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
comments.document:
Rohan (and Dhurba), that can only mean that in finite horizon, in most cases, we are not converging and then we can have many different (optimal) policies depending on the current time step.

These are good insights that I learned just now.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I would say that you would converge to an optimal policy (depending on how much experience you have and what algorithm you are using) and you would converge to have a optimal unique V(s, h) where h is the number of remaining time steps in the MDP. So for the number of time steps remaining you would have an unique optimal value function that can be obtained from that state (and hence can have different optimal policies). </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
comments.document: I would say that you would converge to an optimal policy (depending on how much experience you have and what algorithm you are using) and you would converge to have a optimal unique V(s, h) where h is the number of remaining time steps in the MDP. So for the number of time steps remaining you would have an unique optimal value function that can be obtained from that state (and hence can have different optimal policies).

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>So, for Finite MDP, can I say, $V(s,h)$ makes more sense than $V(s)$?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: So, for Finite MDP, can I say, $V(s,h)$ makes more sense than $V(s)$?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Yes, that is correct. </paragraph></document>
comments.document: Yes, that is correct.

title: HW 3 Question 4.b
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Could you please give some tips into how to solve this? I don't think I see the basis equations for solving this question in Lec 9 slides. </paragraph></document>
document: Could you please give some tips into how to solve this? I don't think I see the basis equations for solving this question in Lec 9 slides.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Not a TA, but wanted to provide some insights.</paragraph><paragraph>For me, the lecture equations were only useful in giving a basic intuition, as they are a bit different from Hoeffding's inequality defined by the problem. I mainly worked from the equations given in the problem instead of those we went over in lecture.</paragraph><paragraph>As for how to solve the problem, I started by manipulating Hoeffding's inequality to get the probability where </paragraph><math>|\widehat r_a - \overline r_a | \leq  \sqrt{\frac{\log(2/\delta)}{2n_e}}</math><paragraph>for some arm a. Then I tried to find the relationship between epsilon and the right hand side of the inequality.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Not a TA, but wanted to provide some insights.

For me, the lecture equations were only useful in giving a basic intuition, as they are a bit different from Hoeffding's inequality defined by the problem. I mainly worked from the equations given in the problem instead of those we went over in lecture.

As for how to solve the problem, I started by manipulating Hoeffding's inequality to get the probability where 

$$|\widehat r_a - \overline r_a | \leq  \sqrt{\frac{\log(2/\delta)}{2n_e}}$$

for some arm a. Then I tried to find the relationship between epsilon and the right hand side of the inequality.

title: Autograder's tests precision in HW3
content:
Hello, is it possible to review the tests' precision to make it less dependent on implementation? I believe my code works correctly and passes all the tests except for this one with delta of 6e-4: 
test_reward_model_update (test_all.TestAll) (0/3)
Test Failed: 0.6857872009277344 != 0.6863778829574585 within 6 places (0.0005906820297241211 difference)
I had to tweak my implementation a lot to make other tests in this suite work as well. Are these strict thresholds really necessary?
document:
Hello, is it possible to review the tests' precision to make it less dependent on implementation? I believe my code works correctly and passes all the tests except for this one with delta of 6e-4:  

test_reward_model_update (test_all.TestAll) (0/3)
Test Failed: 0.6857872009277344 != 0.6863778829574585 within 6 places (0.0005906820297241211 difference)


I had to tweak my implementation a lot to make other tests in this suite work as well. Are these strict thresholds really necessary?

answers.content: Hey, not sure if this helps, but I was also failing this test with a slight accuracy difference initially, and it turns out there actually was a subtle bug in my implementation (in my update step). After fixing that, all the tests were passing.
answers.document: Hey, not sure if this helps, but I was also failing this test with a slight accuracy difference initially, and it turns out there actually was a subtle bug in my implementation (in my update step). After fixing that, all the tests were passing.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks, what kind of bug? I can imagine that, for example, different approaches to handling equally preferred cases can lead to slightly different outcomes. Is this the correct direction?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks, what kind of bug? I can imagine that, for example, different approaches to handling equally preferred cases can lead to slightly different outcomes. Is this the correct direction?

comments.content: For me, it had to do with how I was handling rewards and labels before passing them into F.cross_entropy. I didn't explicitly account for the equally preferred case.
comments.document: For me, it had to do with how I was handling rewards and labels before passing them into F.cross_entropy. I didn't explicitly account for the equally preferred case.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thank you! I was able to make it pass now!<break/>As I suspected, the problem was in the way I treated the equally preferred case. Initially, I treated the labels as class indices, casting the equally preferred case to one of the classes. It looks like the intended way is to treat the labels as class probabilities.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Thank you! I was able to make it pass now!
As I suspected, the problem was in the way I treated the equally preferred case. Initially, I treated the labels as class indices, casting the equally preferred case to one of the classes. It looks like the intended way is to treat the labels as class probabilities.

title: HW 3 3.1 d
content: Hi! I noticed the inconsistency in how we plot the RLHF and DPO returns:RLHF:--rl-steps = 1,000,000--eval-period = 1,0001,000,000 / 1,000 = 1,000 points on X-axisDPO:--num-dpo-steps = 20,000--eval-period = 2,00020,000 / 2,000 = 10 points on X-axisThese are the numbers I get if I run code using the terminal commands provided in the handout. RLHF runs 50x more gradient steps than DPO. This makes the comparison somewhat unfair to DPO based on the plots since it's possible DPO would improve further with more steps. Are we supposed to run and report the plots/comparison using the code provided? Or should we adjust the arguments so that rl-steps = num-dpo-steps and eval-period rlhf =eval-period dpo ?
document:
Hi! I noticed the inconsistency in how we plot the RLHF and DPO returns:

RLHF:

--rl-steps = 1,000,000

--eval-period = 1,000

1,000,000 / 1,000 = 1,000 points on X-axis

DPO:

--num-dpo-steps = 20,000

--eval-period = 2,000

20,000 / 2,000 = 10 points on X-axis

These are the numbers I get if I run code using the terminal commands provided in the handout. RLHF runs 50x more gradient steps than DPO. This makes the comparison somewhat unfair to DPO based on the plots since it's possible DPO would improve further with more steps. 

Are we supposed to run and report the plots/comparison using the code provided? Or should we adjust the arguments so that rl-steps = num-dpo-steps and eval-period rlhf =eval-period dpo ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi, thanks for the catch! You're right.</paragraph><paragraph>In the starter code, the two “step” arguments are not the same unit:</paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph>run_rlhf.py --rl-steps is PPO environment timesteps (online interaction budget).</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>run_dpo.py --num-dpo-steps is DPO optimizer update steps (offline training updates).</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>So the default DPO vs RLHF plot is not a strict budget-matched apples-to-apples benchmark. For grading consistency, please run the required commands as written in the handout, then in your discussion, explicitly note this fairness caveat when comparing methods.</paragraph><paragraph>If you want to include extra analysis, you’re welcome to add an optional matched-budget experiment (e.g., fixed wall-clock or adjusted step budgets), but that is supplementary and not required for the main plot.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Hi, thanks for the catch! You're right.

In the starter code, the two “step” arguments are not the same unit:

run_rlhf.py --rl-steps is PPO environment timesteps (online interaction budget).

run_dpo.py --num-dpo-steps is DPO optimizer update steps (offline training updates).

So the default DPO vs RLHF plot is not a strict budget-matched apples-to-apples benchmark. For grading consistency, please run the required commands as written in the handout, then in your discussion, explicitly note this fairness caveat when comparing methods.

If you want to include extra analysis, you’re welcome to add an optional matched-budget experiment (e.g., fixed wall-clock or adjusted step budgets), but that is supplementary and not required for the main plot.

title: HW3 Environment nix devenv that compiles properly
content:
{ pkgs, ... }:

{
  packages = with pkgs; [
    git
    # You can keep this for running C binaries if you want,
    # but the python build will ignore it due to the commented-out env vars above.
    mujoco

    # Build tools required for compiling MuJoCo bindings
    cmake
    ninja
    pkg-config
    gcc

    # Common libraries required by Python binary wheels (numpy, etc.)
    zlib          # Fixes: libz.so.1 not found
    stdenv.cc.cc  # Fixes: libstdc++.so.6 issues (often needed later)

    # REMOVED: eigen and pybind11
    # 'uv' will automatically download the correct versions of these
    # Python build dependencies during the installation process.

    # X11 only (no wayland packages!)
    xorg.libX11
    xorg.libXcursor
    xorg.libXi
    xorg.libXinerama
    xorg.libXrandr
    libGL

    wayland
    wayland-scanner
    wayland-protocols
    libxkbcommon
  ];

  languages.python = {
    enable = true;
    version = "3.11";

    uv = {
      enable = true;
      sync.enable = true;
    };
  };

  # Ensure OpenGL/X11 libraries are discoverable at runtime.
  # Without this, you might get "cannot open shared object file" errors later.
  env.LD_LIBRARY_PATH = pkgs.lib.makeLibraryPath (with pkgs; [
    libGL
    xorg.libX11
    xorg.libXcursor
    xorg.libXi
    xorg.libXinerama
    xorg.libXrandr
  ]);
}

title: HW 3 3.1e
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Is it expected that the render command errors because there is no SFT checkpoint saved after training? I think I implemented the SFT update correctly, and my code is passing all the autograder tests. But because the SFT policy is always worse than the pretrained checkpoint, the save best checkpoint logic never triggers and no SFT models are saved during training. Does this suggest a bug in my update logic (SFT model should be better than the pretrained checkpoint) or is this expected and I should just change the training code to save the checkpoint regardless of if it is better?</paragraph></document>

title: Last minute: RL Seminar today on Qualia Optimization: Exploring Mathematical Formulations of AI Experience
content: <document version="2.0"><heading level="1">Qualia Optimization: Exploring Mathematical Formulations of AI Experience</heading><paragraph>Philip Thomas</paragraph><paragraph>Associate Professor, University of Massachusetts</paragraph><paragraph>Thursday, February 19, 2026 at 4:00 PM • Packard 202</paragraph><heading level="3">Abstract</heading><paragraph>This talk explores the speculative question: what if current or future AI systems have qualia, such as pain or pleasure? It does so by assuming that AI systems might someday possess qualia—and that the quality of these subjective experiences should be considered alongside performance metrics. Concrete mathematical problem settings, inspired by reinforcement learning formulations and theories from philosophy of mind, are then proposed and initial approaches and properties are presented. This talk serves as an introduction to the paper “Qualia Optimization”.</paragraph><heading level="3">Bio</heading><paragraph>Philip Thomas is an associate professor and the Doctoral Program Director at the Manning College of Information and Computer Sciences at the University of Massachusetts. He has published at top venues, including Science, is president and co-founder of the Reinforcement Learning Conference, and has testified before the US House Committee on Financial Services’ Task Force on Artificial Intelligence. He received his PhD from the University of Massachusetts, where he studied reinforcement learning (RL) under the supervision of Prof. Andy Barto. His research initially focused on policy gradient algorithms, off-policy evaluation, confidence intervals, high-confidence safety guarantees for RL, and fairness; more recently, it has shifted toward the intersection of RL and the philosophy of mind.</paragraph></document>
document:
Qualia Optimization: Exploring Mathematical Formulations of AI Experience

Philip Thomas

Associate Professor, University of Massachusetts

Thursday, February 19, 2026 at 4:00 PM • Packard 202

Abstract

This talk explores the speculative question: what if current or future AI systems have qualia, such as pain or pleasure? It does so by assuming that AI systems might someday possess qualia—and that the quality of these subjective experiences should be considered alongside performance metrics. Concrete mathematical problem settings, inspired by reinforcement learning formulations and theories from philosophy of mind, are then proposed and initial approaches and properties are presented. This talk serves as an introduction to the paper “Qualia Optimization”.

Bio

Philip Thomas is an associate professor and the Doctoral Program Director at the Manning College of Information and Computer Sciences at the University of Massachusetts. He has published at top venues, including Science, is president and co-founder of the Reinforcement Learning Conference, and has testified before the US House Committee on Financial Services’ Task Force on Artificial Intelligence. He received his PhD from the University of Massachusetts, where he studied reinforcement learning (RL) under the supervision of Prof. Andy Barto. His research initially focused on policy gradient algorithms, off-policy evaluation, confidence intervals, high-confidence safety guarantees for RL, and fairness; more recently, it has shifted toward the intersection of RL and the philosophy of mind.

title: Guest Lecture Google DeepMind Shane Gu Wed: World of World Modeling
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I'm happy to share that we will have a guest lecture by Shane Gu next Wednesday! </paragraph><paragraph>Title: World of World Modeling</paragraph><paragraph>Abstract:</paragraph><paragraph>World models are classically associated with action-conditioned video models, but their true scope extends far beyond. This lecture bridges the gap between classic reinforcement learning concepts and modern generative paradigms. We start by contrasting world modeling in symbolic and physical intelligences. We then examine the core algorithmic and representational tensions that define them: shooting versus collocation, explicit simulators versus implicit world models like goal-conditioned value functions, and passive versus active modeling. Finally, through the lens of predictability maximization and the empowerment equation, we reframe the ultimate objective of autonomous agents. The frontier is no longer just world modeling, but world empowering.</paragraph><paragraph>Bio:</paragraph><paragraph>Shixiang Shane Gu is a Senior Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind, currently focusing on the Gemini Thinking team. Previously, he led the Multilinguality team for Gemini Post-Training served as a senior researcher on the ChatGPT team and led Japan market entry at OpenAI, and was a founding member of Google Brain Robotics team. Shane holds a PhD in Machine Learning from the University of Cambridge and the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, supervised by Richard E. Turner, Zoubin Ghahramani, and Bernhard Schölkopf. He earned his B.ASc. in Engineering Science from the University of Toronto under the supervision of Geoffrey E. Hinton. His research has been recognized with a Best Paper Award at CoRL 2019. A Japan-born Chinese Canadian, Shane is fluent in three languages.</paragraph></document>
document:
I'm happy to share that we will have a guest lecture by Shane Gu next Wednesday! 

Title: World of World Modeling

Abstract:

World models are classically associated with action-conditioned video models, but their true scope extends far beyond. This lecture bridges the gap between classic reinforcement learning concepts and modern generative paradigms. We start by contrasting world modeling in symbolic and physical intelligences. We then examine the core algorithmic and representational tensions that define them: shooting versus collocation, explicit simulators versus implicit world models like goal-conditioned value functions, and passive versus active modeling. Finally, through the lens of predictability maximization and the empowerment equation, we reframe the ultimate objective of autonomous agents. The frontier is no longer just world modeling, but world empowering.

Bio:

Shixiang Shane Gu is a Senior Staff Research Scientist at Google DeepMind, currently focusing on the Gemini Thinking team. Previously, he led the Multilinguality team for Gemini Post-Training served as a senior researcher on the ChatGPT team and led Japan market entry at OpenAI, and was a founding member of Google Brain Robotics team. Shane holds a PhD in Machine Learning from the University of Cambridge and the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, supervised by Richard E. Turner, Zoubin Ghahramani, and Bernhard Schölkopf. He earned his B.ASc. in Engineering Science from the University of Toronto under the supervision of Geoffrey E. Hinton. His research has been recognized with a Best Paper Award at CoRL 2019. A Japan-born Chinese Canadian, Shane is fluent in three languages.

comments.content: To your first question, Not really! To the second, As you see, the definition of target lets you specify probability of individual classes. content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph><bold>First question:</bold> Is trying to upper bound the complement of </paragraph><math>\Pr\left(\exists a\in A\ s.t.\ Event\ holds\right)</math><paragraph>i.e.</paragraph><math>1-\Pr\left(\forall a\in A,\ Event\ holds\right)</math><paragraph>the right approach to this problem, or is there a better way to go about this?</paragraph><paragraph><bold>Second question:</bold></paragraph><paragraph>Does HW3 4A require that the the events {Bad event holds for arm A_i} be independent between arms? If so, do we need to prove this is true, or does multi-armed bandit setting guarantee that this will be independent?</paragraph></document>
document:
First question: Is trying to upper bound the complement of 

$$\Pr\left(\exists a\in A\ s.t.\ Event\ holds\right)$$

i.e.

$$1-\Pr\left(\forall a\in A,\ Event\ holds\right)$$

the right approach to this problem, or is there a better way to go about this?

Second question:

Does HW3 4A require that the the events {Bad event holds for arm A_i} be independent between arms? If so, do we need to prove this is true, or does multi-armed bandit setting guarantee that this will be independent?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>That is a way to approach the problem, although there is a more direct way as well. Also note that the proper complement should also negate the event, i.e., "Event doesn't hold".<break/><break/>I don't believe part a requires that the events are independent, however, in this case, it is the case that the events are independent, given that our specific algorithm pulls each arm $n_e$ times regardless of any information we receive about the reward of any other arm.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
That is a way to approach the problem, although there is a more direct way as well. Also note that the proper complement should also negate the event, i.e., "Event doesn't hold".

I don't believe part a requires that the events are independent, however, in this case, it is the case that the events are independent, given that our specific algorithm pulls each arm $n_e$ times regardless of any information we receive about the reward of any other arm.

title: Lecture 11 Poll
content: I had a question about understanding the poll about deterministic rewards bandit problem. In upper confidence bound for deterministic reward, the regret will be sub-linear with probability one. But for upper confidence bounds to work, I have to pull each arm once. If I pull the arm once, then it would mean I have the correct rewards for each arm. I would just pull the arm which returns maximum rewards then. Why is the regret sub-linear in that case?
document:
I had a question about understanding the poll about deterministic rewards bandit problem. 

In upper confidence bound for deterministic reward, the regret will be sub-linear with probability one. But for upper confidence bounds to work, I have to pull each arm once. If I pull the arm once, then it would mean I have the correct rewards for each arm. I would just pull the arm which returns maximum rewards then. Why is the regret sub-linear in that case?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>It seems that you are assuming that, after pulling each arm once, UCB will immediately and forever exploit the empirically best arm with no further exploration. While it is true that in a deterministic multi-arm bandit problem, one pull reveals the exact reward of each arm (since there is no noise), UCB still includes an exploration bonus, so it may pull suboptimal arms a few additional times before their confidence bonuses shrink sufficiently. A suboptimal arm $i$ can only be selected while its upper confidence bound satisfies $\mu_i​+\sqrt{2\log \frac{t}{n_i}​} \geq \mu^*$, where $\mu^*$ denotes the reward of the best arm. This implies that $n_i \leq ​2\log t / \Delta_i^2$​. Because $n_i$​ increases only when arm $i$ is chosen, this condition can hold for at most $O(\log T)$ pulls of that arm up to horizon $T$. Thus, the total regret is $O(\log T)$ (with probability one), which is sublinear in $T$.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: It seems that you are assuming that, after pulling each arm once, UCB will immediately and forever exploit the empirically best arm with no further exploration. While it is true that in a deterministic multi-arm bandit problem, one pull reveals the exact reward of each arm (since there is no noise), UCB still includes an exploration bonus, so it may pull suboptimal arms a few additional times before their confidence bonuses shrink sufficiently. A suboptimal arm $i$ can only be selected while its upper confidence bound satisfies $\mu_i​+\sqrt{2\log \frac{t}{n_i}​} \geq \mu^*$, where $\mu^*$ denotes the reward of the best arm. This implies that $n_i \leq ​2\log t / \Delta_i^2$​. Because $n_i$​ increases only when arm $i$ is chosen, this condition can hold for at most $O(\log T)$ pulls of that arm up to horizon $T$. Thus, the total regret is $O(\log T)$ (with probability one), which is sublinear in $T$.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I see. But here in UCB, there was a variance parameter in the sqrt term and we in general assume the variance is 1. So, UCB assumes the rewards are stochastic with variance = 1 even for deterministic rewards for the regret to be sub-linear.<break/><break/>This brings me to the follow-up question - We are trying to estimate the mean reward of each arm assuming the variance equals to 1. Is that a correct estimate to go with. Given we don't have a knowledge about the distribution of the reward, how do we assume the variance on the reward?</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
I see. But here in UCB, there was a variance parameter in the sqrt term and we in general assume the variance is 1. So, UCB assumes the rewards are stochastic with variance = 1 even for deterministic rewards for the regret to be sub-linear.

This brings me to the follow-up question - We are trying to estimate the mean reward of each arm assuming the variance equals to 1. Is that a correct estimate to go with. Given we don't have a knowledge about the distribution of the reward, how do we assume the variance on the reward?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>You are right that in the context of this question we generally refer to the UCB1 algorithm, asuming that the reward is 1-sub-Gaussian. </paragraph><paragraph>As for your question "Is that a correct estimate to go with": the concentration assumption is present mainly because we can derive sub-linear regret bounds under this assumption. UCB1 does not estimate or plug in a variance when selecting actions — it simply adds an exploration bonus to the empirical mean. In this question, the rewards are deterministic (variance zero), which actually makes the empirical mean exact after one pull, so the assumption is automatically satisfied and the regret guarantee still holds.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
You are right that in the context of this question we generally refer to the UCB1 algorithm, asuming that the reward is 1-sub-Gaussian. 

As for your question "Is that a correct estimate to go with": the concentration assumption is present mainly because we can derive sub-linear regret bounds under this assumption. UCB1 does not estimate or plug in a variance when selecting actions — it simply adds an exploration bonus to the empirical mean. In this question, the rewards are deterministic (variance zero), which actually makes the empirical mean exact after one pull, so the assumption is automatically satisfied and the regret guarantee still holds.

title: union bound on UCB
content: shouldn't this only occur with probability 1 - delta', not 1 - delta?
document: shouldn't this only occur with probability 1 - delta', not 1 - delta?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The easiest way to understand is to know that $1 - \delta$ is the probability of being outside of the UCB [Lecture 9, slide 42], $\delta$ would be the probability of staying inside the UCB. Now, $\delta' = \frac{\delta}{2}$ would split the original probability into 2, on either side of the mean (Imagine the probability graph as a squeezed in bell curve). A vertical line through the middle (mean) will divide the UCB into 2 parts - the left side and the right side.</paragraph></document>
answers.document: The easiest way to understand is to know that $1 - \delta$ is the probability of being outside of the UCB [Lecture 9, slide 42], $\delta$ would be the probability of staying inside the UCB. Now, $\delta' = \frac{\delta}{2}$ would split the original probability into 2, on either side of the mean (Imagine the probability graph as a squeezed in bell curve). A vertical line through the middle (mean) will divide the UCB into 2 parts - the left side and the right side.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think I explained it backward (corrected it), but I will let a TA explain it.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: I think I explained it backward (corrected it), but I will let a TA explain it.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Actually, the slide is correct! Here is the breakdown of why we use $1 - \delta$:</paragraph><list style="ordered"><list-item><paragraph>We want our estimate to be accurate (the 'good event') with high probability. We define this confidence level as $1 - \delta$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Two-Sided Error: Because the formula uses an absolute value $|\mu - \hat{\mu}|$, we are bounding error in two directions (overestimating and underestimating).</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Union Bound: If we let the failure probability for one side be $\delta'$, the total failure probability for both sides is $2\delta'$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>To ensure our total failure probability is $\delta$, we set $2\delta' = \delta$ (or $\delta' = \delta/2$).</paragraph></list-item></list><paragraph>When you plug $\delta/2$ into the $\log(1/\delta')$ term of a standard Hoeffding bound, it becomes $\log(2/\delta)$. So, the formula already 'accounts' for the union bound, allowing us to say the result holds with probability $1 - \delta$.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Actually, the slide is correct! Here is the breakdown of why we use $1 - \delta$:

We want our estimate to be accurate (the 'good event') with high probability. We define this confidence level as $1 - \delta$.

Two-Sided Error: Because the formula uses an absolute value $|\mu - \hat{\mu}|$, we are bounding error in two directions (overestimating and underestimating).

Union Bound: If we let the failure probability for one side be $\delta'$, the total failure probability for both sides is $2\delta'$.

To ensure our total failure probability is $\delta$, we set $2\delta' = \delta$ (or $\delta' = \delta/2$).

When you plug $\delta/2$ into the $\log(1/\delta')$ term of a standard Hoeffding bound, it becomes $\log(2/\delta)$. So, the formula already 'accounts' for the union bound, allowing us to say the result holds with probability $1 - \delta$.

title: Question about MABs
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Given that MABs are a simplification of MDPs and they have a optimism under uncertainty framework, is there an equivalent framework for MDPs - then why not just use that? Is it a tractability thing? Struggling to see why exactly MABs are useful still since everything that exists for MABs exists for MDPs.</paragraph><paragraph>Edit1: assuming MABs are useful, how do we know when to use MABs vs when to use MDPs?</paragraph></document>
document:
Given that MABs are a simplification of MDPs and they have a optimism under uncertainty framework, is there an equivalent framework for MDPs - then why not just use that? Is it a tractability thing? Struggling to see why exactly MABs are useful still since everything that exists for MABs exists for MDPs.

Edit1: assuming MABs are useful, how do we know when to use MABs vs when to use MDPs?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Great question! Indeed, there are “optimism under uncertainty” (OFU) style algorithms for MDPs (see, e.g., <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/1510.08906">https://arxiv.org/pdf/1510.08906</link> and references therein). </paragraph><paragraph>The reason we still study/use MABs is that they are a drastically simpler special case. You can view a stochastic K-armed bandit as an MDP with a single state (or trivial self-loop dynamics): there is no state evolution to learn or plan over, so the only challenge is balancing exploration vs exploitation to identify the best action.</paragraph><paragraph>In a general MDP, (1) actions affect <italic>future states</italic> and therefore future rewards, so you must plan over a horizon, and (2) the optimal policy is a mapping from states to actions, so you potentially need to learn good behavior across many state–action pairs. So while you can reduce a MAB to an MDP, doing so is usually unnecessary and inefficient—bandit algorithms exploit the extra structure to be simpler and more sample-efficient.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Great question! Indeed, there are “optimism under uncertainty” (OFU) style algorithms for MDPs (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/pdf/1510.08906 and references therein). 

The reason we still study/use MABs is that they are a drastically simpler special case. You can view a stochastic K-armed bandit as an MDP with a single state (or trivial self-loop dynamics): there is no state evolution to learn or plan over, so the only challenge is balancing exploration vs exploitation to identify the best action.

In a general MDP, (1) actions affect future states and therefore future rewards, so you must plan over a horizon, and (2) the optimal policy is a mapping from states to actions, so you potentially need to learn good behavior across many state–action pairs. So while you can reduce a MAB to an MDP, doing so is usually unnecessary and inefficient—bandit algorithms exploit the extra structure to be simpler and more sample-efficient.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for this response! Could you please provide intuition around when I would use one framework v/s the other? </paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks for this response! Could you please provide intuition around when I would use one framework v/s the other?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph/><paragraph>Bandit models are best when you can try an action and then see its payoff quickly, without that choice substantially changing what options or outcomes look like later. For example, suppose you want to treat an acute eye-infection. Each drug is an “arm,” and within a week you observe a simple reward (infection cleared = 1, not cleared = 0), so you can rapidly learn which arm tends to work best while still occasionally exploring alternatives. The key assumption is that today’s choice mainly affects today’s reward, not the structure of tomorrow’s decision. <break/><break/>In contrast, chronic conditions like a long-term back injury involve state changes over time—pain level, mobility, side effects, adherence, and recovery trajectory—so the outcome of one treatment affects what will be effective or (even possible) later. That kind of sequential dependence fits an MDP, where you model states, actions, transition dynamics, and long-run cumulative reward rather than a one-shot, immediately observed payoff. In short, bandits are “learn the best option from fast feedback,” while MDPs are “plan and learn in a system where actions shape the future.”</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Bandit models are best when you can try an action and then see its payoff quickly, without that choice substantially changing what options or outcomes look like later. For example, suppose you want to treat an acute eye-infection. Each drug is an “arm,” and within a week you observe a simple reward (infection cleared = 1, not cleared = 0), so you can rapidly learn which arm tends to work best while still occasionally exploring alternatives. The key assumption is that today’s choice mainly affects today’s reward, not the structure of tomorrow’s decision. 

In contrast, chronic conditions like a long-term back injury involve state changes over time—pain level, mobility, side effects, adherence, and recovery trajectory—so the outcome of one treatment affects what will be effective or (even possible) later. That kind of sequential dependence fits an MDP, where you model states, actions, transition dynamics, and long-run cumulative reward rather than a one-shot, immediately observed payoff. In short, bandits are “learn the best option from fast feedback,” while MDPs are “plan and learn in a system where actions shape the future.”

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks! Once you wrote this down, it made sense to me that there are a set of decision making frameworks each with different assumptions of what's stationary/observable and the correct tool will naturally emerge from an understanding of the problem itself.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks! Once you wrote this down, it made sense to me that there are a set of decision making frameworks each with different assumptions of what's stationary/observable and the correct tool will naturally emerge from an understanding of the problem itself.

title: Question/Fun Observation about Bellman Optimal Equation
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Just noticed that the relationship b/w the bellman optimal operator and optimal value function<break/><break/>BV = V</paragraph><paragraph>looks pretty similar to the eigenvector equation</paragraph><paragraph>Ax = Lambda * x</paragraph><paragraph>I guess now I'm starting to see why there is only one optimal value function and why BOE has a max operator where just like eigenvectors represent the dominant directionality of data, BOE governs the dominant pathway/evolution through the environment. <break/><break/>Is this true? Are there some resources to explore this analogy further?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Just noticed that the relationship b/w the bellman optimal operator and optimal value function

BV = V

looks pretty similar to the eigenvector equation

Ax = Lambda * x

I guess now I'm starting to see why there is only one optimal value function and why BOE has a max operator where just like eigenvectors represent the dominant directionality of data, BOE governs the dominant pathway/evolution through the environment. 

Is this true? Are there some resources to explore this analogy further?

answers.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>My 2 cents: BOE shrinks the distance between points until the fixed point is reached. After which, it doesn't matter how many times you apply BOE to the point, it shrinks no further. On the other hand, eigenvectors scale a vector by a scalar, while keeping the direction fixed. </paragraph><paragraph>So, I don't see a correlation.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
My 2 cents: BOE shrinks the distance between points until the fixed point is reached. After which, it doesn't matter how many times you apply BOE to the point, it shrinks no further. On the other hand, eigenvectors scale a vector by a scalar, while keeping the direction fixed. 

So, I don't see a correlation.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for the response! I really appreciate it :-)</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thanks for the response! I really appreciate it :-)

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Here's a couple of my thoughts on it: </paragraph><list style="unordered"><list-item><paragraph>The Fixed-Point: You are right that both involve finding an "invariant" vector. The Bellman optimality equation is a fixed-point equation: $V^* = B^*V^*$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Stationary Distributions: Your "dominant directionality" intuition applies to Markov Chains. The stationary distribution of a MDP under a <italic>fixed</italic> policy is the eigenvector of the transition matrix with eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Linearity: The eigenvector equation $Ax = \lambda x$ describes linear maps. The Bellman operator $B^*$ is nonlinear because of the $\max_a$ operation. This nonlinearity is what makes RL more complex than basic linear algebra.</paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Uniqueness: In linear algebra, uniqueness usually comes from the rank of the matrix. In RL, the reason there is only one $V^*$ is the Fixed-Point Theorem. In discounted MDPs ($\gamma \in (0, 1)$), $B^*$ is a contraction mapping, meaning it pulls all possible value functions toward a single unique point.</paragraph></list-item></list></document>
answers.document:
Here's a couple of my thoughts on it: 

The Fixed-Point: You are right that both involve finding an "invariant" vector. The Bellman optimality equation is a fixed-point equation: $V^* = B^*V^*$.

Stationary Distributions: Your "dominant directionality" intuition applies to Markov Chains. The stationary distribution of a MDP under a fixed policy is the eigenvector of the transition matrix with eigenvalue $\lambda = 1$.

Linearity: The eigenvector equation $Ax = \lambda x$ describes linear maps. The Bellman operator $B^*$ is nonlinear because of the $\max_a$ operation. This nonlinearity is what makes RL more complex than basic linear algebra.

Uniqueness: In linear algebra, uniqueness usually comes from the rank of the matrix. In RL, the reason there is only one $V^*$ is the Fixed-Point Theorem. In discounted MDPs ($\gamma \in (0, 1)$), $B^*$ is a contraction mapping, meaning it pulls all possible value functions toward a single unique point.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thanks for taking time to try and answer this question, it's only after going into rabbit holes I realized that this question is not easy and the connections across the fields are highly mathematical and seem to manifest in narrow cases/constructions:(quite frankly mostly beyond my grasp right now). Some papers I found:<break/>- Solving something called average reward learning(I don't think it's part of CS234?) using the eigenvector equation<break/>- The HJB equation(which seems to be the continuous form of the Bellman equation) can be solved for some kind of control in the max-plus space using the eigenvector equation in that space. This paper kinda addresses the linearity thing though, since linearity/non-linearity can only be defined relative to the underlying space.<break/><break/>So it wasn't as connected as I thought. Thanks again!<break/></paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Thanks for taking time to try and answer this question, it's only after going into rabbit holes I realized that this question is not easy and the connections across the fields are highly mathematical and seem to manifest in narrow cases/constructions:(quite frankly mostly beyond my grasp right now). Some papers I found:
- Solving something called average reward learning(I don't think it's part of CS234?) using the eigenvector equation
- The HJB equation(which seems to be the continuous form of the Bellman equation) can be solved for some kind of control in the max-plus space using the eigenvector equation in that space. This paper kinda addresses the linearity thing though, since linearity/non-linearity can only be defined relative to the underlying space.

So it wasn't as connected as I thought. Thanks again!

content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I would like to clarify whether the use of AI tools is permitted for improving wording and grammar in project write-ups, strictly for language polishing rather than content generation.</paragraph><paragraph><italic>Below is the AI policy for assignments, which may not directly apply to the project report:</italic> <break/><italic>"You are permitted to use generative AI tools such as Gemini, GPT-4 and Co-Pilot in the same way that human collaboration is considered acceptable: you are not allowed to directly ask for solutions or copy code, and <underline>you should indicate if you have used generative AI tools</underline>. Similar to human collaboration help, you are ultimately responsible and accountable for your own work. We may check students' homework, exams and projects to enforce this policy."</italic></paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
I would like to clarify whether the use of AI tools is permitted for improving wording and grammar in project write-ups, strictly for language polishing rather than content generation.

Below is the AI policy for assignments, which may not directly apply to the project report: 
"You are permitted to use generative AI tools such as Gemini, GPT-4 and Co-Pilot in the same way that human collaboration is considered acceptable: you are not allowed to directly ask for solutions or copy code, and you should indicate if you have used generative AI tools. Similar to human collaboration help, you are ultimately responsible and accountable for your own work. We may check students' homework, exams and projects to enforce this policy."

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi there! Yes, the same AI policy applies to the project report. Using tools like Gemini or GPT-4 for language polishing, grammar checks, and improving flow is permitted, provided you follow these two core requirements: 1) indicating usage of generative AI tools, and 2) you are ultimately responsible for the technical accuracy of the report. AI can sometimes introduce "hallucinations" or subtle factual errors.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document: Hi there! Yes, the same AI policy applies to the project report. Using tools like Gemini or GPT-4 for language polishing, grammar checks, and improving flow is permitted, provided you follow these two core requirements: 1) indicating usage of generative AI tools, and 2) you are ultimately responsible for the technical accuracy of the report. AI can sometimes introduce "hallucinations" or subtle factual errors.

The quiz will be held in class next Wednesday. SCPD students will follow the same procedure as for the midterm.
