title: Remind me how the Bellman equation works
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Remind me how the Bellman equation works.</paragraph></document>
document: Remind me how the Bellman equation works.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The Bellman equation expresses the relationship between the value of a state and the values of subsequent states. For a state-value function $V(s)$, it is written as:<break/> $$V(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s') \mid S_t = s\right]$$<break/> Here, $R_{t+1}$ is the immediate reward, $\gamma$ is the discount factor, and $s'$ is the next state. This equation captures the idea that the value of a state depends on the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the resulting state.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
The Bellman equation expresses the relationship between the value of a state and the values of subsequent states. For a state-value function $V(s)$, it is written as:
 $$V(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[R_{t+1} + \gamma V(s') \mid S_t = s\right]$$
 Here, $R_{t+1}$ is the immediate reward, $\gamma$ is the discount factor, and $s'$ is the next state. This equation captures the idea that the value of a state depends on the expected immediate reward plus the discounted value of the resulting state.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The Bellman equation expresses the value of a state $s$ as the expected sum of the immediate reward $R$ and the discounted value of the next state $s'$, weighted by the discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$. For a value function $V(s)$, it is written as:<break/> $$V(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R + \gamma V(s') \right]$$<break/> This equation captures the trade-off between immediate rewards and long-term future rewards, forming the basis for many reinforcement learning algorithms.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
The Bellman equation expresses the value of a state $s$ as the expected sum of the immediate reward $R$ and the discounted value of the next state $s'$, weighted by the discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1]$. For a value function $V(s)$, it is written as:
 $$V(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[ R + \gamma V(s') \right]$$
 This equation captures the trade-off between immediate rewards and long-term future rewards, forming the basis for many reinforcement learning algorithms.

title: New support for forum conceptual questions
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi everyone, </paragraph><paragraph>This Ed Forum provides a place to get support, but it is still hard for us to provide support as quickly as would be desirable, particularly to those in other time zones. We are piloting a test LLM we created based on (IRB-approved) anonymized forum data from a past offering of CS234. It is designed to support answering conceptual questions. For any responses to any other question types, its answer is not reliable (though for non-conceptual questions it should default to responding that a course staff will follow up).</paragraph><paragraph>Each day a staff member will still review all LLM-provided responses and explicitly confirm if the LLM-provided responses are correct or add any details. The staff will also respond to all non-conceptual questions, such as logistics questions. <bold>In all situations, the CA / staff will serve as the final word on any logistics / grading / exceptions.</bold></paragraph><paragraph>In our tests of an early version of system, our LLM-provided responses were ranked as better than generic LLM-provided responses to conceptual CS234 forum questions.</paragraph><paragraph>As always, we welcome any feedback.</paragraph><paragraph>We hope that this provides additional timely support to you in these last few weeks of the course!</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
Hi everyone, 

This Ed Forum provides a place to get support, but it is still hard for us to provide support as quickly as would be desirable, particularly to those in other time zones. We are piloting a test LLM we created based on (IRB-approved) anonymized forum data from a past offering of CS234. It is designed to support answering conceptual questions. For any responses to any other question types, its answer is not reliable (though for non-conceptual questions it should default to responding that a course staff will follow up).

Each day a staff member will still review all LLM-provided responses and explicitly confirm if the LLM-provided responses are correct or add any details. The staff will also respond to all non-conceptual questions, such as logistics questions. In all situations, the CA / staff will serve as the final word on any logistics / grading / exceptions.

In our tests of an early version of system, our LLM-provided responses were ranked as better than generic LLM-provided responses to conceptual CS234 forum questions.

As always, we welcome any feedback.

We hope that this provides additional timely support to you in these last few weeks of the course!

title: Lecture 9: poll question
content: For the second choice, both RLHF and DPO learn from the preference data right ? They can only be as better as the Pair wise preferences we feed via the Trajectory sampling correct ? Wondering if they can better than the best preference data we feed in?
document: For the second choice, both RLHF and DPO learn from the preference data right ? They can only be as better as the Pair wise preferences we feed via the Trajectory sampling correct ? Wondering if they can better than the best preference data we feed in?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>[**Edit: we removed the bot response since it was incorrect]</paragraph><paragraph>Seems like the bot is answering a different question based on the "quality" of the preferences rather than the quality of the examples that the preferences are comparing. (So the bot is wrong in terms of the actual question).<break/><break/>Yes, RLHF and DPO both "learn" from the preference data, but for example, RLHF learns an explicit reward model. We can then use this reward model to do any RL method to update our policy. So, it may be the case that we can learn a lot about the reward model of the environment, even from relatively suboptimal examples. Then we use that learned reward model to train really good policies.<break/><break/>DPO aims to do something similar to RLHF, but skips the explicit reward model creation and RL portion to just update the policy function directly in a closed-form way. So it can also technically update the policy to be better than any examples displayed in the preference set.<break/><break/>You should compare this to imitation learning or behavior cloning methods, which try to learn a policy directly by mimicking the data.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
[**Edit: we removed the bot response since it was incorrect]

Seems like the bot is answering a different question based on the "quality" of the preferences rather than the quality of the examples that the preferences are comparing. (So the bot is wrong in terms of the actual question).

Yes, RLHF and DPO both "learn" from the preference data, but for example, RLHF learns an explicit reward model. We can then use this reward model to do any RL method to update our policy. So, it may be the case that we can learn a lot about the reward model of the environment, even from relatively suboptimal examples. Then we use that learned reward model to train really good policies.

DPO aims to do something similar to RLHF, but skips the explicit reward model creation and RL portion to just update the policy function directly in a closed-form way. So it can also technically update the policy to be better than any examples displayed in the preference set.

You should compare this to imitation learning or behavior cloning methods, which try to learn a policy directly by mimicking the data.

title: Give me some intuition on Bayesian Bandits [Testing the bot]
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Explain Bayesian Bandits in layman's terms!</paragraph></document>
document: Explain Bayesian Bandits in layman's terms!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Seems like the bot's responses are reasonable.<break/><break/>> What's the math behind exploration?<break/><break/>It depends on the specific algorithm! The one we covered the most in class and the one you should be familiar with is Thompson sampling, where we "explore" by choosing an arm based off of sampling from our posterior distribution. But at a high level, our probability of choosing an arm should match the probability (under our current belief) that the arm is optimal. Since this gives a stochastic policy, we are still able to "explore" across different arms.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Seems like the bot's responses are reasonable.

> What's the math behind exploration?

It depends on the specific algorithm! The one we covered the most in class and the one you should be familiar with is Thompson sampling, where we "explore" by choosing an arm based off of sampling from our posterior distribution. But at a high level, our probability of choosing an arm should match the probability (under our current belief) that the arm is optimal. Since this gives a stochastic policy, we are still able to "explore" across different arms.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Thank you!</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Thank you!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Bayesian Bandits refer to a class of algorithms for solving multi-armed bandit problems, where the goal is to maximize cumulative rewards by balancing exploration (trying new options) and exploitation (choosing known good options).</paragraph><paragraph>In layman’s terms, imagine you’re at a casino with multiple slot machines (each a "bandit"). Each machine has an unknown probability of paying out. A Bayesian Bandit approach starts with a belief (prior) about each machine’s payout rate. After each pull, it updates these beliefs (posterior distributions) based on observed outcomes. The algorithm then selects the next machine to pull by sampling from these updated beliefs, favoring machines that appear promising while still occasionally testing others to refine estimates.</paragraph><paragraph>This method systematically trades off immediate rewards for long-term learning, adapting its strategy as more data is gathered.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Bayesian Bandits refer to a class of algorithms for solving multi-armed bandit problems, where the goal is to maximize cumulative rewards by balancing exploration (trying new options) and exploitation (choosing known good options).

In layman’s terms, imagine you’re at a casino with multiple slot machines (each a "bandit"). Each machine has an unknown probability of paying out. A Bayesian Bandit approach starts with a belief (prior) about each machine’s payout rate. After each pull, it updates these beliefs (posterior distributions) based on observed outcomes. The algorithm then selects the next machine to pull by sampling from these updated beliefs, favoring machines that appear promising while still occasionally testing others to refine estimates.

This method systematically trades off immediate rewards for long-term learning, adapting its strategy as more data is gathered.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Does it have a potential to ignore a good arm?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Does it have a potential to ignore a good arm?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Bayesian Bandits balance exploration and exploitation by maintaining probabilistic beliefs about each arm's reward potential. While they prioritize arms with higher estimated rewards, the sampling mechanism inherently includes some exploration. However, if an arm's true reward is initially underestimated due to random sampling variability, it could be selected less frequently than optimal in the short term. Over time, as posterior distributions update with more data, the algorithm should converge to the better-performing arms. The risk of ignoring a good arm depends on the prior assumptions and the rate of posterior updates, but the framework is designed to mitigate this risk through continued exploration.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Bayesian Bandits balance exploration and exploitation by maintaining probabilistic beliefs about each arm's reward potential. While they prioritize arms with higher estimated rewards, the sampling mechanism inherently includes some exploration. However, if an arm's true reward is initially underestimated due to random sampling variability, it could be selected less frequently than optimal in the short term. Over time, as posterior distributions update with more data, the algorithm should converge to the better-performing arms. The risk of ignoring a good arm depends on the prior assumptions and the rate of posterior updates, but the framework is designed to mitigate this risk through continued exploration.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Give me a concrete example about how it mitigates this risk.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Give me a concrete example about how it mitigates this risk.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Consider an online advertising scenario where multiple ads (arms) compete for user clicks. Suppose a new ad (a "good arm") initially receives mixed responses—some users click, others don’t. A Bayesian Bandit starts with a prior belief about each ad’s click-through rate. After each interaction, it updates the posterior distribution for each ad.</paragraph><paragraph>Even if the new ad’s estimated performance lags behind others due to random variability, the algorithm continues to sample it occasionally (exploration) because its posterior distribution still allows for high potential reward. Over time, as more data accumulates, the posterior narrows, and the ad’s true performance is recognized. This prevents the algorithm from prematurely dismissing a good arm due to early noise.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
Consider an online advertising scenario where multiple ads (arms) compete for user clicks. Suppose a new ad (a "good arm") initially receives mixed responses—some users click, others don’t. A Bayesian Bandit starts with a prior belief about each ad’s click-through rate. After each interaction, it updates the posterior distribution for each ad.

Even if the new ad’s estimated performance lags behind others due to random variability, the algorithm continues to sample it occasionally (exploration) because its posterior distribution still allows for high potential reward. Over time, as more data accumulates, the posterior narrows, and the ad’s true performance is recognized. This prevents the algorithm from prematurely dismissing a good arm due to early noise.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>What's the math behind exploration?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: What's the math behind exploration?

title: HW3 DPO code : NN - Mean and Std
content: When we designated half of network output as mean and half as standard deviation, I am assuming the choice wasn't arbitrary but by design but how does the neural network know these are means and std? The DPO loss function tries to minimize the mean difference between log probs and it plays into tuning the NN params., so I am not able to connect the dots.
document: When we designated half of network output as mean and half as standard deviation, I am assuming the choice wasn't arbitrary but by design but how does the neural network know these are means and std? The DPO loss function tries to minimize the mean difference between log probs and it plays into tuning the NN params., so I am not able to connect the dots.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The neural network does not inherently distinguish between mean and standard deviation outputs; this is explicitly defined by the model's architecture and the loss function. When the output is split into two parts (e.g., mean and log_std), the loss function uses these components to compute the objective (e.g., minimizing the difference between predicted and target log probabilities). During training, gradients from this loss propagate back through the network, adjusting parameters to refine both the mean and standard deviation outputs. The design choice reflects the problem's requirement to model distributional outputs, with the loss function ensuring alignment between the network's predictions and the desired behavior.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The neural network does not inherently distinguish between mean and standard deviation outputs; this is explicitly defined by the model's architecture and the loss function. When the output is split into two parts (e.g., mean and log_std), the loss function uses these components to compute the objective (e.g., minimizing the difference between predicted and target log probabilities). During training, gradients from this loss propagate back through the network, adjusting parameters to refine both the mean and standard deviation outputs. The design choice reflects the problem's requirement to model distributional outputs, with the loss function ensuring alignment between the network's predictions and the desired behavior.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>and the loop back is because we use mean and standard deviation to select actions, right?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: and the loop back is because we use mean and standard deviation to select actions, right?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The loop in the algorithm is indeed related to the use of mean and standard deviation parameters in action selection. When a policy outputs these parameters (e.g., for a Gaussian distribution), actions are sampled from the corresponding distribution during execution. The training loop iteratively updates these parameters based on the outcomes of the actions taken, refining the policy to improve performance. The connection between the parameters and action selection is enforced through the policy's design and the optimization objective, which directly incorporates the distributional outputs into the learning process.</paragraph></document>

When a policy outputs these parameters (e.g., for a Gaussian distribution), actions are sampled from the corresponding distribution during execution. The training loop iteratively updates these parameters based on the outcomes of the actions taken, refining the policy to improve performance. title: Question regarding MCTS Parallelization
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi, I had a question as Prof.Brunskill mentioned that one of the major advantages of MCTS is that it is highly parallelizable.</paragraph><paragraph>However, I'm struggling to understand how parallelization doesn't break the exploration mechanics of the UCT/PUCT formula.</paragraph><paragraph>My understanding of the  selection step (max(Q+U)) is that it heavily relies on the U  value shrinking sequentially. Every time a node is visited, its visit count N goes up, its U value drops, and the algorithm is eventually forced to explore a different branch.</paragraph><paragraph>If we run K rollouts completely in parallel, won't all the parallel threads look at the root node at the exact same time, calculate the exact same Q+U values, and all traverse down the exact same branch? If the visit counts aren't updated sequentially, it seems like parallelizing the rollouts would completely destroy the exploration aspect of the algorithm.</paragraph></document>
document:
Hi, I had a question as Prof.Brunskill mentioned that one of the major advantages of MCTS is that it is highly parallelizable.

However, I'm struggling to understand how parallelization doesn't break the exploration mechanics of the UCT/PUCT formula.

My understanding of the  selection step (max(Q+U)) is that it heavily relies on the U  value shrinking sequentially. Every time a node is visited, its visit count N goes up, its U value drops, and the algorithm is eventually forced to explore a different branch.

If we run K rollouts completely in parallel, won't all the parallel threads look at the root node at the exact same time, calculate the exact same Q+U values, and all traverse down the exact same branch? If the visit counts aren't updated sequentially, it seems like parallelizing the rollouts would completely destroy the exploration aspect of the algorithm.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Since the bot is not replying, I found this paper that's relevant to your question - <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.11755">https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.11755</link>. </paragraph><paragraph>It proposes subtracting a virtual loss from a node's value while traversing the node, making this node less attractive for the other threads to traverse, thereby, encouraging exploration. This virtual loss is added back during backpropagation. A side effect is that exploitation suffers.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Since the bot is not replying, I found this paper that's relevant to your question - https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.11755. 

It proposes subtracting a virtual loss from a node's value while traversing the node, making this node less attractive for the other threads to traverse, thereby, encouraging exploration. This virtual loss is added back during backpropagation. A side effect is that exploitation suffers.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The parallelization of MCTS relies on managing shared state updates carefully. While the UCT/PUCT formula depends on visit counts to balance exploration and exploitation, parallel implementations typically use synchronization mechanisms (e.g., locks, atomic operations) to ensure consistent updates to node visit counts. This prevents race conditions and maintains the exploration term's effectiveness. However, naive parallelization (e.g., no synchronization) could indeed lead to redundant exploration or reduced diversity in paths taken. Advanced techniques, such as asynchronous updates or distributed tree storage, are often employed to preserve the algorithm's exploration mechanics while enabling parallelism. The key challenge lies in coordinating updates without compromising the statistical validity of the visit counts.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The parallelization of MCTS relies on managing shared state updates carefully. While the UCT/PUCT formula depends on visit counts to balance exploration and exploitation, parallel implementations typically use synchronization mechanisms (e.g., locks, atomic operations) to ensure consistent updates to node visit counts. This prevents race conditions and maintains the exploration term's effectiveness. However, naive parallelization (e.g., no synchronization) could indeed lead to redundant exploration or reduced diversity in paths taken. Advanced techniques, such as asynchronous updates or distributed tree storage, are often employed to preserve the algorithm's exploration mechanics while enabling parallelism. The key challenge lies in coordinating updates without compromising the statistical validity of the visit counts.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>No but my question is not about synchronization mechanisms on the software side. If we have two threads that are calculating max(Q+U) and thread 1 calculates that and picks action A and starts a simulation, then thread 2 would calculate max(Q+U) and then still pick the same action A  as thread 1 since thread 1 hasn't finished its simulation yet which means no exploration will actually happen if the K rollouts are parallelized. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
comments.document: No but my question is not about synchronization mechanisms on the software side. If we have two threads that are calculating max(Q+U) and thread 1 calculates that and picks action A and starts a simulation, then thread 2 would calculate max(Q+U) and then still pick the same action A  as thread 1 since thread 1 hasn't finished its simulation yet which means no exploration will actually happen if the K rollouts are parallelized.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In parallel MCTS, if multiple threads independently compute $\max(Q + U)$ without accounting for concurrent updates, they may indeed select the same action before any visit counts are updated. This could reduce exploration diversity, as the exploration term $U$ depends on shared state (visit counts) that hasn't been modified yet. However, the design of parallel implementations often incorporates mechanisms to mitigate this, such as asynchronous updates, prioritizing different branches through heuristic diversification, or allowing partial overlap of tree expansions. The challenge lies in balancing parallel efficiency with maintaining the statistical validity of exploration.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: In parallel MCTS, if multiple threads independently compute $\max(Q + U)$ without accounting for concurrent updates, they may indeed select the same action before any visit counts are updated. This could reduce exploration diversity, as the exploration term $U$ depends on shared state (visit counts) that hasn't been modified yet. However, the design of parallel implementations often incorporates mechanisms to mitigate this, such as asynchronous updates, prioritizing different branches through heuristic diversification, or allowing partial overlap of tree expansions. The challenge lies in balancing parallel efficiency with maintaining the statistical validity of exploration.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Explain virtual loss technique as described here - https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.11755</paragraph></document>
comments.document: Explain virtual loss technique as described here - https://arxiv.org/pdf/1810.11755

title: Practice quiz Q12
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Hi! I was wondering what might be an example that would satisfy question 12 answer b in the practice quiz?</paragraph></document>
document: Hi! I was wondering what might be an example that would satisfy question 12 answer b in the practice quiz?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>For pessimism: Imagine Arm A has a true mean of 0.9 (but you've only pulled it once and got a 0) and Arm B has a true mean of 0.1 (and you've pulled it once and got a 1). A pessimistic agent will look at the low lower bound of the high-potential Arm A and never pull it again.So the agent stops exploring and gets stuck on a sub-optimal arm forever. It misses the optimal reward by a constant amount every single step, so the total regret grows at a linear rate ($O(T)$).</paragraph></document>
answers.document: For pessimism: Imagine Arm A has a true mean of 0.9 (but you've only pulled it once and got a 0) and Arm B has a true mean of 0.1 (and you've pulled it once and got a 1). A pessimistic agent will look at the low lower bound of the high-potential Arm A and never pull it again.So the agent stops exploring and gets stuck on a sub-optimal arm forever. It misses the optimal reward by a constant amount every single step, so the total regret grows at a linear rate ($O(T)$).

Hi everyone!

Our poster session is next Wednesday, March 11th, 3:00 - 5:00 PM, at AT&T lawn behind the Gates Building. You may arrive as early as 2:45 PM to set up your poster, and early arrival is encouraged.

title: RLHF - KL term
content: <document version="2.0"><list style="number"><list-item><paragraph>In RLHF, why is it important to stay close to the original model? </paragraph></list-item><list-item><paragraph>Why are we using policy as a proxy for model?</paragraph></list-item></list></document>
document:
In RLHF, why is it important to stay close to the original model? 

Why are we using policy as a proxy for model?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>+1 to bot responses! </paragraph></document>
answers.document: +1 to bot responses!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In RLHF, the KL divergence term in the loss function ensures the policy remains close to the original (pretrained) model. This prevents drastic changes that could destabilize training or erase useful behaviors learned during pretraining. The original model serves as a reliable baseline, and staying close to it helps maintain robustness and generalization.</paragraph><paragraph>The policy is used as a proxy for the original model during fine-tuning because it is the version being optimized. By aligning the policy with the original model (via the KL term), we ensure that improvements from reward modeling do not come at the cost of losing the original model's foundational capabilities. This balance is critical for stable and effective training.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
In RLHF, the KL divergence term in the loss function ensures the policy remains close to the original (pretrained) model. This prevents drastic changes that could destabilize training or erase useful behaviors learned during pretraining. The original model serves as a reliable baseline, and staying close to it helps maintain robustness and generalization.

The policy is used as a proxy for the original model during fine-tuning because it is the version being optimized. By aligning the policy with the original model (via the KL term), we ensure that improvements from reward modeling do not come at the cost of losing the original model's foundational capabilities. This balance is critical for stable and effective training.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>To your second point, a policy is like a strategy to navigate in a given model, not the model itself. Why is it an effective proxy for a model?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: To your second point, a policy is like a strategy to navigate in a given model, not the model itself. Why is it an effective proxy for a model?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In RLHF, the policy serves as a proxy for the original model because it represents the model's behavior under the new objective (e.g., reward maximization). The original model's parameters define a baseline distribution over actions (or outputs), and the policy is a modified version of this distribution, constrained by the KL divergence term to avoid large deviations. This ensures the policy retains the original model's foundational capabilities while adapting to the reward signal. The KL term effectively regularizes the policy to stay close to the original model's behavior, preventing instability or loss of general knowledge during fine-tuning.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: In RLHF, the policy serves as a proxy for the original model because it represents the model's behavior under the new objective (e.g., reward maximization). The original model's parameters define a baseline distribution over actions (or outputs), and the policy is a modified version of this distribution, constrained by the KL divergence term to avoid large deviations. This ensures the policy retains the original model's foundational capabilities while adapting to the reward signal. The KL term effectively regularizes the policy to stay close to the original model's behavior, preventing instability or loss of general knowledge during fine-tuning.

title: Lecture14 : Check your understanding.
content: Can i know like why were the options 1 and 2 are false and 3 is true, isn't MCTS also has the Limitations like the Expectimax tree where the Size of tree scaling is limited by (|S||A|)^|H| ?
document: Can i know like why were the options 1 and 2 are false and 3 is true, isn't MCTS also has the Limitations like the Expectimax tree where the Size of tree scaling is limited by (|S||A|)^|H| ?

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>+1 to bot response!</paragraph></document>
answers.document: +1 to bot response!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The selection of MCTS depends on the trade-off between state/action space sizes and horizon length. For option 1 (short horizon, small $|S|$, $|A|$), exhaustive methods like Expectimax are more efficient, making MCTS less suitable. For option 2 (long horizon, large $|A|$, small $|S|$), the large action space increases the branching factor, leading to infeasible tree growth despite small states. MCTS struggles here due to high exploration costs. For option 3 (long horizon, large $|S|$, small $|A|$), the tree's growth is constrained by the smaller action space, allowing MCTS to effectively reuse state information through sampling. The exponential scaling $(|S||A|)^{|H|}$ applies to exhaustive search, but MCTS mitigates this via heuristic sampling, making it viable when $|A|$ is small.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The selection of MCTS depends on the trade-off between state/action space sizes and horizon length. For option 1 (short horizon, small $|S|$, $|A|$), exhaustive methods like Expectimax are more efficient, making MCTS less suitable. For option 2 (long horizon, large $|A|$, small $|S|$), the large action space increases the branching factor, leading to infeasible tree growth despite small states. MCTS struggles here due to high exploration costs. For option 3 (long horizon, large $|S|$, small $|A|$), the tree's growth is constrained by the smaller action space, allowing MCTS to effectively reuse state information through sampling. The exponential scaling $(|S||A|)^{|H|}$ applies to exhaustive search, but MCTS mitigates this via heuristic sampling, making it viable when $|A|$ is small.

title: Error on Midterm 1C's Answer
content: Hello,I had a question about the answer key for part 1(c) of the MC/TD evaluation problem on the midterm. The solution lists option C as correct, which states that batch MC will have the minimum MSE possible relative to the true V^π. However, I believe there may be a discrepancy between this claim and what Sutton & Barto Chapter 6.3 describes.The textbook explicitly states that batch MC minimizes MSE "from the actual returns in the training set," but not relative to the true V^π since these are different quantities. Figure 6.2 from the book directly measures RMS error relative to the true V^π on the random walk task, and TD is strictly below MC across all episode counts. If batch MC truly achieved minimum MSE relative to the true V^π, it would be impossible for TD to beat it on that exact metric. Wouldn't this counterexample be sufficient to show the claim doesn't hold in general? I was very confused about this on the exam and therefore did not select part (C) as true. Any clarification would be greatly appreciated on this point.I saw another post on ed where a student brought up a similar concern (https://edstem.org/us/courses/91118/discussion/7671084) but I had some concerns about the TA's response. Specifically, the step where we minimize E[(V̂ - V^π)²] over all possible V̂ and arrive at V̂ = V^π treats V̂ as a free parameter we can set to any value. But V̂ isn't a free parameter so we can't just choose that V̂ = V^π. What MC actually gives us is an unbiased estimator, meaning E[V̂] = V^π. This makes the MSE equal to the variance: MSE = Bias² + Variance = 0 + Variance = Variance. Therefore MC achieves an MSE equal to its variance, which may be favorable among unbiased estimators, but being the best unbiased estimator does not mean being the best estimator overall. A biased estimator can achieve lower total MSE if its variance reduction more than compensates for the introduced bias. I believe that this is what TD(0) does.
document:
Hello,

I had a question about the answer key for part 1(c) of the MC/TD evaluation problem on the midterm. The solution lists option C as correct, which states that batch MC will have the minimum MSE possible relative to the true V^π. However, I believe there may be a discrepancy between this claim and what Sutton & Barto Chapter 6.3 describes.

The textbook explicitly states that batch MC minimizes MSE "from the actual returns in the training set," but not relative to the true V^π since these are different quantities. Figure 6.2 from the book directly measures RMS error relative to the true V^π on the random walk task, and TD is strictly below MC across all episode counts. If batch MC truly achieved minimum MSE relative to the true V^π, it would be impossible for TD to beat it on that exact metric. Wouldn't this counterexample be  sufficient to show the claim doesn't hold in general? I was very confused about this on the exam and therefore did not select part (C) as true. Any clarification would be greatly appreciated on this point.

I saw another post on ed where a student brought up a similar concern (https://edstem.org/us/courses/91118/discussion/7671084) but I had some concerns about the TA's response. Specifically, the step where we minimize E[(V̂ - V^π)²] over all possible V̂ and arrive at V̂ = V^π treats V̂ as a free parameter we can set to any value. But V̂ isn't a free parameter so we can't just choose that V̂ = V^π. What MC actually gives us is an unbiased estimator, meaning E[V̂] = V^π. This makes the MSE equal to the variance: MSE = Bias² + Variance = 0 + Variance = Variance. 

Therefore MC achieves an MSE equal to its variance, which may be favorable among unbiased estimators, but being the best unbiased estimator does not mean being the best estimator overall. A biased estimator can achieve lower total MSE if its variance reduction more than compensates for the introduced bias. I believe that this is what TD(0) does.

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>I think you're correct in your understanding! For this question option (1c), we ended up granting everyone credit due to this ambiguity. Originally, the question was intended to refer to the empirical MSE, but many students interpreted this as minimizing with respect to $V^\pi$. So, it was really how you define 'MSE' in this case, and so with your interpretation, C would not be selected. </paragraph></document>
answers.document: I think you're correct in your understanding! For this question option (1c), we ended up granting everyone credit due to this ambiguity. Originally, the question was intended to refer to the empirical MSE, but many students interpreted this as minimizing with respect to $V^\pi$. So, it was really how you define 'MSE' in this case, and so with your interpretation, C would not be selected.

title: Understand UCT Search Simulation
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>During Upper Confidence Tree (UCT) Search simulation, what policy are we using for simulation? Do we maintain and update UCB for all nodes in the simulated tree and always select the action with the highest UCB, or do we only do this for actions from the root node of the simulated tree? (I'm looking at page 12 of lecture 13 slides.)</paragraph></document>
document: During Upper Confidence Tree (UCT) Search simulation, what policy are we using for simulation? Do we maintain and update UCB for all nodes in the simulated tree and always select the action with the highest UCB, or do we only do this for actions from the root node of the simulated tree? (I'm looking at page 12 of lecture 13 slides.)

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>+1 to bot responses! </paragraph></document>
answers.document: +1 to bot responses!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>During UCT Search simulation, the policy used for simulation is typically a <bold>default policy</bold> (e.g., random or heuristic-based), not the UCB formula. The UCB values are maintained for all nodes in the tree, but they are <bold>updated only along the path from the root to the leaf node</bold> that was selected during the simulation. This update occurs after the simulation completes, based on the outcome of the simulated trajectory. The UCB formula itself is applied during the selection phase (to choose nodes for expansion), not during the simulation phase.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: During UCT Search simulation, the policy used for simulation is typically a default policy (e.g., random or heuristic-based), not the UCB formula. The UCB values are maintained for all nodes in the tree, but they are updated only along the path from the root to the leaf node that was selected during the simulation. This update occurs after the simulation completes, based on the outcome of the simulated trajectory. The UCB formula itself is applied during the selection phase (to choose nodes for expansion), not during the simulation phase.

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>When you say "The UCB values are maintained for all nodes in the tree, but they are updated only along the path from the root to the leaf node that was selected during the simulation." are you referring to the tree that was constructed during simulation which will be discarded after simulation, or the real tree representing real rollouts?</paragraph></document>
comments.document: When you say "The UCB values are maintained for all nodes in the tree, but they are updated only along the path from the root to the leaf node that was selected during the simulation." are you referring to the tree that was constructed during simulation which will be discarded after simulation, or the real tree representing real rollouts?

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The UCB values are maintained and updated in the <bold>real tree</bold> representing actual rollouts, not a temporary simulation tree. During backpropagation, the results of the simulation are propagated along the path from the leaf node back to the root in the <bold>persistent tree structure</bold> that accumulates information across all simulations. The tree is not discarded after each simulation; instead, it is incrementally built and refined over multiple iterations of the search process.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The UCB values are maintained and updated in the real tree representing actual rollouts, not a temporary simulation tree. During backpropagation, the results of the simulation are propagated along the path from the leaf node back to the root in the persistent tree structure that accumulates information across all simulations. The tree is not discarded after each simulation; instead, it is incrementally built and refined over multiple iterations of the search process.

title: Practice quiz question 7
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Why is it true that "The Bradley-Terry (reward) model is trained to minimize the negative log likelihood of the preference labels?"</paragraph></document>
document: Why is it true that "The Bradley-Terry (reward) model is trained to minimize the negative log likelihood of the preference labels?"

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>+1 to bot response!</paragraph></document>
answers.document: +1 to bot response!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The Bradley-Terry model is trained to minimize the negative log likelihood of the preference labels because this corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the observed preferences under the model's assumptions. For each pairwise comparison, the model assigns a probability that one item is preferred over another based on their parameters. Minimizing the negative log likelihood ensures the parameters are adjusted to make the observed preferences as probable as possible. This formulation directly aligns with the model's goal of learning a reward function that explains the preference data.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The Bradley-Terry model is trained to minimize the negative log likelihood of the preference labels because this corresponds to maximizing the likelihood of the observed preferences under the model's assumptions. For each pairwise comparison, the model assigns a probability that one item is preferred over another based on their parameters. Minimizing the negative log likelihood ensures the parameters are adjusted to make the observed preferences as probable as possible. This formulation directly aligns with the model's goal of learning a reward function that explains the preference data.

title: Lecture 11: Check you Understanding
content: Can i get some intuition on like why the option 1 is True and 2 is False ? I was confused on this part when i was trying to solve them. Thanks for help.
document: Can i get some intuition on like why the option 1 is True and 2 is False ? I was confused on this part when i was trying to solve them. Thanks for help.

answers.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>Bot's answer seems to be answering something different so it's incorrect.</paragraph><paragraph>I believe this is in the setting where rewards are delayed in comparison to when we would want to take the next action. Think of UCB as an example. As described in lecture, we would only update the upper confidence bounds when we actually get feedback about what user did. So before then, if we selected an action based on the highest upper confidence bound, and we didn't change anything before the time to take the next action came, we would still choose that same action as before. So the "deterministic" part is where we always make the same decision based on the current upper confidence bounds.</paragraph><paragraph>I believe Prof. Brunskill touched upon a similar example at the end of lecture on 2/18.</paragraph><paragraph>On the other hand, in the algorithm for Thompson sampling, there's inherent stochasticity in terms of sampling from the posterior. So before we even get feedback, we could plausibly choose a different action at the next timestep due to the randomness.</paragraph><paragraph>This can be desirable since, for example, we don’t want to keep trying the same action over and over. Imagine if all feedback was delayed for one day. Then in the worst case, for the entire day we would choose one action. Yes, we would learn a lot about that one action, but we might learn nothing about some other actions.</paragraph></document>
answers.document:
Bot's answer seems to be answering something different so it's incorrect.

I believe this is in the setting where rewards are delayed in comparison to when we would want to take the next action. Think of UCB as an example. As described in lecture, we would only update the upper confidence bounds when we actually get feedback about what user did. So before then, if we selected an action based on the highest upper confidence bound, and we didn't change anything before the time to take the next action came, we would still choose that same action as before. So the "deterministic" part is where we always make the same decision based on the current upper confidence bounds.

I believe Prof. Brunskill touched upon a similar example at the end of lecture on 2/18.

On the other hand, in the algorithm for Thompson sampling, there's inherent stochasticity in terms of sampling from the posterior. So before we even get feedback, we could plausibly choose a different action at the next timestep due to the randomness.

This can be desirable since, for example, we don’t want to keep trying the same action over and over. Imagine if all feedback was delayed for one day. Then in the worst case, for the entire day we would choose one action. Yes, we would learn a lot about that one action, but we might learn nothing about some other actions.

comments.content: <document version="1.0"><paragraph>To touch upon why 2 is false, we often have upper bounds on regret that allow for theoretical comparison, but it can be the case that algorithms perform better than their upper bounds in certain situations, and I believe it was mentioned at some point that Thompson sampling does appear to function quite well in real-world applications, especially given the stochastic behavior mentioned previously. </paragraph><paragraph>In addition, the regret bounds for Thompson sampling are not bad at all, and definitely sublinear.</paragraph></document>
comments.document:
To touch upon why 2 is false, we often have upper bounds on regret that allow for theoretical comparison, but it can be the case that algorithms perform better than their upper bounds in certain situations, and I believe it was mentioned at some point that Thompson sampling does appear to function quite well in real-world applications, especially given the stochastic behavior mentioned previously. 

In addition, the regret bounds for Thompson sampling are not bad at all, and definitely sublinear.

title: Guidance on depth/breadth of project?
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Is there any more information on how many algorithms/benchmarks/other metrics we need for a successful final project? Or pointers to past final projects? I'm unsure how much bandwidth to spend implementing additional algorithms vs. getting more results/contextualizing results from an algorithm we've already implemented. I recognize that the project is maybe intentionally open-ended, but any more detail on what success looks like would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document: Is there any more information on how many algorithms/benchmarks/other metrics we need for a successful final project? Or pointers to past final projects? I'm unsure how much bandwidth to spend implementing additional algorithms vs. getting more results/contextualizing results from an algorithm we've already implemented. I recognize that the project is maybe intentionally open-ended, but any more detail on what success looks like would be greatly appreciated. Thanks!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In general, we expect that your project should have thorough empirical results and involve a substantial coding component (assuming this is not a theory project). Correspondingly, I think you are pretty much free to choose whichever direction you find most interesting (e.g., implementing more algorithms vs diving deep on the existing algorithms). If you have specific concerns about whether what you've done qualifies as sufficient, I'd encourage you to ask your project CA directly. Hope this helps!</paragraph></document>
answers.document: In general, we expect that your project should have thorough empirical results and involve a substantial coding component (assuming this is not a theory project). Correspondingly, I think you are pretty much free to choose whichever direction you find most interesting (e.g., implementing more algorithms vs diving deep on the existing algorithms). If you have specific concerns about whether what you've done qualifies as sufficient, I'd encourage you to ask your project CA directly. Hope this helps!

comments.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The final project is designed to demonstrate your ability to apply reinforcement learning concepts to a well-defined problem. Success is measured by the clarity of your problem formulation, the rigor of your analysis, and the depth of your insights rather than the number of algorithms or benchmarks implemented. Focus on selecting a coherent research question, justifying your methodological choices, and thoroughly evaluating your approach against relevant baselines. For context, reviewing the project description and past course materials (if available) may provide further guidance on expectations.</paragraph></document>
comments.document: The final project is designed to demonstrate your ability to apply reinforcement learning concepts to a well-defined problem. Success is measured by the clarity of your problem formulation, the rigor of your analysis, and the depth of your insights rather than the number of algorithms or benchmarks implemented. answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>Keep the guidelines aside; it's much better to code it yourself. </paragraph><paragraph>Even after seemingly correct implementation of SAC, I am struggling to see my agent struggle to learn and I can't explain why, and therefore, I am debugging and debugging, with no success so far. Keep the guidelines aside; it's much better to code it yourself. 

Even after seemingly correct implementation of SAC, I am struggling to see my agent struggle to learn and I can't explain why, and therefore, I am debugging and debugging, with no success so far. But the process of debugging has made so many things clear about discrete vs continuous actor implementations of SAC (Categorical Vs Normal distributions)! that I can confidently say, that the real learning is happening right now. 

It's a different thing to code 3-5 lines per method in assignments and different to code the entire algorithm by yourself; and then you understand the importance of the theoretical nuances, such as $\alpha$, the entropy regularization factor, incorporating which, by the way hasn't fixed my code, so now I am thinking, it has something to do with the Replay Buffer. Anyways, Good Luck!

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>+1 to bot answers, and yes, it is a good learning process to code without AI!</paragraph></document>
answers.document: +1 to bot answers, and yes, it is a good learning process to code without AI!

title: Off policy learning and RL
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>In the lecture today, prof. Brunskill talked about off-policy learning and using REINFORCE to learn the optimal policy, which can then be used to train the agent. </paragraph><paragraph>I am wondering if we already know the optimal policy, what's the need to train the agent? We can simply tell the agent to use this policy. </paragraph><paragraph>Secondly, is it so that the true optimal policy can never be known (because we may never know the true behavior of the world) and the policy that we have learned using REINFORCE is optimal so far, in the sense that it maximizes the value of the initial state (but the true value is hidden (unexplored yet) and could be 100X of the max we have seen so far).</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
In the lecture today, prof. Brunskill talked about off-policy learning and using REINFORCE to learn the optimal policy, which can then be used to train the agent. 

I am wondering if we already know the optimal policy, what's the need to train the agent? We can simply tell the agent to use this policy. 

Secondly, is it so that the true optimal policy can never be known (because we may never know the true behavior of the world) and the policy that we have learned using REINFORCE is optimal so far, in the sense that it maximizes the value of the initial state (but the true value is hidden (unexplored yet) and could be 100X of the max we have seen so far).

answers.content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>The emphasis is on <italic>if we knew the optimal policy</italic>. If we actually knew it, we wouldn't train. We would just use it.<break/><break/>However, we don't usually know it ever, so algorithms like REINFORCE train a parameterized policy to approximate it. In most real environments, there's issues like the state space being too large, or the true environment dynamics never being fully known. REINFORCE then results in a policy that's typically optimal within the explored space, possibly a local optimum, but again, not guaranteed to be a true global optimum. </paragraph><paragraph/></document>
answers.document:
The emphasis is on if we knew the optimal policy. If we actually knew it, we wouldn't train. We would just use it.

However, we don't usually know it ever, so algorithms like REINFORCE train a parameterized policy to approximate it. In most real environments, there's issues like the state space being too large, or the true environment dynamics never being fully known. REINFORCE then results in a policy that's typically optimal within the explored space, possibly a local optimum, but again, not guaranteed to be a true global optimum.

title: Quiz Q3
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>re Q3 about the PDL. In lecture (also answer key), it was mentioned that (B) is the only correct answer. This is definitely correct</paragraph><math>J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}{s \sim d{\pi'}, a \sim \pi'} \left[ A^{\pi}(s, a) \right]</math><paragraph>However, I was wondering if we could also look at option (C) by swapping the roles of $\pi$ and $\pi'$. If we write the lemma for $J(\pi) - J(\pi')$, we get:</paragraph><paragraph>$$J(\pi) - J(\pi') = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}<italic>{s \sim d</italic>{\pi}, a \sim \pi} \left[ A^{\pi'}(s, a) \right]$$</paragraph><paragraph>Multiplying both sides by $-1$ gives us the performance improvement:</paragraph><paragraph>$$J(\pi') - J(\pi) = - \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}<italic>{s \sim d</italic>{\pi}, a \sim \pi} \left[ A^{\pi'}(s, a) \right]$$</paragraph><paragraph>Not the textbook PDL form, but in this mathematically equivalent form, the difference <bold>depends on</bold> the advantage under the new policy ($A^{\pi'}$) and the state distribution of the old policy ($d_{\pi}$)?</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
re Q3 about the PDL. In lecture (also answer key), it was mentioned that (B) is the only correct answer. This is definitely correct

$$J(\pi') - J(\pi) = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}{s \sim d{\pi'}, a \sim \pi'} \left[ A^{\pi}(s, a) \right]$$

However, I was wondering if we could also look at option (C) by swapping the roles of $\pi$ and $\pi'$. If we write the lemma for $J(\pi) - J(\pi')$, we get:

$$J(\pi) - J(\pi') = \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}{s \sim d{\pi}, a \sim \pi} \left[ A^{\pi'}(s, a) \right]$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ gives us the performance improvement:

$$J(\pi') - J(\pi) = - \frac{1}{1-\gamma} \mathbb{E}{s \sim d{\pi}, a \sim \pi} \left[ A^{\pi'}(s, a) \right]$$

Not the textbook PDL form, but in this mathematically equivalent form, the difference depends on the advantage under the new policy ($A^{\pi'}$) and the state distribution of the old policy ($d_{\pi}$)?

title: Research Study Using De-Identified Course Forum Data
content: <document version="2.0"><paragraph>As part of this CS234 course, you are being asked to participate in a research study for training machine learning models and developing a large language model forum chatbot that could be used to support future students. Unless you opt-out of participating in this research by using this Google form (below), your de-identified posts in our classroom forums will be used as study data. This form will go to Aishwarya Mandyam, who is not part of the course staff. She will only share the opt-out list with me after I submit grades for CS234.</paragraph><paragraph>The de-identified data will be used to train machine learning models and develop a large language model forum chatbot that could be used to support future students such as by providing some timely forum answers in future classes, particularly outside of standard Pacific time business hours, which may be of particular use to some students.</paragraph><paragraph>Your participation in this study is voluntary. Your privacy will be strictly protected: data from the forums will be de-identified and stored on Stanford encrypted and password protected storage. De-identified data will only be analyzed and used after the course is completed, grades are submitted, and will not be used to assess student or staff performance.</paragraph><paragraph>If you do not want your de-identified class forum data to be included, you may fill in this Google form</paragraph><paragraph><link href="https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSe9Sce8MSfi9KGDzJwmGWHI_4cG4bkqG_svbb49W7otTQ-IvQ/viewform">https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSe9Sce8MSfi9KGDzJwmGWHI_4cG4bkqG_svbb49W7otTQ-IvQ/viewform</link></paragraph><paragraph>If you have any questions about this study, feel free to reach out to me at ebrun@stanford.edu.</paragraph><paragraph>Independent Contact: If you are not satisfied with how this study is being conducted, or if you have any concerns, complaints, or general questions about the research or your rights as a participant, please contact the Stanford Institutional Review Board (IRB) to speak to someone independent of the research team at (650)-723-2480 or toll-free at 1-866-680-2906. You can also write to the Stanford IRB, Stanford University, 1705 El Camino Real, Palo Alto, CA 94306.</paragraph><paragraph/></document>
document:
As part of this CS234 course, you are being asked to participate in a research study for training machine learning models and developing a large language model forum chatbot that could be used to support future students. Unless you opt-out of participating in this research by using this Google form (below), your de-identified posts in our classroom forums will be used as study data. This form will go to Aishwarya Mandyam, who is not part of the course staff. She will only share the opt-out list with me after I submit grades for CS234.

The de-identified data will be used to train machine learning models and develop a large language model forum chatbot that could be used to support future students such as by providing some timely forum answers in future classes, particularly outside of standard Pacific time business hours, which may be of particular use to some students.

Your participation in this study is voluntary. Your privacy will be strictly protected: data from the forums will be de-identified and stored on Stanford encrypted and password protected storage. De-identified data will only be analyzed and used after the course is completed, grades are submitted, and will not be used to assess student or staff performance.

If you do not want your de-identified class forum data to be included, you may fill in this Google form

https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSe9Sce8MSfi9KGDzJwmGWHI_4cG4bkqG_svbb49W7otTQ-IvQ/viewform

If you have any questions about this study, feel free to reach out to me at ebrun@stanford.edu.

Independent Contact: If you are not satisfied with how this study is being conducted, or if you have any concerns, complaints, or general questions about the research or your rights as a participant, please contact the Stanford Institutional Review Board (IRB) to speak to someone independent of the research team at (650)-723-2480 or toll-free at 1-866-680-2906. You can also write to the Stanford IRB, Stanford University, 1705 El Camino Real, Palo Alto, CA 94306.
